problem: HDP topic model training is hard to parallelize at scale because standard formulations make the Gibbs updates for the global topic proportions Ψ cumbersome, limiting document-level parallelism and computational tractability.
method: Integrate out document-level topic proportions to obtain Pólya sequences with Bernoulli indicators and introduce a latent sufficient statistic l that counts draws from Ψ. Prove that Ψ is conditionally conjugate given l and derive a stick-breaking full conditional via the generalized Dirichlet, which enables a fully parallel partially collapsed Gibbs sampler where z can be sampled in parallel across documents. Make the sampler practical by a finite-dimensional truncation that projects the stick-breaking tail onto a single flag topic K*, avoiding infinite computations while preserving the correct finite-model posterior.
paper_title: Sparse Parallel Training of Hierarchical Dirichlet Process Topic Models

problem: Existing HDP samplers often fail to fully exploit both document–topic and topic–word sparsity and require per-token auxiliary variables that scale poorly, leading to high per-iteration cost and memory usage on large corpora.
method: Develop a doubly sparse parallel sampler (Algorithm 2) by approximating the Dirichlet conditional for topic–word distributions φ with a Poisson Pólya Urn, yielding sparse Φ and enabling per-token updates to z that iterate over only the nonzeros with complexity O[min(K^(m)_d(i), K^(Φ)_v(i))]. Absorb the Ψ term into an alias table to keep constant overhead in the z step. Replace per-token Bernoulli indicators with direct sampling of the sufficient statistic l using document–topic count summaries D_{k,j}, making its cost independent of the number of documents. The resulting algorithm has constant-cost non-z steps, scales efficiently, and trains on PubMed’s 768M tokens on a single multi-core machine within ~3.4 days.
paper_title: Sparse Parallel Training of Hierarchical Dirichlet Process Topic Models

problem: LLM-based logical reasoning over natural text struggles with deep, multi-hop proofs: forward-chaining requires combinatorial subset selection and has unclear halting, and models often fail to correctly handle cases where a goal is neither provable nor disprovable (UNKNOWN).
method: LAMBADA performs backward chaining from the goal and implements each reasoning step with few-shot prompted LM modules: Fact Check (prove/disprove via facts), Rule Selection (identify rules whose consequents unify with the goal), Goal Decomposition (derive sub-goals from rule antecedents), and Sign Agreement (determine PROVED vs DISPROVED based on polarity). It runs depth-first with a max-depth halting criterion and returns UNKNOWN when no applicable rules remain or depth is exhausted, and it reduces search to linear scans over rules/facts instead of combinatorial selection. This approach substantially improves label and proof accuracy on ProofWriter-PUD, PrOntoQA, and ParaRules, especially at depth-5 and on UNKNOWN cases.
paper_title: LAMBADA: Backward Chaining for Automated Reasoning in Natural Language

problem: Modular forward-chaining systems (e.g., Selection-Inference) become inefficient and error-prone as theories grow—subset selection gets harder, redundant inferences accumulate, and many LM calls are required.
method: LAMBADA designs two-stage LM submodules to simplify decisions: Fact Check splits into (i) relevant-fact selection and (ii) entailment/contradiction verification; Rule Selection splits into (i) extracting each rule’s consequent and (ii) checking applicability to the goal. It adds a simple reranker that tries shorter rules first, and employs caching plus loop avoidance to prevent redundant work. This yields an efficient depth-first search that makes far fewer LM calls (up to 11.8x fewer than SI at depth-5) and avoids redundant inferences while maintaining accuracy.
paper_title: LAMBADA: Backward Chaining for Automated Reasoning in Natural Language

problem: Controlled text perturbation for evaluation and robustness typically requires task-specific models or sparse controls that don’t specify how to realize targeted edits, making them costly and hard to generalize.
method: Tailor finetunes a pretrained seq2seq model (T5) to follow structured control codes derived from PropBank SRL, covering three control types: semantic roles, keyword content with specificity (complete/partial/sparse), and span ordering via predicate form and blank positions. Inputs use a bracketed header of controls plus a context with blanks; outputs explicitly tag generated spans, decoupling semantic intent from surface placement and enabling strict or flexible edits by masking subsets of arguments. To enforce adherence to controls, Tailor adds unlikelihood training with negative samples created by perturbing roles, lemmas, and specificities, yielding higher controllability and closeness than MLE alone.
paper_title: Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls

problem: Creating high-quality contrast sets for diverse NLP tasks is labor-intensive and can introduce artifacts; scalable, targeted, and compositional perturbations are needed to probe model behavior with fewer spurious cues.
method: Tailor introduces a library of primitive, human-readable perturbation operations that edit control codes (e.g., change tense/voice, move blanks, swap core arguments, delete/expand keywords) and can be composed for complex, context-aware rewrites. These operations generate contrast sets across BoolQ, SQuAD, UD parsing, and MATRES with high top-K validity and reduce token-level label artifacts relative to original data, while providing complementary lexical diversity to human-written contrasts. The same primitives unify varied manual strategies (e.g., entity changes, PP attachment flips) into a single controllable generation pipeline.
paper_title: Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls

problem: NLI models over-rely on lexical-overlap heuristics; prior data augmentation methods improve robustness but are template-bound and limited by specific syntactic configurations.
method: Using Tailor’s semantic-role controls, SWAP_CORE perturbations systematically invert agent/patient roles to generate challenging hypotheses for SNLI with minimal augmentation (≈2% of train). Augmenting with these perturbations improves HANS accuracy by about 5.8 points overall and 29.2 points on non-entailment without reducing in-domain SNLI accuracy, outperforming a prior syntactic augmentation baseline. This shows semantically controlled, fine-grained edits can more broadly target heuristic failures than template-based methods.
paper_title: Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls

problem: Applying English readability formulas to Bengali is faulty due to mismatched education systems and missing language resources (e.g., syllable counts, easy-word lists), leaving Bengali without a reliable document-level readability tool.
method: Construct a Bengali document-level dataset (618 documents across classes 1–12 and additional children/adult sources) and map Bangladeshi class ages (BN age) to U.S. age groups for fair, age-to-age evaluation. Build/update core resources: extend a Bengali pronunciation dictionary to cover >65K words for syllable counting and manually curate a 3,396-word ‘easy’ Bengali list for Dale–Chall. Evaluate ARI, Flesch, Flesch–Kincaid, Gunning Fog, SMOG by matching predicted U.S. ages to BN ages; find ARI performs best (correct on 8/14 documents), while syllable-dependent formulas perform poorly, indicating ARI’s relative suitability for Bengali without heavy resources.
paper_title: Simple or Complex? Learning to Predict Readability of Bengali Texts

problem: Document-level neural readability modeling is hampered by long-range dependencies and scarce labeled Bengali data; a practical, supervised predictor is needed that works at sentence level.
method: Create a large Bengali sentence-level corpus (96,335 sentences; 40,102 simple, 56,233 complex) by splitting curated documents, de-duplicating, and cleaning cross-set labels via cosine similarity filtering with fastText embeddings and manual verification. Train a BiLSTM with global average/max pooling and fuse two linguistically motivated features—Character Length (CL) and Consonant Conjunct count (CC)—computed per sentence. Conduct extensive ablations over pretrained representations (Word2Vec, GloVe, fastText, BPEmb, ULMFiT, TransformerXL, LASER, LaBSE); the best configuration (BiLSTM+pooling+CL+CC with LaBSE embeddings) achieves 86.7% accuracy/F1 on test, with CL+CC yielding consistent gains. Additionally, fastText supervised n-gram baselines reach up to 87.4% accuracy.
paper_title: Simple or Complex? Learning to Predict Readability of Bengali Texts

problem: Consonant conjuncts (ligature clusters) are strong indicators of Bengali text complexity, but prior work lacks a concrete, accurate algorithm to count them automatically.
method: Propose an efficient consonant conjunct counting algorithm that detects conjunct formations based on Bengali orthography (e.g., virama-mediated consonant clusters) and computes CC per word/sentence. Validate the method on a 341-word human-annotated corpus spanning varying difficulties, achieving perfect agreement (correct CC counts for all words). Integrate CC as a feature in the sentence-level classifier to improve readability prediction.
paper_title: Simple or Complex? Learning to Predict Readability of Bengali Texts

problem: Most Transformer analyses focus on attention weights or the multi-head attention (ATTN) alone, overlooking residual connections (RES) and layer normalization (LN), which can misestimate how much contextual information is actually mixed into token representations.
method: Extend norm-based analysis to the entire attention block (ATTN+RES+LN) by deriving an exact additive decomposition, including a 'distributive law' for LN, so the block output is written as a sum over inputs without approximation. Define a context-mixing ratio to quantify the relative strength of context mixing versus self-preservation. Applied to BERT/RoBERTa across datasets and sizes, the proposed ATTNRESLN-N method finds low mixing ratios (~16–19% on average), versus ~84–97% from attention-only and ~48–49% from residual-aware weight-based methods, showing RES and LN largely cancel ATTN mixing and the preserving path dominates (<0.5).
paper_title: Incorporating Residual and Normalization Layers into Analysis of Masked Language Models

problem: It remains unclear why the attention block suppresses the mixing effect induced by multi-head attention.
method: Analyze the ATTN module’s affine transformations by integrating per-head value and output projections into a single affine map and measuring its expansion rate via singular values; for large models this map shrinks inputs (e.g., BERT-base ≈0.88, BERT-large ≈0.80 average expansion rate < 1). Combined with a negative correlation between attention weights and the norms of transformed value vectors, the attention computation further downscales context signals. Additionally, LN’s learned scaling γ reduces the ATTN contribution relative to RES. Together these mechanisms explain the substantially reduced mixing ratio in the full block.
paper_title: Incorporating Residual and Normalization Layers into Analysis of Masked Language Models

problem: We lack a precise picture of where in depth, for which tokens, and with what linguistic biases masked LMs actually mix contextual information.
method: Using the block-level decomposition, profile mixing by layer and token type, finding higher mixing in earlier layers and notably elevated mixing for [MASK] tokens in middle/deep layers (≈19–30% vs overall ≈15–20%), consistent with infilling behavior. Further, observe a strong negative correlation between token frequency and mixing ratio (e.g., Spearman ρ as low as −0.84), indicating BERT discounts high-frequency words and emphasizes low-frequency ones, aligning with classic weighting heuristics like SIF.
paper_title: Incorporating Residual and Normalization Layers into Analysis of Masked Language Models

problem: OCR-based document understanding needs to fuse spatial layout with text in LLMs, but existing approaches either bloat input length by turning coordinates into many tokens or underuse the LLM’s autoregressive strengths, hurting efficiency and accuracy.
method: LayTextLLM introduces a Spatial Layout Projector that maps each bounding box’s [x1, y1, x2, y2] into a single learned embedding, treated as one token and interleaved with the corresponding OCR text tokens. This preserves autoregressive modeling while greatly reducing sequence length compared to coordinate-as-tokens and is even shorter than DocLLM on most datasets. The model also adds 1,000 special tokens (<B0>–<B999>) to efficiently decode coordinates. Empirically, it surpasses prior OCR-based SOTA, improving VQA by 10.7% and KIE by 15.2% over DocLLM under similar settings.
paper_title: A Bounding Box is Worth One Token - Interleaving Layout and Text in a Large Language Model for Document Understanding

problem: Standard next-token pretraining does not explicitly teach an LLM to align text content with document layout or to map between text spans and their spatial coordinates.
method: The paper designs three layout-text alignment pretraining tasks built from off-the-shelf OCR: (1) Line-level Layout Decoding asks the model to merge word-level OCR into line-level text and decode their coordinates; (2) Text-to-Layout Prediction prompts the model to predict coordinates for given line texts; and (3) Layout-to-Text Prediction requires generating text from given bounding box tokens. These complementary tasks train bidirectional grounding between text and layout and scale easily with OCR data. Ablations show these tasks especially benefit layout-aware objectives like KIE.
paper_title: A Bounding Box is Worth One Token - Interleaving Layout and Text in a Large Language Model for Document Understanding

problem: Key Information Extraction often requires not only extracting the correct value but also localizing it in the document; typical SFT lacks explicit spatial grounding supervision.
method: LayTextLLM adds a Spatially-Grounded KIE (SG-KIE) auxiliary task during SFT, prompting the model to return both the textual answer and its coordinates in specified formats for entity linking and semantic entity recognition. This supervision strengthens precise coordinate generation (supported by the special coordinate tokens) and improves KIE performance in ablations. The model further demonstrates its ability to decode coordinates accurately, achieving 77.5% IoU>0.5 on FUNSD when the textual answer is correct.
paper_title: A Bounding Box is Worth One Token - Interleaving Layout and Text in a Large Language Model for Document Understanding

problem: LLMs often face erroneous user prompts without receiving explicit error-handling instructions, yet existing evaluations focus on passive error handling and lack a comprehensive, proactive benchmark spanning detection, identification, correction, and guidance across diverse error types.
method: Introduce Mis-prompt, a proactive error-handling benchmark with four tasks (Error Detection, Identification, Correction, Guidance) and a taxonomy covering 4 primary and 14 secondary error categories. Construct a 14,969-instance dataset of erroneous Wh-questions by (i) transforming FEVEROUS, CommonsenseQA, and ROCStories, and (ii) directly generating prompts with GPT-4o, enforcing clauses with erroneous information and a false-statement+question structure. Provide ground truths for all four tasks, de-duplicate with Sentence-BERT at cosine >0.85, and perform manual review with substantial agreement (Fleiss’ κ=0.78) yielding a 93.76% acceptance rate.
paper_title: Mis-prompt: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Proactive Error Handling

problem: There is no standardized, scalable protocol to quantify LLMs’ proactive error-handling capability or to assess how training can improve it.
method: Design an automated evaluation pipeline that uses GPT-4o as a judgment model with task-specific prompts and F1 scoring, validated by human assessment. Benchmark 13 LLMs under zero/1/3-shot, CoT, and LoRA-based SFT. Results show weak zero-shot performance (avg F1≈37.53%), with detection easiest and accurate correction/guidance weakest, and reveal that supervised fine-tuning on error-handling instances yields large gains (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B 35.15→81.77, Qwen-2.5-32B 43.24→89.55). Error-category analysis further shows strengths on factual errors and weaknesses on language errors and incomplete information.
paper_title: Mis-prompt: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Proactive Error Handling

problem: Most AI-generated text (AIGT) detectors operate at the document level and fail when documents contain a mix of human-authored and AI-edited sentences. There is no suitable dataset to study fine-grained, sentence-level AIGT detection in such mixed-provenance texts.
method: Construct SeqXGPT-Bench, a sentence-level benchmark where human documents from diverse sources are partially continued/edited by multiple LLMs. Using the first 1–3 human sentences as prompts, continuations are generated by GPT-2, GPT-Neo, GPT-J, LLaMA, and GPT-3.5-turbo; sentences are labeled by origin. The benchmark supports particular-model binary, mixed-model binary, and mixed-model multiclass setups with a 90/10 split, and includes a document-level dataset and an out-of-distribution sentence-level test set built from TriviaQA.
paper_title: SeqXGPT: Sentence-Level AI-Generated Text Detection

problem: Sentence-level AIGT detection is challenging for perplexity-based and perturbation-based zero-shot methods (e.g., DetectGPT) due to short-text sensitivity and ineffective perturbations, and for semantic fine-tuning baselines due to overfitting. A robust, fine-grained detector is needed.
method: SeqXGPT uses white-box features by extracting token-wise log probabilities from multiple LLMs and aligning them to words to form per-word feature vectors across models. Treating these vectors as temporal sequences, a 1D CNN stack first captures local patterns, followed by a Transformer-based context network to model long-range dependencies. The task is formulated as word-level sequence labeling with BIO-style labels, and sentence labels are decided by majority vote over word labels. This design outperforms DetectGPT, Sniffer, and RoBERTa baselines on sentence-, multiclass-, and document-level detection and shows strong OOD generalization; ablations verify that both CNN and Transformer components are necessary.
paper_title: SeqXGPT: Sentence-Level AI-Generated Text Detection

problem: Classifying each sentence independently ignores cross-sentence context within a document, which can degrade accuracy on short sentences and in mixed-provenance texts.
method: Reformulate sentence-level AIGT detection as sequence labeling over the entire document: assign BIO-style word labels and derive each sentence’s label via majority over its words. This formulation leverages document context and is empirically validated by RoBERTa baselines, where the sequence-labeling variant (Seq-RoBERTa) outperforms sentence classification (Sent-RoBERTa) in multiclass and OOD settings.
paper_title: SeqXGPT: Sentence-Level AI-Generated Text Detection

problem: Chart understanding models are often task-specific, rely on limited chart sources, and lack instruction-following generality, hindering broad real-world chart comprehension and reasoning.
method: Create ChartInstruct, a large chart-specific instruction-following dataset (~191K instructions over ~71K charts) spanning diverse real-world charts collected from 157 web domains and prior corpora. Use Gemini Pro Vision to extract underlying data tables/titles for web-crawled charts, then generate multi-task instructions with GPT-3.5/GPT-4 (covering summarization, open-ended QA, fact checking, chain-of-thought reasoning with tool use, code generation, and novel GPT-4–distilled tasks). The dataset is shown to be diverse and of generally good quality, and is used to instruction-tune chart models.
paper_title: ChartInstruct: Instruction Tuning for Chart Comprehension and Reasoning

problem: General VLMs (e.g., LLaVA with CLIP) are suboptimal for charts and struggle to align chart visual features with LLMs for instruction following.
method: Design an end-to-end chart VLM by replacing LLaVA’s CLIP with the chart-pretrained UniChart vision encoder and adding an adapter. Train in two stages: (i) an alignment phase that freezes the vision encoder and LLM and fine-tunes only the adapter on chart-to-table and chart summarization to align visual features; (ii) instruction tuning on ChartInstruct with the vision encoder frozen while updating the adapter and LLM (tested with Llama2-7B and Flan-T5-XL-3B). This yields state-of-the-art results on multiple chart benchmarks (e.g., improved OpenCQA and Chart-to-Text) and better adherence to instructions in human evaluation.
paper_title: ChartInstruct: Instruction Tuning for Chart Comprehension and Reasoning

problem: For reasoning-intensive chart tasks, directly consuming image features may miss precise data patterns; LLMs benefit from structured, faithful chart data as input.
method: Introduce a two-step pipeline: first derender the chart image to a textual data table using UniChart, then concatenate the table with the instruction and feed it to an LLM (Llama2 or Flan-T5), skipping any visual alignment. Instruction-tune the LLM on ChartInstruct. This pipeline excels on reasoning-heavy tasks, achieving new state-of-the-art on ChartQA and chart fact-checking; ablations show reasoning-oriented instruction tasks (CoT and coding) are critical to these gains.
paper_title: ChartInstruct: Instruction Tuning for Chart Comprehension and Reasoning

problem: State-of-the-art toxicity classifiers perform well on existing benchmarks but may fail to detect implicit toxicity. It is unclear whether LLMs can readily generate such undetectable implicit toxic language.
method: Use zero-shot prompting of GPT-3.5-turbo with an instruction to produce implicit toxic responses via figurative devices (euphemism, circumlocution, sarcasm, metaphor, etc.). Evaluate attack success against five toxicity classifiers (Perspective-API, OpenAI Moderation, RoBERTa-based TOXIGEN and BAD, and Davinci003). LLM-generated implicit toxicity yields much higher attack success rates than prior datasets, e.g., 96.69% (Perspective-API) and 58.47% (Davinci003).
paper_title: Unveiling the Implicit Toxicity in Large Language Models

problem: How to systematically induce LLMs to generate implicit—but hard-to-detect—toxicity; naive rewards from existing classifiers tend to push models to non-toxic rather than implicitly toxic outputs.
method: Propose a three-stage RL-based attack for a local LLM (LLaMA-13B): (1) supervised warm-start on implicit toxic data automatically generated via GPT-3.5-turbo prompting; (2) train a reward model using comparison data built from a three-class labeling scheme (implicit toxic > explicit toxic > non-toxic) annotated by GPT-3.5-turbo; (3) optimize with PPO using an ensemble reward that combines the learned preference model with a penalty from an external toxicity classifier (BAD) and KL regularization to the supervised policy. This boosts attack success markedly, e.g., to 99.55% (Perspective-API), 97.81% (Moderation), 82.51% (TOXIGEN), 90.16% (BAD), and 62.85% (Davinci003). Ablations show removing the learned reward drives the model toward non-toxic outputs (annotated toxic probability drops to 20.90%).
paper_title: Unveiling the Implicit Toxicity in Large Language Models

problem: High-quality, task-specific NLP training data is often scarce. Purely LLM-generated synthetic datasets tend to lack diversity and difficulty, while directly using existing datasets can be misaligned with the target task format and objectives.
method: DataTune automatically repurposes public datasets for a new task via two stages: (1) dual-stage dataset retrieval from HuggingFace—first using a bi-encoder retriever (DataFinder) to propose candidates, then an LLM-based reranker that considers dataset descriptions, schema, and sample rows, with self-consistency to select robust datasets; and (2) an LLM-driven transformation pipeline with Task Expansion (enrich task spec), Schema Selection (identify relevant columns), a Planning Module (produce a step-by-step transformation plan), and an Execution Module (apply the plan per row) to generate task-aligned input/output examples. Transforming multiple top-ranked datasets preserves source diversity and yields harder, less-duplicated examples while maintaining label accuracy, leading to consistent fine-tuning gains over few-shot prompting and over using synthetic or retrieved data alone.
paper_title: Better Synthetic Data by Retrieving and Transforming Existing Datasets

problem: Single-source data generation (only transformed or only synthetic) may cover limited regions of the task distribution, capping fine-tuning benefits.
method: Combine DataTune-transformed data with purely synthetic LLM-generated data for training. The two sources are complementary—t-SNE of MiniLM embeddings shows largely disjoint clusters—and jointly training delivers additive improvements, outperforming strong baselines such as Prompt2Model and achieving higher average scores on BIG-Bench tasks than either source alone.
paper_title: Better Synthetic Data by Retrieving and Transforming Existing Datasets

problem: There is no benchmark that evaluates actual (graded, context-dependent) causality between real-world events across multiple documents, making it hard to assess whether LLMs truly understand causal relationships in narratives.
method: Introduce CRAB, a benchmark with fine-grained causal judgments (0–100) on ~2.7K event pairs drawn from 20 news stories and 173 documents. The pipeline selects top articles per story, extracts main events with GPT-3 (text-davinci-003), filters and expert-verifies events, builds timelines with cross-document event disambiguation, and crowdsources 7 annotators per pair with expert adjudication for high-variance cases (26.7%). CRAB organizes data into causal frames and chains (e.g., mediation, confounding, collider) enabling stratified evaluation of causal reasoning.
paper_title: CRAB: Assessing the Strength of Causal Relationships Between Real-World Events

problem: Existing evaluations do not systematically test LLMs across multiple granularities and structures of causal reasoning, limiting insight into their abilities and failure modes.
method: Define three evaluation settings over CRAB: (i) pairwise causality inference (binary, 4-class, and scalar-to-class), (ii) graded responsibility via MCQ over causal frames, and (iii) causal chain inference over structured patterns (mediation, confounding, collider). Provide multi-document contexts in prompts and assess with Macro-F1 and Exact Match, further stratifying by in-document vs cross-document and by causal frame/chain types. Evaluate GPT-3, GPT-4, and Flan-Alpaca, finding generally poor performance—especially on complex structures (e.g., collider, mixed frames) and cross-document pairs—and a notable drop on post-Jan 2022 events, suggesting limits to generalization beyond pretraining knowledge.
paper_title: CRAB: Assessing the Strength of Causal Relationships Between Real-World Events

problem: Extracting semantically salient real-world events from news text at the right granularity is challenging; traditional extractive methods often miss or mis-granularize key events.
method: Use a generative event-extraction approach with GPT-3 prompted to list main events per article, followed by automatic filtering (e.g., minimal length, quote-speech removal) and expert validation. Empirically, this approach yields higher precision/granularity than extractive baselines (e.g., achieving F1 ≈ 69.8% on a manually constructed 88-article set), and supports timeline construction and cross-document event merging for the final 384 unique events used in CRAB.
paper_title: CRAB: Assessing the Strength of Causal Relationships Between Real-World Events

problem: RLHF-aligned large language models are often overconfident: their expressed probabilities do not match actual correctness, and many black-box LLMs do not expose per-token logits, making standard post-hoc logit calibration difficult. We need a plug-and-play way to produce well-calibrated per-answer confidences for multiple-choice QA without relying on logits.
method: UF Calibration decomposes confidence into two factors: (1) Uncertainty about the question, estimated via the normalized entropy of the sampled answer distribution obtained from K samples; and (2) Fidelity to each candidate answer, elicited by substituting that option’s content with 'All other options are wrong.' and using greedy decoding to build a hierarchical fidelity chain that reveals how strongly the model prefers the original choice. Elements in the chain are assigned position-based weights (τ^i), and fidelities are aggregated across chains weighted by their sampled frequencies. The final confidence for answer a_i is Conf(Q, a_i) = (1 − Uncertainty(Q)) · F(a_i). This method requires no access to logits, is robust across temperatures, and achieves improved calibration (lower ECE, lower IPR, higher CE) across four MCQA benchmarks and multiple RLHF LMs.
paper_title: Calibrating the Confidence of Large Language Models by Eliciting Fidelity

problem: Existing calibration metrics like ECE alone can miss critical aspects of LLM confidence behavior, such as non-monotonic reliability (low-confidence answers being more accurate than higher-confidence ones) and over-concentration of reported confidences in narrow ranges.
method: Introduce two complementary metrics: Inverse Pair Ratio (IPR), which measures monotonicity violations in the reliability diagram by quantifying the proportion of inverse bin pairs, and Confidence Evenness (CE), which evaluates how uniformly confidence mass is distributed across bins to detect over-concentration (e.g., clustering at 0.8–0.9). Used alongside ECE, these metrics provide a more comprehensive view of 'truly well-calibrated' confidence and reveal that UF Calibration yields more monotonic and evenly distributed confidence than verbalization- and logit-based baselines.
paper_title: Calibrating the Confidence of Large Language Models by Eliciting Fidelity

problem: LLMs often hallucinate factual answers and tend to answer even when they lack sufficient knowledge, making QA systems unreliable and hard to control or trace.
method: L2R enforces Knowledge Scope Limitation by decoupling knowledge from the LLM into an independent, structured knowledge base of atomic facts that is initially empty and curated over time. For each question, it retrieves top-k facts and the LLM is explicitly instructed to rely only on this retrieved evidence, not its internal knowledge. A dual refusal mechanism gates answering: a soft refusal where the LLM judges answerability from the retrieved information, and a hard refusal that computes a score from the retrieved items’ confidence and semantic similarity (L2 distance) and compares it to a human-set threshold α. The system answers only if both checks pass, and when answering it returns the supporting evidence, step-by-step reasoning, and the final answer; otherwise it returns REFUSAL.
paper_title: Learn to Refuse: Making Large Language Models More Controllable and Reliable through Knowledge Scope Limitation and Refusal Mechanism

problem: Populating a high-quality knowledge base from scratch is labor-intensive; starting with an empty KB limits coverage and utility.
method: Automatic Knowledge Enrichment (AKE) bootstraps the KB using three LLM agents: a Question Generation Agent to create candidate questions from seeds, an Answer Generation Agent to produce answers with a confidence score C∈[0,1], and a QA-to-Knowledge Agent that converts QA pairs into concise, single-fact declarative statements while preserving C. The resulting pseudo-knowledge entries, each with an associated confidence, are added to the structured KB. These confidences are subsequently used in retrieval fusion and in the hard refusal scoring, enabling rapid KB growth with quality moderated by self-rated confidence and without human effort.
paper_title: Learn to Refuse: Making Large Language Models More Controllable and Reliable through Knowledge Scope Limitation and Refusal Mechanism

problem: Existing hate speech detection models struggle to capture contextual nuance, and there is no systematic study on how to prompt LLMs to leverage their knowledge for context-aware hate speech detection.
method: Design and systematically evaluate four prompting strategies for GPT-3.5—(1) a general prompt, (2) a general prompt augmented with a formal hate speech definition, (3) few-shot prompting with 10 balanced exemplars from HateXplain, and (4) a tailored chain-of-thought (CoT) prompt that operationalizes the formal definition via step-by-step reasoning (e.g., identifying targets and disparaging words). On HateXplain, the CoT prompt achieves the best results (Accuracy 0.85, Precision 0.80, Recall 0.95, F1 0.87), outperforming the other prompts; the general+definition variant offers minimal gains over the general prompt, and few-shot yields very high recall (0.97) but lower precision (0.67).
paper_title: An Investigation of Large Language Models for Real-World Hate Speech Detection

problem: It is unclear whether LLMs prompted out-of-the-box can match or surpass fine-tuned transformer baselines for real-world hate speech detection across diverse datasets.
method: Benchmark GPT-3.5 with a simple general prompt against fine-tuned BERT and RoBERTa on four English datasets (HateXplain, COVID-HATE, CallMeSexist, USElectionHate). GPT-3.5 matches or exceeds baselines on multiple datasets—for example, on HateXplain it attains F1 0.82 vs. RoBERTa 0.76 (+7.9%) and BERT 0.66 (+24.2%), and its overall totals are competitive with RoBERTa and superior to BERT—demonstrating that LLMs with lightweight prompting can be viable detectors, though recall lags on some datasets (e.g., CallMeSexist, USElectionHate) under a general prompt.
paper_title: An Investigation of Large Language Models for Real-World Hate Speech Detection

problem: The effectiveness of LLM prompting strategies for hate speech detection in non-English languages is not well understood.
method: Evaluate the same four prompting strategies on the Chinese sexism dataset SWSR. All prompts show modest accuracy (0.61–0.68) and low F1; notably, CoT attains high precision (0.86) but very low recall (0.21), yielding F1 0.34. This reveals limitations of current prompting—especially English CoT reasoning steps—when applied to non-English hate speech detection.
paper_title: An Investigation of Large Language Models for Real-World Hate Speech Detection

problem: It is unclear which patterns in natural pre-training text give rise to language models’ in-context learning (ICL) despite the distribution shift between natural text and in-context prompts.
method: Introduce and formalize parallel structures (PS): pairs of phrases within the same context window that follow the same underlying distribution. Propose a gradient-based PS-strength detector: take a pretrained LM, perform one gradient step on a former phrase (context, token), and measure log-likelihood improvement on a latter phrase; larger improvement implies stronger PS (Sec. 3.2). Make the detector scalable by (i) restricting latter phrases to short, overlapping segments and (ii) minibatching former phrases, achieving strong correlation with the exact per-phrase update (+0.71 Pearson; m=12, l=128, η=1e-4).
paper_title: Parallel Structures in Pre-training Data Yield In-Context Learning

problem: Whether parallel structures causally drive ICL remains unverified on real pre-training data.
method: Causally test PS by ablating them in pre-training data: replace the last token of each detected latter phrase with a random token, then continue pre-train GPT-2 Small–XL on OpenWebText and evaluate ICL on 9 tasks with 64–128 shots. Removing PS causes a large average ICL drop (51.1% relative) versus random ablation (1.5%), consistent across model sizes, while fine-tuning accuracy remains comparable to random ablation, indicating the loss is specifically in ICL ability (Tables 2–3, Sec. 6.1).
paper_title: Parallel Structures in Pre-training Data Yield In-Context Learning

problem: It is unknown if PS effects are merely due to n-gram repetitions or generic long-range dependencies.
method: Disentangle via controlled ablations: (a) Exclude n-gram repetitions when scoring PS (-PS+Rp); ablating these non-repetition PS still yields a 37.9% average ICL drop and notably hurts copy (81.8% relative drop), showing PS beyond copying are critical (Sec. 6.2). (b) Ablate long-range dependencies not explained by PS (-Dp+PS); this yields a smaller 17.5% average drop versus 51.1% for PS, demonstrating PS have a larger causal effect on ICL than generic long-range dependency (Sec. 6.2).
paper_title: Parallel Structures in Pre-training Data Yield In-Context Learning

problem: Existing negation benchmarks rarely isolate whether and how negative morphemes take semantic scope, making it hard to tell if models truly learn scoped negation or rely on shallow heuristics.
method: Construct ScoNe-NLI by extending the negated portion of MoNLI into six-example contrast sets per base item that control the presence of zero, one, or two negations and whether each negation scopes, yielding 1,202 examples per split. Using the MoNLI lexical split (disjoint substituted items across train/test), evaluate NLI models: DeBERTa and RoBERTa fine-tuned only on MNLI/ANLI/FEVER or other negation-focused sets underperform on ScoNe-NLI, whereas further fine-tuning on MoNLI + ScoNe-NLI attains near-perfect performance across all six conditions (e.g., DeBERTa 100% on all splits), showing models can learn scoped negation and generalize to new lexical items.
paper_title: ScoNe: Benchmarking Negation Reasoning in Language Models With Fine-Tuning and In-Context Learning

problem: It is unclear whether large language models can learn scoped negation via in-context learning on NLI-style prompts, and whether prompt design remedies common negation failures.
method: Evaluate InstructGPT (text-davinci-002/003) on ScoNe-NLI with six prompt formats (Structured, Brown et al., Conditional Q, Conditional Truth, Hypothesis Q, Reasoning) in zero- and few-shot settings at temperature 0.0, mapping 'yes' to entailment. The best setup (few-shot Reasoning with davinci-003) reaches 82% overall but shows systematic errors: high accuracy where negation can effectively be ignored (No Negation 99%, One Not Scoped 97%, Two Not Scoped 98%, Two Scoped 89) and low where exactly one negation scopes (One Scoped 69%, One Scoped + One Not Scoped ≈43–48%), mirroring an Ignore-Negation baseline. This indicates LLMs struggle to learn negation scope in-context for NLI-formatted tasks.
paper_title: ScoNe: Benchmarking Negation Reasoning in Language Models With Fine-Tuning and In-Context Learning

problem: The observed failures on NLI-style prompts may stem from a mismatch with LLM pretraining rather than an inability to reason about negation scope.
method: Introduce ScoNe-NLG, a sentence-completion dataset of 74 contrastive triplets embedding negation reasoning in short narratives (no negation, scoped negation, non-scoping negation). Evaluate InstructGPT (davinci-003) in zero- and few-shot without prompt adaptation beyond demonstrations; human annotators judge coherence with high agreement (Fleiss κ 0.84/0.91). Results show strong performance (92% zero-shot, 95% few-shot) across conditions, without the ScoNe-NLI failure modes, suggesting LLMs can reason about scoped negation when examples align with their generative pretraining regime.
paper_title: ScoNe: Benchmarking Negation Reasoning in Language Models With Fine-Tuning and In-Context Learning

problem: Existing CV and NLP pretraining fail to jointly model fine-grained interactions between image regions and long natural-language sequences, limiting performance on cross-modal tasks like retrieval and visual commonsense reasoning.
method: Unicoder-VL is a single-stream multi-layer Transformer (initialized from BERT) that ingests wordpieces together with region-level visual features and 5-D box geometry from a frozen Faster R-CNN (top-100 RoIs), projecting visual and location features into a shared space with layer normalization. It is cross-modally pre-trained on 3.8M image–caption pairs with three tasks: (i) Masked Language Modeling masking 15% of words and predicting them conditioned on all image regions; (ii) Masked Object Classification masking 15% of regions (90% replaced with zero vectors) and predicting detector-derived object labels from cross-modal context; (iii) Visual–Linguistic Matching using the [CLS] representation to classify matched vs. mismatched pairs with both negative-image and negative-caption sampling. This yields context-aware, aligned token and region embeddings that fuse vision and language early.
paper_title: Unicoder-VL: A Universal Encoder for Vision and Language by Cross-modal Pre-training

problem: Turning a pre-trained cross-modal encoder into a strong image–text retrieval model and assessing generalization without task-specific supervision.
method: For retrieval, Unicoder-VL scores image–text pairs via a learned similarity s(w,v) from the [CLS]-based cross-modal representation and is fine-tuned with a hardest-negative triplet loss in both directions (image-to-text, text-to-image), sampling three negatives per positive. This delivers new state-of-the-art results on MSCOCO and Flickr30k (e.g., Flickr30k R@1: 86.2% sentence, 71.5% image), substantially outperforming prior methods. For zero-shot retrieval, it directly reuses the pre-trained VLM classifier as the matching score without fine-tuning, achieving competitive R@K (e.g., MSCOCO 1K R@1: 54.4% sentence, 43.4% image; Flickr30k R@1: 64.3% sentence, 48.4% image), indicating the learned alignment generalizes beyond the pretraining data.
paper_title: Unicoder-VL: A Universal Encoder for Vision and Language by Cross-modal Pre-training

problem: Image-text matching suffers because image representations from regional detectors or CNNs miss global semantic relationships among objects and stuff, limiting alignment to rich sentence-level concepts.
method: Start from bottom-up region features (Faster R-CNN) and construct a fully connected relationship graph over regions where edge weights are learned pairwise affinities in an embedding space. Perform reasoning with residual Graph Convolutional Networks on this graph (using the learned affinity matrix) to propagate context and produce relationship-enhanced region features, without requiring external relationship annotations.
paper_title: Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching

problem: For matching a whole sentence to an image, one needs a holistic image embedding that captures key objects and high-level semantics while filtering redundant regional information.
method: Apply global semantic reasoning by feeding the relationship-enhanced region features sequentially into a GRU with update and reset gates. The memory cell incrementally accumulates discriminative content and the final hidden state becomes the image representation. Regions are ordered (e.g., by detector confidence) to encourage effective accumulation; this gated memory over globally contextualized regions yields stronger whole-image embeddings for retrieval.
paper_title: Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching

problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
method: Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.
paper_title: Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching

problem: Learn robust joint representations from temporal multimodal sequences (e.g., audio–video, video–sensor) that capture both cross-modal relationships and temporal dependencies.
method: Introduce CorrRNN: an unsupervised RNN encoder–decoder with a multimodal GRU that maintains a fused state h_t alongside modality-specific states h^1_t and h^2_t. A correlation module maximizes the empirical correlation between modality-specific projections within each mini-batch at every time step, scaled in the objective by a λ term. Training jointly optimizes this correlation term together with reconstruction losses, yielding a temporal joint space that encodes cross-modal structure.
paper_title: Deep Multimodal Representation Learning from Temporal Data

problem: During temporal fusion, different modalities can be intermittently noisy or unreliable; a model should dynamically downweight corrupted inputs and emphasize coherent signals over time.
method: Add a Dynamic Weighting (soft attention) module that scores each modality’s current input against the previous fused state using bilinear functions (with learned matrices A_1, A_2), then normalizes the scores via Laplace smoothing to produce modality weights. These weights modulate the multimodal GRU updates, allowing the encoder to emphasize the more coherent modality at each time step and improving robustness when one modality degrades.
paper_title: Deep Multimodal Representation Learning from Temporal Data

problem: RL-based seekers for goal-oriented vision-and-language tasks typically rely on a single policy, limiting strategic diversity and failing to capture the multimodal nature of effective query generation. This makes exploration inefficient and prevents the agent from reasoning over uncertainty in its own knowledge.
method: Maintain a Bayesian distribution over the seeker’s policy parameters rather than a single policy, sampling multiple query hypotheses per state. Formulate the posterior over policies as π(θ|C+,I) ∝ exp(J(θ)/α) π0(θ), where α controls exploration, and optimize the expected return with KL regularization to the prior. Approximate this posterior with Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) over a set of particles, using policy gradients ∇θJ(θ) to drive particles toward high-reward regions while a kernel repulsion term preserves diversity across modes. This yields diverse RNN-based query generators (particles) that better cover multiple strategies and reduce collapse to a single mode.
paper_title: Gold Seeker: Information Gain from Policy Distributions for Goal-oriented Vision-and-Langauge Reasoning

problem: Standard seekers do not know which information is most valuable to acquire next and suffer from sparse extrinsic rewards, leading to inefficient question/query generation.
method: Introduce a RESPONDER that models the environment by predicting a distribution over answers p(a|s,q;ω) and scoring them via a utility u(·) to compute a query gain Gω(s,q). At each step, sample multiple queries from the policy particles and select the one maximizing the (upper-bounded) information gain estimated by the RESPONDER, with occasional ε-greedy exploration. Incorporate this gain as an intrinsic reward (scaled by η) into the RL objective so updates reflect both anticipated and realized information value. This reward shaping guides the seeker to ask questions that most reduce internal uncertainty and improves sample efficiency.
paper_title: Gold Seeker: Information Gain from Policy Distributions for Goal-oriented Vision-and-Langauge Reasoning

problem: Standard reconstruction objectives may not sufficiently enforce sharing across modalities or support missing/noisy modalities; need objectives that encourage both within- and cross-modal information retention.
method: Equip the decoder with a multiaspect reconstruction objective: (i) fused-reconstruction from the joint representation to both modalities, (ii) self-reconstruction within each modality, and (iii) cross-reconstruction across modalities, with a β hyperparameter to balance modality scales. Combined with the correlation maximization in the encoder, this objective yields richer joint and enhanced unimodal features, boosting cross-modality and shared-representation learning performance.
paper_title: Deep Multimodal Representation Learning from Temporal Data

problem: Vision-and-language navigation in unseen environments requires both long-horizon planning over many possible locations and fine-grained grounding of objects/scenes described in instructions. Prior methods either limit the agent to local actions (hindering global planning) or use coarse map node features with recurrent memory (hindering long-range reasoning and fine-grained grounding).
method: DUET builds a topological map online with visited and navigable nodes, updating node visuals by aggregating partial observations across steps. It runs two cross-modal transformers: a coarse-scale, map-level encoder over all nodes and the instruction, and a fine-scale, local encoder over the current panorama’s image and object features with the instruction. A dynamic fusion converts local action scores into the global action space using a backtrack score from neighboring visited nodes, then fuses coarse and fine predictions via a learned gate to produce a single global action over all mapped nodes (including stop). The selected global target is executed via shortest-path planning on the current map, enabling efficient exploration and backtracking while preserving fine-grained grounding.
paper_title: Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Standard self-attention over node features neglects the environment’s topology, making it harder to reason about spatial layout and proximity relationships that are critical for navigation planning on a map.
method: The coarse-scale encoder introduces graph-aware self-attention (GASA) that augments attention scores with pairwise graph distances derived from the evolving topological map, biasing attention toward topologically relevant nodes. Node embeddings also incorporate egocentric location encodings (orientation and distance to the current node) and navigation-step encodings (last visit time), plus a connected 'stop' node. This graph-aware cross-modal transformer jointly encodes nodes and text, improving layout-aware reasoning and leading to better path efficiency as validated by ablations.
paper_title: Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
method: DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.
paper_title: Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Graph data augmentation for semi-supervised node classification is challenging: naive/random edge edits can degrade performance, and we need a label-free way to denoise observed graphs by promoting intra-class connectivity and suppressing inter-class edges.
method: GAUG-M uses a neural edge predictor (Graph Auto-Encoder, GAE) to estimate edge probabilities over all node pairs, then deterministically modifies the graph by adding the top i|E| non-edges and removing the bottom j|E| existing edges by predicted probability. The resulting modified graph G_m replaces the original graph for both training and inference, effectively denoising structure by approximating class-homophily. Experiments show learned additions/removals increase intra-class edges and improve node classification vs. random edits.
paper_title: Data Augmentation for Graph Neural Networks

problem: In dynamic or latency-constrained settings, the graph cannot be modified at inference time, so we need augmentation that improves generalization while training on plausible structural variants but testing on the original graph.
method: GAUG-O builds an end-to-end, stochastic augmentation pipeline: a GAE predicts edge probabilities M, which are interpolated with the original adjacency A via a mixing coefficient α to form P; per iteration, a sparse variant adjacency A' is sampled using a relaxed Bernoulli (Gumbel-Softmax) with a straight-through estimator, exposing the node classifier to new graph variants each step. Training optimizes a joint loss combining node-classification (L_nc) and edge-prediction reconstruction (β·L_ep) to prevent drift from A. At inference, the GNN operates on the original graph, yielding improved performance via learned, differentiable graph augmentation.
paper_title: Data Augmentation for Graph Neural Networks

problem: Most image captioning systems rely on pre-trained object detectors (e.g., Faster R-CNN) to extract region features, creating a two-stage pipeline that is computationally heavy and not end-to-end; LSTM decoders further limit training efficiency and expressiveness.
method: PureT is a fully Transformer-based, one-stage captioner trained end-to-end. It uses Swin Transformer to extract grid-level visual features and computes a global image feature via mean pooling. A refining encoder alternates windowed and shifted-window self-attention (W-MSA/SW-MSA) to capture intra-image relations; the global feature is injected as an extra token into keys/values within each window and is also refined by querying all grid tokens, enabling mutual guidance between local and global context. A standard Transformer decoder then generates captions from the refined grid and global features, yielding state-of-the-art results on MSCOCO.
paper_title: End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning

problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
method: PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.
paper_title: End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning

problem: Vision-language models lack a large-scale, controlled benchmark to test systematic compositional generalization—i.e., whether they can recombine seen atoms (objects, attributes, relations) into novel compounds and handle unseen atoms.
method: CREPE introduces a systematicity evaluation built from Visual Genome that partitions data into Seen Compounds, Unseen Compounds, and Unseen Atoms splits per model’s pretraining set (CC-12M, YFCC-15M, LAION-400M) by parsing training captions into scene graphs to mark seen atoms/compounds. It pairs each image-text with hard negatives: HN-ATOM (single-atom foils) and HN-COMP (compound foils), generated using WordNet antonyms/grand-hypernyms and filtered with BERT to remain plausible while incorrect. Models are evaluated zero-shot via image↔text retrieval with Recall@K, revealing consistent performance drops when compounds are unseen (e.g., up to 12.5% R@1 drop on LAION-trained models).
paper_title: CREPE: Can Vision-Language Foundation Models Reason Compositionally?

problem: The best choice of visual backbone for Transformer decoders in captioning—and how backbone–decoder combinations interact—has been unclear (region vs. grid features; CNN vs. ViT-family).
method: The paper systematically compares backbones (Faster R-CNN with ResNet/ResNeXt region features, ViT grid features, and Swin Transformer grid features) across multiple decoders (M2 Transformer, X-Transformer, standard Transformer, and PureT). Results show Swin Transformer grid features deliver the largest, most consistent gains—especially with decoders using standard MSA—while CNN-based region features pair better with LSTM or Transformer variants using non-standard attention. These findings guide backbone selection and motivate PureT’s Swin-based, standard-MSA design.
paper_title: End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning

problem: There is no scalable way to assess the productivity of vision-language models—their ability to comprehend increasingly complex captions and resist failure modes like bag-of-words behavior and mishandled negation.
method: CREPE builds a productivity evaluation by generating subgraphs of varying complexity (n=4–12) via random walks on Visual Genome scene graphs, then producing captions with templates (n=4) or GPT-3 (n≥5) with filtering for atom coverage. It adds three hard negative types: HN-ATOM (atomic foils), HN-SWAP (permuting atoms to test binding, filtered with RoBERTa entailment), and HN-NEG (negations at caption or atom level). Zero-shot retrieval across complexities shows performance decays with complexity and nears random on swap negatives, across diverse architectures (e.g., CLIP, CyCLIP, ALBEF, FLAVA).
paper_title: CREPE: Can Vision-Language Foundation Models Reason Compositionally?

problem: Existing image-text retrieval benchmarks rarely provide controlled, validated hard negatives that isolate specific compositional failure modes, confounding conclusions about models’ compositionality.
method: CREPE provides a reusable hard-negative generation pipeline: for systematicity, HN-ATOM and HN-COMP (two compound foils each with an atomic foil) built using WordNet antonyms/grand-hypernyms and BERT-based plausibility filtering; for productivity, HN-SWAP with RoBERTa entailment checks and HN-NEG with graph-consistency checks to avoid accidental truths. Human verification confirms high quality (e.g., ~92% for HN-ATOM) while documenting noise for HN-SWAP/NEG, enabling attribution of retrieval errors to precise compositional phenomena.
paper_title: CREPE: Can Vision-Language Foundation Models Reason Compositionally?

problem: Embodied agents following natural-language instructions in household environments must execute long, compositional tasks that require recalling distant past observations and actions. Recurrent policies often struggle to capture such long-term dependencies, leading to failures on long-horizon vision-and-language navigation (VLN) tasks like ALFRED.
method: Introduce Episodic Transformer (E.T.), a multimodal transformer policy with full-episode observability. A transformer-based language encoder processes the instruction; a visual encoder (ResNet-50 backbone with projection) embeds each observed frame; and a learned action embedding encodes prior actions. These sequences are concatenated and fed to a multimodal transformer with causal attention to prevent access to future timesteps, using positional/temporal encodings. The model predicts actions for all timesteps in a single forward pass with cross-entropy over the full sequence. Ablations show that access to full visual history is critical (16+ past frames approach full-episode results), while more than one past action does not help; compared to an LSTM baseline, E.T. improves task success (e.g., 33.8% vs 23.2% on seen; 3.2% vs 2.4% on unseen).
paper_title: Episodic Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Natural human instructions exhibit high linguistic variability, making it hard for agents to learn robust instruction grounding and generalize to unseen environments from limited annotations.
method: Use synthetic instructions as an intermediate task specification derived from PDDL subgoal actions (e.g., Goto Bed, Put Apple Table), decoupling language variability from visual grounding. (1) Pretrain a seq2seq translator from human to synthetic instructions and transfer the translator’s encoder weights to initialize the agent’s language encoder, outperforming no pretraining and generic BERT pretraining (e.g., 37.6/3.8 vs 33.8/3.2 seen/unseen). (2) Jointly train with additional synthetic demonstrations (≈45K) annotated by subgoal actions, sharing encoders but using separate token tables for human/synthetic text, which yields larger gains than alternative synthetic sources (expert frames or speaker-generated text) and substantially boosts success rates on human-instruction evaluation (e.g., to 38.5/5.4; combining pretraining + joint training reaches 46.6/7.3 on val and 38.42/8.57 on test seen/unseen).
paper_title: Episodic Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: In remote embodied visual grounding (REVERIE), an agent must follow high-level instructions to both navigate to the correct location (where to stop) and localize the target object (what to attend to). Existing approaches struggle to infer scene-level intent from sparse language and to robustly ground objects across multi-view panoramas.
method: Pretrain two complementary cross-modal grounding tasks with ViLBERT to inject where/what priors before learning a navigation policy. (1) Scene Grounding: formulate viewpoint–instruction alignment as a 5-way multiple-choice task using 36-view panoramas with ResNet features; compute alignment scores from ViLBERT’s CLS/IMG tokens and optimize a cross-entropy loss to identify the target viewpoint (e.g., 85.67%/66.43% accuracy on Val Seen/Unseen). (2) Object Grounding: a two-stage scheme—first fine-tune on single-image grounding, then further fine-tune on viewpoint-level grounding (aggregating all boxes across views) to capture cross-image object relations; this ViLPointer significantly outperforms prior pointers (e.g., MattNet) at the ground-truth viewpoint (73.26% vs 68.45 on Val Seen; 67.45% vs 56.63 on Val Unseen).
paper_title: Scene-Intuitive Agent for Remote Embodied Visual Grounding

problem: Generating reliable navigation actions under high-level instructions requires integrating scene-level and object-level evidence over time, but standard recurrent policies have limited long-horizon memory and weak guidance from underspecified language.
method: Use a memory-augmented attentive action decoder that treats the pretrained grounding models as encoders and fuses them with an addressable scene memory. The decoder: (a) conditions the instruction on the current panorama via ViLEncoder (ViLBERT+BiLSTM) to produce grounded text features; (b) performs object-level referring for each navigable view with ViLPointer using top-k box features; (c) builds orientation-aware navigable-view representations; and (d) maintains a scene memory storing embedded observations and previous actions, processed by Transformer blocks to yield a memory-augmented state s_a_t for action prediction via attention. At inference, it fuses action logits with scene/object grounding scores (l_t + g_sg + g_og) to reduce search bias and shorten trajectories. Trained with mixed IL+RL losses, this design achieves new state-of-the-art results on REVERIE across seen/unseen splits.
paper_title: Scene-Intuitive Agent for Remote Embodied Visual Grounding

problem: Existing visual commonsense reasoning benchmarks are largely Western-centric and do not evaluate models’ ability to use geo- and culture-specific commonsense, leading to unknown robustness on non-Western visual contexts.
method: Introduce GD-VCR, a geo-diverse visual commonsense reasoning benchmark with 328 images and 886 QA pairs from Western, East Asian, South Asian, and African regions. The dataset is built via a three-stage pipeline: (1) collect images with explicit regional characteristics; (2) author high-order, object-referenced questions and answers in English using VCR-style question templates derived by clustering POS-tagged VCR questions with K-Means to minimize format mismatch; (3) construct 4-way multiple-choice by selecting distractors from the original VCR dev set’s correct answers using a relevance model (BERT-base) and a similarity model (stsb-roberta-base) to ensure high relevance (threshold ≥0.9) and low similarity (≤0.2) to the gold answer. Dataset stats (e.g., OOV rate 6.75% vs. VCR dev 12.70%) show comparable text/image properties while emphasizing regional commonsense.
paper_title: Broaden the Vision: Geo-Diverse Visual Commonsense Reasoning

problem: How well do state-of-the-art V+L models trained on Western-centric data generalize to questions requiring geo-specific commonsense, and what drives any performance disparities?
method: Fine-tune VisualBERT and ViLBERT on the original VCR training set and evaluate on GD-VCR across regions, plus conduct a human study with Western annotators. Findings: both models show large accuracy drops on non-Western regions (e.g., VisualBERT West 64.37% vs. East Asia 45.51%, −18.86 pp), while humans exhibit much smaller gaps. Analyze by scenario keywords and observe the largest gaps on culture-heavy scenarios (wedding, religion, festival). Further, annotate additional low-order QA pairs on the same images to disentangle perception vs. reasoning: models perform similarly across regions on low-order recognition (~60% with much smaller West vs. non-West gaps, e.g., −6.41 pp for East Asia) but degrade substantially on high-order reasoning in non-Western regions (e.g., Africa −26.25 pp), implicating missing geo-specific commonsense rather than basic visual recognition.
paper_title: Broaden the Vision: Geo-Diverse Visual Commonsense Reasoning

problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
method: Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.
paper_title: Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training

problem: Feeding many captions into a text-only QA model can exceed encoder length limits and incur quadratic self-attention cost if naively concatenated, hurting scalability and accuracy.
method: Adopt Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD): encode each question+caption pair separately and concatenate only the encoded representations as inputs to the decoder’s cross-attention. This avoids encoder length caps and is more efficient than Fusion-in-Encoder, enabling the model to exploit up to 100 captions. Empirically, FiD continues to improve with more captions while FiE saturates, and in the full plug-and-play pipeline (BLIP + UnifiedQAv2) yields state-of-the-art zero-shot VQA: e.g., PNP-VQA (11B PLM) surpasses Flamingo-80B by 8.5% on VQAv2 and PNP-VQA (738M PLM) outperforms FewVLMlarge on GQA by 9.1%.
paper_title: Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training

problem: Stochastic gradient descent relies on noisy minibatch losses and fixed/tuned step sizes, which can slow or destabilize convergence. There is a need for an optimizer that explicitly models loss noise and adapts its step size automatically without second-order overhead.
method: Model the minibatch loss as a noisy scalar observation of the empirical risk, ˆL_k(x) = ˆL(x) + v_k with v_k ~ N(0, R_k), and cast training as adapting parameters so that ˆL_k tracks a target ˆL_target,k. Apply an Extended Kalman Filter with measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x) and constant-mean state dynamics, but make it scalable by approximating the posterior covariance P_k as a scaled identity and replacing H_k^T H_k with its largest eigenvalue, so the scalar innovation S_k is trivial to invert. This yields KOALA-V, an SGD-like update whose effective learning rate adapts automatically based on the current loss, its gradient, P_k, and R_k; furthermore, scheduling ˆL_target,k provides a loss-adaptivity mechanism equivalent to a learning-rate schedule.
paper_title: KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity

problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
method: Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.
paper_title: KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity

problem: Kalman-based optimization must scale to very deep networks and remain stable, with theoretical guarantees where possible.
method: Introduce a layer-wise approximation: replace the scalar observation with a B-dimensional vector of identical observations and use a block-diagonal H_k so the update decomposes into B layer-wise equations. Approximate each block’s H_k^T H_k S_k^{-1} by its largest block eigenvalue and take the maximum across blocks to approximate the full term, which empirically improves convergence. Provide convergence analysis (Theorem 1) showing that under Lipschitz and gradient-norm conditions and with a decreasing target-risk schedule, ˆL(x_k) converges and the gradient norm tends to zero in the limit.
paper_title: KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity

problem: Existing visual grounding focuses on objects and often relies on explicit referring expressions; in people-centric grounding, using real names causes extreme sparsity and biased cues, and there is no large-scale benchmark that forces contextual reasoning about human interactions rather than identity memorization.
method: Introduce the Who's Waldo dataset (~271K image–caption pairs) for person-centric visual grounding where all person names in captions are masked to [NAME]. Automatically construct ground-truth links by: (a) detecting people and estimating faces/keypoints (MMDetection SAC Cascade R-CNN + MMPose DarkPose), (b) extracting and clustering person mentions with FLAIR NER and AllenNLP coreference, (c) associating referred people with Wikimedia identities that have primary face images, and (d) computing FaceNet embeddings and solving a minimum-weight bipartite matching with a 0.46 similarity threshold to obtain partial name–box links. Validate test annotations with AMT to retain >98.5% accurate links and release identity-disjoint train/val/test splits.
paper_title: Who's Waldo? Linking People Across Text and Images

problem: Given an image with multiple detected people and a caption where person names are masked, link each referred person (mention cluster) to the correct person box using only contextual visual–linguistic cues, while ignoring unreferred people.
method: Adapt UNITER to learn joint image–text representations: extract spatial-visual features for each detected person and token/position embeddings for text, represent each referred person by the average embedding over its [NAME] mentions, and form a box–name cosine similarity matrix. Train with (i) intra-image supervised cross-entropy losses over softmax-normalized rows and columns of the similarity matrix for ground-truth links, (ii) an inter-image binary matching loss using positive/negative pairs from single-name/single-box samples, and (iii) an unlinked-box classification loss by introducing a learned null-name and selecting small/blurry faces as positives to encourage ignoring unreferred boxes. At inference, pick the most similar box per name; the approach achieves 63.5% accuracy on Who's Waldo, surpassing strong baselines and heuristics (e.g., 57.7% for L→R Largest), with ablations showing L_intra and UNITER pretraining are critical.
paper_title: Who's Waldo? Linking People Across Text and Images

problem: VLA models often depend on large VLMs and heavy robotic pretraining, and it is unclear which vision-language representations are most effective for mapping to actions.
method: The paper conducts a systematic study of VL-to-action bridging conditions by comparing Raw VLM features versus learnable ActionQuery features, and single-layer versus all-layer conditioning. Experiments on LIBERO-Long show that middle-layer Raw features outperform deep-layer Raw features, deep-layer ActionQuery features outperform shallow-layer ones, and using all layers generally beats single layers. Guided by these findings, the method selects both all-layer Raw and all-layer ActionQuery latents as conditions for the policy to maximize actionable multimodal information transfer.
paper_title: VLA-ADAPTER: AN EFFECTIVE PARADIGM FOR TINY-SCALE VISION-LANGUAGE-ACTION MODEL

problem: How to efficiently and effectively map multimodal VL representations to continuous action with a tiny backbone and minimal tuning, while remaining robust even when the backbone is frozen.
method: The paper introduces a lightweight L1-based Policy with Bridge Attention that operates layer-by-layer in sync with the VLM depth. At each layer, the action latent attends via two cross-attentions to (i) Raw features with a learnable gate tanh(g) that modulates their injection and (ii) ActionQuery features concatenated with a learned proprioceptive embedding, plus a self-attention on the action latent. This design autonomously injects the most useful conditions into the action space and is trained end-to-end; when the backbone is frozen, only ActionQuery and the policy are learned. The approach achieves high success rates and fast throughput on LIBERO and CALVIN using a 0.5B backbone without robotic-data pretraining, and remains strong with a frozen backbone.
paper_title: VLA-ADAPTER: AN EFFECTIVE PARADIGM FOR TINY-SCALE VISION-LANGUAGE-ACTION MODEL

problem: Bridging VL to action often fails when VLMs are frozen or when the interface between perception and policy is poorly designed (e.g., masked zero tokens), limiting learnability and efficiency.
method: The method employs learnable ActionQuery tokens that are inserted into the VLM input sequence (rather than masked zeros) so they fully participate in attention and can be trained from scratch even with a frozen VLM. The ActionQuery tokens are later combined with proprioceptive embeddings in Bridge Attention for action generation. An ablation on token count finds too few queries limit multimodal aggregation and too many cause redundancy; 64 ActionQuery tokens strike the best balance, underpinning strong performance and explaining why the approach succeeds with a frozen backbone.
paper_title: VLA-ADAPTER: AN EFFECTIVE PARADIGM FOR TINY-SCALE VISION-LANGUAGE-ACTION MODEL

problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
method: Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.
paper_title: Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding

problem: Reduce multimodal hallucinations at inference time without retraining by counteracting the VLM’s progressive shift toward the language prior while preserving fluency.
method: Propose Multi-Modal Mutual-Information Decoding (M3ID), which models conditioned logits as a fading-memory interpolation and derives an intervention that adds a time-varying correction ((1−γ_t)/γ_t)(l_c−l_u) to the conditioned logits l_c, where γ_t = exp(−λt) and l_u are unconditioned logits. This maximizes mutual information between image and tokens (amplifying tokens that ‘surprise’ the language prior) and suppresses the correction when the model is highly confident (max probability ≥ α) to avoid penalizing obvious tokens. M3ID is training-free, requires only an additional unconditioned forward pass, and improves grounding on LLaVA: e.g., for 13B, CHAIRi drops from 7.4 to 5.5 and POPE All accuracy rises from 63.8% to 77.5%.
paper_title: Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding

problem: Further strengthen visual grounding in VLMs without human annotations by learning a generation policy that prefers image-grounded continuations over language-prior-driven ones.
method: Recast prompt amplification as a preference optimization problem and pair M3ID with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Generate self-supervised preference pairs where the positive y_w is sampled from the M3ID-enhanced conditioned model p*(y|x,c) and the negative y_l from the unconditioned model p(y|x), with negatives constrained by appending the first M3ID-generated sentence to the prompt. Fine-tune with the DPO loss to prefer grounded continuations relative to a reference model, yielding additional gains over M3ID alone (e.g., LLaVA-13B CHAIRi 5.5→5.3 and POPE All 77.5%→79.2%).
paper_title: Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding

problem: Dense per-point 3D maps that fuse 2D foundation model features are memory-inefficient, hard to maintain, and lack structure; robots need scalable, open-vocabulary scene representations built online from RGB-D.
