method: ConceptGraphs performs object-centric 3D mapping by running class-agnostic 2D segmentation (e.g., SAM), extracting per-mask CLIP/DINO embeddings, projecting masked regions to 3D, and associating detections across views using a combined geometric nearest-neighbor overlap and semantic (normalized cosine) similarity with greedy assignment. Matched detections are fused by averaging features and merging/downsampling point clouds to form compact object nodes, enabling open-vocabulary reasoning without any 3D training/finetuning and supporting efficient, dynamic updates with a low memory footprint.
paper_title: ConceptGraphs : Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Perception and Planning

problem: Existing 3D scene graphs are closed-vocabulary and often rely on trained relation predictors, limiting flexible spatial and commonsense reasoning needed for planning.
method: ConceptGraphs constructs open-vocabulary scene graphs by captioning each object via an LVLM across its best views and summarizing with an LLM, then proposing sparse connectivity by computing pairwise 3D bounding-box IoU and pruning with a minimum spanning tree. For each candidate edge, an LLM infers spatial/commonsense relations (e.g., on, in, likely stored-in) from the object captions and 3D locations, yielding concise, semantically rich edges without training a relation model.
paper_title: ConceptGraphs : Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Perception and Planning

problem: Robots must ground abstract natural-language queries (including affordances and negations) to physical scenes to plan navigation and manipulation actions.
method: The scene graph is serialized to JSON (id, bbox extents/centers, tag, caption) and given to an LLM to retrieve relevant objects and return their poses for downstream controllers. An LVLM validates target presence; if missing, the LLM proposes plausible containers/locations; the LLM also infers traversability (pushability) from captions. This enables object retrieval, navigation, search-and-relocate, and open-vocabulary pick-and-place on real platforms (Jackal UGV, Spot Arm) using only off-the-shelf LVLM/LLM components.
paper_title: ConceptGraphs : Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Perception and Planning

problem: In REVERIE, concise high-level instructions and limited supervision make scene- or object-only visual representations insufficient for aligning viewpoints with instructions and predicting correct actions in unseen environments.
method: Construct a spatio-temporal knowledge graph per navigable direction by fusing detected objects with external commonsense from ConceptNet. Refine this knowledge via a CLIP-based selector that ranks and keeps the top-k facts using similarity to the whole image and its objects. Encode concepts as text (CLIP-T) with node-type and directional encodings (object orientation w.r.t. agent), and link nodes using ConceptNet relations while adding a history node to capture temporal context.
paper_title: Augmented Commonsense Knowledge for Remote Object Grounding

problem: Standard cross-modal transformers ignore graph structure and lack a mechanism to leverage temporal concept history, weakening concept–instruction grounding for navigation.
method: Introduce ACK’s knowledge graph-aware cross-modal encoder: queries are the concatenation of concept nodes and the previous-step concept history, while instruction embeddings serve as keys/values. Replace vanilla self-attention with a graph-aware variant that incorporates the knowledge-graph adjacency when computing attention weights. Aggregate multi-head concept attentions into a single per-direction concept vector via learned attention and weighted sum, yielding enriched concept features that enhance the agent’s visual representation.
paper_title: Augmented Commonsense Knowledge for Remote Object Grounding

problem: Local action selection in VLN lacks explicit commonsense reasoning, leading to suboptimal choices even when global planning is strong (e.g., in DUET).
method: Add a commonsense-based decision-making pipeline that reasons over the aggregated concept features to output a local navigation score and an object score. Fuse these scores with the baseline’s dynamic fusion during policy learning, and train with an additional loss for this pipeline alongside SAP and OG. Ablations show this head, together with graph-aware attention and concept history, consistently improves navigation and grounding metrics on REVERIE.
paper_title: Augmented Commonsense Knowledge for Remote Object Grounding

problem: Current VLM evaluations largely assume text inputs are consistent with images, leaving it unclear how models behave when visual and textual information conflict or when text is noisy, misleading, or irrelevant.
method: Construct a cross-task benchmark that injects three controlled textual variations—Match, Corruption, and Irrelevance—into four vision-centric datasets (VQAv2, DocVQA, MathVista, and a real-world Brand Recognition task). Define the Text Preference Ratio (TPR) to quantify whether models choose text- vs image-based answers when they disagree, alongside normalized accuracy to measure robustness. Evaluating 10 VLMs reveals a pervasive 'blind faith in text' (high TPR and large accuracy drops under corrupted text), especially in open models, highlighting safety risks in realistic settings such as brand recognition on webpages.
paper_title: Words or Vision: Do Vision-Language Models Have Blind Faith in Text?

problem: VLMs over-trust misleading text, causing large performance degradation under corrupted context; a practical training recipe is needed to reduce this text bias while preserving the ability to leverage helpful text.
method: Apply supervised fine-tuning with text augmentation that mixes a small dataset (~1,000 examples) evenly across five data types: text-only, original VQA, and VQA with matched, corrupted, and irrelevant context. Using standard SFT with LoRA, this approach substantially reduces text bias and boosts performance under corruption (e.g., LLaVA-NeXT-7B corruption accuracy from 28.69% to 71.25%; Qwen2-VL-7B from 50.79% to 82.72%) while maintaining or improving macro accuracy across datasets. Ablations show text-only data prevents blanket rejection of text, and scaling SFT data further decreases reliance on corrupted text while preserving effective use of matched text.
paper_title: Words or Vision: Do Vision-Language Models Have Blind Faith in Text?

problem: The root cause of VLMs’ text bias is not well understood, limiting principled ways to improve cross-modal reliability.
method: Provide a theoretical ERM-based generalization analysis for transformer VLMs trained on mixed pure-text (size N) and multi-modal (size M) data. The bounds show expected losses on text vs multi-modal inputs depend on dataset imbalance and cross-modal approximation error; when N ≫ M (typical due to heavy LLM pretraining), the model achieves lower loss on pure-text than multi-modal data, predisposing it to favor textual information. This explains the observed 'blind faith in text' and motivates more balanced training distributions.
paper_title: Words or Vision: Do Vision-Language Models Have Blind Faith in Text?

problem: LVLMs lack a rigorous, knowledge-neutral evaluation of their fundamental abilities to perceive and reason over visual graphs; prior work mainly targets complex graph-theory or multi-hop problems, obscuring whether failures stem from basic perception or higher-order reasoning.
method: Introduce VGCURE, a comprehensive benchmark of 22 tasks spanning 9 understanding and 13 reasoning categories across 10 anonymized synthetic and real-world graph types, with both QA and fact-checking samples (223,646 total). Graphs are anonymized to minimize leakage from pretrained knowledge, and tasks probe core competencies such as node/edge counting, neighbor/degree queries, 2-hop relational reasoning, relation analogy, and path existence/shortest paths. Broad zero-shot evaluations on 14 LVLMs reveal systematic weaknesses, especially on relational/structural tasks (e.g., Edge Number Query and Neighbor Query) and under increasing structural complexity.
paper_title: Benchmarking and Improving Large Vision-Language Models for Fundamental Visual Graph Understanding and Reasoning

problem: State-of-the-art LVLMs underperform on structural/relational aspects of visual graphs (e.g., edges, multi-hop relations), indicating insufficient structure-aware perception and reasoning.
method: Propose MCDGRAPH, a structure-aware, self-supervised fine-tuning framework comprising three tasks: (1) Masked Graph Infilling that predicts masked nodes/edges using the image plus corresponding text triples; (2) Contrastive Graph Discrimination that trains models to distinguish isomorphic/non-isomorphic graphs and be robust to layout changes; (3) Graph Description that compels holistic structural summaries (node/edge counts and entity names). Using LoRA for efficient adaptation and 20k anonymized training samples, MCDGRAPH substantially boosts LVLM performance, particularly on edge-related tasks (e.g., InternVL2 EN QA 9.78→40.45; Qwen2‑VL EN QA 16.38→25.92) and improves NR; it generalizes to unseen visual styles and naming conventions and yields gains on downstream graph tasks (e.g., VisionGraph Maximum Flow and FACTKG).
paper_title: Benchmarking and Improving Large Vision-Language Models for Fundamental Visual Graph Understanding and Reasoning

problem: It is unclear whether LVLM failures on graph tasks arise mainly from weak visual graph perception or from limitations in the underlying language model’s graph reasoning.
method: Establish a diagnostic comparison by converting visual graphs to text triples and evaluating backbone LLMs versus their LVLM counterparts, coupled with structural/complexity impact studies and a fine-grained error taxonomy. Results show LVLMs lag behind their backbone LLMs on both understanding and reasoning, with the largest drop on edge counting (EN), indicating a visual perception bottleneck; overall LLM accuracies remain below 45%, evidencing limited graph-reasoning capability. Error analysis highlights Relation Misunderstanding as the dominant failure mode (54%), followed by Structural Hallucination (17%), among others.
paper_title: Benchmarking and Improving Large Vision-Language Models for Fundamental Visual Graph Understanding and Reasoning

problem: Medical vision-language models often hallucinate and misalign text with images, partly because existing preference optimization data in medicine lacks clinical relevance and rarely stresses attention to lesion regions.
method: MMedPO curates clinically meaningful multimodal preference pairs via two dispreference strategies: (a) plausible hallucinated responses, obtained by sampling target Med-LVLMs and using GPT-4o to evaluate/select—or generate when needed—the most medically inaccurate yet plausible dispreferred answer; and (b) lesion-region neglect, created by using a medical visual tool (e.g., MedKLIP) to localize disease regions and injecting localized noise there while keeping the ground-truth response, making the noised-input pair dispreferred. The merged dataset (Dt ∪ Dv) yields harder, clinically grounded contrasts that better drive alignment.
paper_title: MMedPO: Aligning Medical Vision-Language Models with Clinical-Aware Multimodal Preference Optimization

problem: Treating all preference samples equally leads to suboptimal alignment because clinical informativeness varies, and single-LLM evaluation of medical relevance can be biased.
method: MMedPO assigns a quantified clinical relevance score to each preference pair and uses it as a per-sample weight in a weighted DPO objective. For text-hallucination pairs, a multi-agent collaboration of multiple Med-LLMs conducts iterative debate to reach a consensus clinical relevance score for the dispreferred response; for lesion-noise pairs, confidence from the visual tool’s lesion heatmaps serves as the relevance score. Scores are normalized and clipped to stable ranges before being injected as weights into the DPO loss, yielding more clinically aligned and factual outputs.
paper_title: MMedPO: Aligning Medical Vision-Language Models with Clinical-Aware Multimodal Preference Optimization

problem: Generic vision–language models are trained on 2D images and lack mechanisms to ground language to objects situated in 3D embodied environments, where spatial relations and local context matter for remote object grounding.
method: Adapt a pretrained VL backbone (ViLBERT) to 3D by replacing 2D box encodings with 3D region positional embeddings (x, y, z, r) normalized relative to the observing viewpoint, with depth estimated from the median depth inside each region. Enrich local context by adding Mask-RCNN proposal regions and average features from neighboring viewpoints to each viewpoint batch. Fine-tune with a sigmoid/BCE objective over regions within a sampled viewpoint and apply viewpoint-level negative augmentation (random viewpoints without the target) to suppress false positives. These 3D encodings and context cues significantly improve grounding, as shown by ablations removing 3D encoding and context proposals that reduce RGS/SR (Tables 3–4).
paper_title: RREx-BoT: Remote Referring Expressions with a Bag of Tricks

problem: Remote grounding in familiar homes requires selecting a referent from tens to hundreds of thousands of proposals, but VL models produce within-batch scores that are not directly comparable across batches, making large-scale 3D ranking challenging.
method: Build a topological map via frontier-based exploration, but cap exploration to L steps (derived from training path statistics) to bound the search space. At inference, group proposals by viewpoint and score each viewpoint’s regions (with local neighborhood features) in separate batches, then perform a global argmax over the per-viewpoint best scores to select the referent; navigate via the shortest path to the predicted target. Viewpoint grouping calibrates cross-batch comparisons and, together with the distance limit, dramatically reduces errors; removing grouping or the distance cap causes large drops in RGS/SR (Table 3; Table 4). This scalable batching and search strategy yields state-of-the-art grounding on REVERIE and FAO.
paper_title: RREx-BoT: Remote Referring Expressions with a Bag of Tricks

problem: There is no focused benchmark to test whether vision-language models can recognize human actions when salient contextual objects are absent, making it hard to isolate true gesture understanding from background or prop cues.
method: Introduce MIME, a video-based QA benchmark of 86 mimed actions built from motion-capture retargeted to 3D characters and rendered for systematic remixing. Each action has 10 controlled variants by perturbing character (male/female/adversarial suit), background (aligned, misaligned, blank), and viewpoint (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°), totaling 860 samples. MIME supports both multiple-choice (choice condition) and free-form short answers (naming condition), enabling robust ablations of recognition under different contexts and angles (Fig. 2–3; §§2.1–2.3, 3.1).
paper_title: Can Vision Language Models Understand Mimed Actions?

problem: Do current VLMs genuinely understand human gestures, or do they rely on contextual shortcuts?
method: Evaluate diverse open and API VLMs and humans on MIME and a control set, REAL, which contains live-action videos of the same actions with full context. Humans perform near-perfectly on both, but VLMs drop sharply on MIME (e.g., best zero-shot 52.3% MC and 19.8% FF), and under adversarial settings many models fall below 10%. Aligned backgrounds strongly boost accuracy while misaligned/blank harm it, indicating reliance on scene context rather than motion (Fig. 5; Table 1; §4.1–4.2).
paper_title: Can Vision Language Models Understand Mimed Actions?

problem: Can prompting or light adaptation improve VLM mime recognition, and where do models fail?
method: Test Chain-of-Thought, few-shot in-context learning, and small-scale fine-tuning. Few-shot ICL notably boosts API models in MC (e.g., GPT-4o Mini from 41.9% to 74.4% on base; up to 94.2% with aligned background), but FF remains low; fine-tuning on small splits shows little/no gains (Table 4; §5.1–5.2). A failure-mode analysis of Gemini 1.5 Flash with CoT shows most errors stem from incorrect motion descriptions (54% partially correct, 16% completely incorrect) rather than only misinterpretation; aligned backgrounds correct 43% of prior errors, while misaligned backgrounds induce 24% confusion (§5.3).
paper_title: Can Vision Language Models Understand Mimed Actions?

problem: Video dialog requires tracking fine-grained, evolving information across multiple modalities (video, audio, dialog history, current question). Prior models typically track only one modality or rely on synthetic datasets, limiting real-world performance.
method: MST MIXER introduces explicit multi-modal dialog state tracking on top of a BART-based VLM by inserting modality-specific state tokens and, at selected encoder layers, selecting K salient tokens per modality via their attention to the state token. It then performs a two-stage divide-and-conquer mixing: Stage I learns per-modality local latent graphs to refine features; Stage II parses all local graphs into a global, modality-agnostic latent graph to refine cross-modal structure. The refined node features are scattered back into the VLM hidden states and used to update the state tokens, improving answer generation in the wild.
paper_title: Multi-Modal Video Dialog State Tracking in the Wild

problem: The relational structure among multi-modal constituents is unknown, and directly learning a single global cross-modal graph is unstable and ineffective.
method: A novel multi-modal graph structure learning framework uses variational inference with separate local and global ELBO losses and a two-stream design. For each modality, an initial adjacency is built via kNN (k=4); the missing edges are learned as latent graphs using: (i) a stream that predicts edges by cosine similarity with learnable per-modality weights and propagates features via APPNP, and (ii) a stream that first refines features using the initial graphs and then predicts a second latent graph; the two are combined. Multi-modal conditioning incorporates other modalities’ features when inferring each modality’s latent graph. The global graph is initialized as a block-diagonal of local graphs and learned similarly, yielding stable improvements over transformer-only and random-graph baselines.
paper_title: Multi-Modal Video Dialog State Tracking in the Wild

problem: Injecting graph-refined multi-modal features into a pretrained sequence model can destabilize training if not carefully integrated.
method: MST MIXER inserts MIXER layers after every Δ encoder layers, updates modality state embeddings by averaging the refined node features, and blends the graph-refined features back into the corresponding BART hidden states via a scatter operation with a residual coefficient λ. This plug-and-play integration yields consistent gains, with ablations identifying λ=0.9 and Δ=4 as effective settings.
paper_title: Multi-Modal Video Dialog State Tracking in the Wild

problem: Robots need a unified way to understand and act on both tools and articulated objects, jointly reasoning about 3D motion constraints (e.g., joint axes/types, 6D pose) and fine-grained affordances (grasp/functional regions). Prior work often treats tools and articulated objects separately and lacks general task reasoning.
method: UniAff introduces a structured, object-centric part representation ψi = {Ai, Bi, Gi, Fi, Ji, Li} that encodes per-part 6D pose (Ai), rotated 2D part boxes (Bi), grasp (Gi) and functional (Fi) affordance boxes, manipulation type (Ji), and a descriptive sentence (Li). Rotated BBOXes are defined by four vertices to compactly capture geometry/orientation, and manipulation types span four articulation classes (bottle cap, revolute, sliding, prismatic) plus a 'freedom object' type for 6-DoF tools. The formulation is trained via VQA-style prompts over four tasks (2D-Part-Detection, 6D-Pose-Detection incl. type, 2D-Grasp-Affordance, 2D-Functional-Affordance), enabling a single model to infer all parameters required for manipulation.
paper_title: UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models

problem: There is a lack of large-scale, richly labeled data covering both tools and articulated objects with consistent part-level 3D constraints and affordances; collecting/annotating real data is costly.
method: UniAff builds a synthetic, auto-labeled dataset grounded in the unified ψ representation: 600 tools across 12 categories (230 real scans from PACE and OmniObject3D plus 370 newly scanned) with grasp/functional part segmentation and 6D pose; and 900 articulated objects across 19 categories from PartNet-Mobility with modified meshes to separate handles. Near-realistic rendering produces diverse scenes and object states, automatically labeling {A,B,G,F,J,L}, including joint axes for articulated parts and state-aware grasp affordance sampling. The dataset is organized into VQA tasks (≈130K each for part, pose/type, grasp; 10K for functional) for multi-task training.
paper_title: UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models

problem: Vision-language models struggle to jointly capture fine-grained local affordances and global 3D motion constraints from images, especially under resolution limits and with single-encoder designs.
method: UniAff fine-tunes an MLLM based on SPHINX with an LLaMA2 backbone using an 'Any Resolution' strategy that tiles 448×448 inputs into sub-images to preserve details. A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.
paper_title: UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models

problem: Text-to-3D generation lacks a robust, human-aligned, reference-free evaluation across appearance, surface quality, and text fidelity; existing similarity metrics require ground truth or capture only prompt alignment.
method: Gen3DEval is a task-tuned vision-language model built on LLaVA/Llama3 that early-fuses visual and text tokens to judge which of two 3D objects is better along a requested dimension. It ingests up to eight multi-view renders (RGB and surface normals) of the objects and a question, and outputs a preference. Training proceeds in two stages: (1) pre-train only the vision-to-language projector with image-summary VQA over multi-view 3D renders; (2) supervised fine-tune the LLM + projector on pairwise preference VQA for appearance, surface, and text fidelity. CLIP is selected as the image encoder after ablations for best generalization, enabling a reference-free evaluator aligned with human judgments.
paper_title: Gen3DEval: Using vLLMs for Automatic Evaluation of Generated 3D Objects

problem: There is a scarcity of diverse, labeled supervision that captures 3D-specific artifacts and nuanced text fidelity differences needed to train a reliable 3D quality evaluator.
method: The authors curate a composite dataset combining multi-view renders and captions of approximately 140k artist-created meshes, large-scale human pairwise preferences over outputs from numerous 3D generative methods, and extensive synthetic comparisons. Synthetic data is produced by perturbing meshes (e.g., Laplacian smoothing, bevels/extrusions, texture blur/seams), adding NeRF/Gaussian-splat artifacts (floaters, transparency, disconnected parts), and creating textual/appearance variants via Llama3.2 and multi-view video diffusion; low-relevance pairs are filtered using CLIP similarity. These VQA-style pairs supervise Gen3DEval to recognize appearance, surface, and text fidelity cues typical of modern 3D generators.
paper_title: Gen3DEval: Using vLLMs for Automatic Evaluation of Generated 3D Objects

problem: There is no standardized, scalable protocol to benchmark and rank text-to-3D methods across multiple quality dimensions without relying on ground-truth assets.
method: Gen3DEval-Bench is introduced with 80 diverse prompts (balanced animate/inanimate and single/composite objects) and a metric pipeline. For each prompt and method pair, four views per asset are sampled; Gen3DEval performs pairwise judgments (using the prompt only for text fidelity), and decisions are parsed and aggregated with an ELO rating to produce per-dimension and overall rankings. Applying this protocol to 10 popular 3D generators yields a leaderboard and demonstrates stronger alignment with human preferences than GPT-4o and classical metrics like CLIP/ImageReward/PickScore.
paper_title: Gen3DEval: Using vLLMs for Automatic Evaluation of Generated 3D Objects

problem: Most LVLMs encode images and videos into separate feature spaces, causing misalignment before projection; shallow projection layers then struggle to let a single LLM learn cross-modal interactions, degrading unified image–video reasoning.
method: Video-LLaVA performs alignment before projection by using LanguageBind encoders to map both images and videos into a shared language/text feature space, yielding a unified visual representation. Initialized from OpenCLIP and further aligned with large-scale video–text pairs, these unified features are passed through a single shared projection layer into the LLM. Ablations replacing the aligned image encoder with separated MAE/CLIP variants show consistent drops, confirming that unified representation improves both video QA (Figure 4; Table 2) and image QA/toolkits (Table 5), and the full model surpasses Video-ChatGPT by 5.8–18.6% across MSRVTT, MSVD, TGIF, and ActivityNet.
paper_title: Video-LLaVA: Learning United Visual Representation by Alignment Before Projection

problem: Training with a single modality or training images and videos separately underuses cross-modal complementarities, limiting a model’s ability to understand both images and videos with one LLM.
method: Video-LLaVA adopts a two-stage, dynamically joint training pipeline that mixes images and videos in every batch. Stage 1 ‘understanding’ trains on large-scale single-turn image/video–text pairs; Stage 2 instruction tuning uses multi-turn image/video instruction datasets with the LLM involved. This efficient setup (1 epoch understanding) lets modalities mutually enhance learning: adding images improves all four video QA datasets (Table 6), while adding videos boosts 8/9 image benchmarks and reduces hallucination (Figure 5; Table 4). Overall, the jointly trained model outperforms specialist baselines, including consistent gains over Video-ChatGPT on video QA and strong results on image benchmarks.
paper_title: Video-LLaVA: Learning United Visual Representation by Alignment Before Projection

problem: Deep real-vs-fake classifiers trained on images from one generator family (e.g., GANs) fail to generalize to unseen families like diffusion and autoregressive models; they latch onto model-specific low-level artifacts so the real class becomes a sink and diffusion fakes are often predicted as real.
method: Perform detection without learning task-specific features by classifying in a frozen, task-agnostic feature space from a large pretrained vision-language model. Concretely, embed images with a frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 visual encoder and use cosine-distance nearest neighbor against real/fake feature banks built from a single generator’s data. This avoids overfitting to one model’s fingerprints and yields strong cross-family generalization, improving over the SoTA [49] by about +15.05 mAP and +25.90% accuracy on unseen diffusion and autoregressive models, and even outperforming an oracle-threshold baseline.
paper_title: Towards Universal Fake Image Detectors that Generalize Across Generative Models

problem: Nearest-neighbor over large feature banks can be memory-intensive; a lightweight detector is desirable while preserving universal generalization across generator families.
method: Freeze the CLIP ViT-L/14 backbone and train only a single linear layer (logistic head) for real-vs-fake (linear probing). This retains the benefits of task-agnostic features with a few hundred trainable parameters, is compute/memory friendly, and matches or exceeds nearest neighbor generalization. It improves over [49] by +19.49 mAP and +23.39% accuracy on unseen diffusion/autoregressive models.
paper_title: Towards Universal Fake Image Detectors that Generalize Across Generative Models

problem: It is unclear which feature spaces and data recipes make fake-image detectors generalize across unseen generator families.
method: Ablations show that internet-scale, caption-supervised CLIP features are crucial: CLIP visual encoders (especially ViT-L/14) separate real/fake far better than ImageNet-pretrained backbones. The approach generalizes regardless of the training fake source (GAN or diffusion), even cross-family (e.g., training on LDM data generalizes to GANs with large mAP gains), and remains robust to reduced training-set size and nearest-neighbor k. These findings provide practical guidance on backbone choice and data requirements for universal fake detection.
paper_title: Towards Universal Fake Image Detectors that Generalize Across Generative Models

problem: Existing VL benchmarks confound spatial reasoning with other skills and real-world priors, making it unclear whether models truly understand basic relations like left/right, on/under, in front of/behind.
method: Introduce three tightly controlled benchmarks where only the spatial preposition varies between caption options: What'sUp (820 real photos in 205 sets controlling lighting/background; each set includes on/under/left/right for the same object pair, enabling pair- and set-wise consistency metrics), COCO-spatial, and GQA-spatial (curated to isolate mutually exclusive prepositions). Evaluate 18 VL models by selecting the correct caption among options that differ only by preposition. All models perform poorly vs. humans (e.g., BLIP-VQA averages ~56% vs. human ~99%), revealing systematic failures in basic spatial reasoning.
paper_title: What's 'up' with vision-language models? Investigating their struggle with spatial reasoning

problem: Why do pre-trained VL models struggle to learn spatial relations despite strong performance on other tasks?
method: Analyze LAION-2B (used for CLIP-style pretraining) and find spatial prepositions are extremely rare (<0.22% after denoising), often ambiguous (e.g., frame-of-reference issues), and seldom required by the contrastive objective, limiting learnable signal. Complement with a representation probe using visual analogies on What'sUp: CLIP embeddings show ~9% analogy accuracy for prepositions versus ~61% for colors (and 100% direct color classification), indicating weak linear structure for spatial relations in learned image features. Together, these diagnoses point to insufficient and noisy supervision for spatial relations during pretraining and objectives that do not incentivize learning them.
paper_title: What's 'up' with vision-language models? Investigating their struggle with spatial reasoning

problem: Can simple modeling or data interventions recover spatial reasoning in current VL models?
method: Test three data-informed interventions: (a) re-normalize image–text scores by caption priors computed on external image sets; yields small/inconsistent gains across datasets. (b) Replace the rare word 'behind' with frequent LAION token 'in the background'; CLIP accuracy on the relevant subset jumps from ~52% to ~67%, suggesting latent but poorly surfaced spatial knowledge. (c) Finetune CLIP on COCO/GQA-spatial, a LAION subset rich in prepositions, and with hard-negative captions made by preposition swaps; results show limited or distribution-specific gains (COCO/GQA up, What'sUp down) and inability to fit hard negatives (very high train loss), indicating naive finetuning and prompting are insufficient without stronger inductive biases or denser supervision.
paper_title: What's 'up' with vision-language models? Investigating their struggle with spatial reasoning

problem: Zero-shot VLMs (e.g., CLIP) struggle to localize and refer to specific regions or keypoints when prompted only via text, and the common crop-based visual prompting discards valuable context.
method: Introduce visual prompt engineering via marking: directly edit the image by overlaying a thin red circle around each candidate region/keypoint while keeping the rest of the image intact, then score compatibility with engineered text prompts using CLIP (compute s(i_a, t_q) and choose argmax). This simple red-circle marker emerges as the most effective among shapes/colors tested and consistently outperforms cropping for naming and localizing keypoints (Tables 1, 4). Applied to referring expression comprehension, circle each proposal (MAttNet boxes), subtract the mean score over negative queries to reduce bias toward universally salient regions, and ensemble CLIP backbones (RN50x16 + ViT-L/14@336) with minor circle-based augmentations (blur/gray outside). This achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot REC on most RefCOCO/RefCOCO+/RefCOCOg splits (Table 5).
paper_title: What does CLIP know about a red circle? Visual prompt engineering for VLMs

problem: It is unclear why such a simple visual marker (a red circle) elicits strong localization abilities in VLMs and how model/data scale affect this emergent behavior.
method: Empirically analyze training-data evidence and scaling. Train a detector to find annotated images in YFCC15M (a CLIP pretraining subset), identify 70 circled/annotated images among a large sample (~0.001% prevalence), showing that such patterns exist but are rare (Fig. 5). Compare many CLIP/OpenCLIP variants across backbones and datasets (WIT-400M, LAION-2B, YFCC-15M, etc.) and observe that larger vision encoders and certain pretraining corpora correlate with markedly better red-circle prompting performance (Table 3, Fig. 6). Conclude that large capacity and large-scale data enable this rare-but-present supervision to induce the emergent marking capability.
paper_title: What does CLIP know about a red circle? Visual prompt engineering for VLMs

problem: Visual prompt engineering may amplify harmful biases when applied to images of people, risking unethical outcomes.
method: Quantify zero-shot classification into positive/neutral/criminal categories on FaceSynthetics and COCO person crops, with and without adding a red circle. Across ViT encoders, adding the circle notably increases the rate of 'criminal' classifications (e.g., +9–32% absolute depending on model/dataset; Table 6), and circled people are more likely to be labeled as missing/suspected murderer (Fig. 8). This reveals that visual prompting can trigger negative associations learned from training data and should not be used on sensitive content without mitigation.
paper_title: What does CLIP know about a red circle? Visual prompt engineering for VLMs

problem: Existing vision-and-language navigation (VLN) benchmarks focus on ground agents and do not capture the challenges of UAV navigation in outdoor, large-scale, 3D environments requiring altitude control, richer spatial reasoning, and obstacle avoidance.
method: Introduce AerialVLN: a UAV-based VLN task, simulator, and dataset built on Unreal Engine 4 with Microsoft AirSim plugins, supporting continuous navigation in 25 city-level, dynamic environments (weather/illumination, moving objects). The agent operates in first-person with RGB and depth inputs and an 8-action, 4-DoF control space (move/turn, ascend/descend, strafe). The dataset contains 8,446 human-piloted trajectories paired with 25,338 crowd-sourced instructions (avg. 83 words), and includes fine-grained sub-path to sub-instruction alignment to facilitate cross-modal grounding and long-horizon reasoning.
paper_title: AerialVLN : Vision-and-Language Navigation for UAVs

problem: Student-forcing training in continuous VLN typically labels ground-truth actions using the shortest path to the goal from the current state, which often conflicts with natural language instructions describing non-shortest human trajectories and amplifies error when the agent deviates.
method: Propose Look-ahead Guidance (LAG) to generate training supervision aligned with the human path: from the current location, (1) take the shortest path back to the human (ground-truth) trajectory, (2) look ahead 10 steps along that trajectory, and (3) set the ground-truth action as the first step on the shortest path to this look-ahead point. Integrating LAG with a Cross-Modal Attention (CMA) baseline improves generalization on AerialVLN-S unseen splits (e.g., SR 4.5%→5.1% and SDTW 1.0%→1.4% on val unseen; SR 4.3%→4.5% and SDTW 1.2%→1.3% on test unseen), indicating better instruction-following under deviation.
paper_title: AerialVLN : Vision-and-Language Navigation for UAVs

problem: Real-world document retrieval must handle heterogeneous formats (HTML, PDFs, slides) and modalities (text, images, layout). Current pipelines depend on parsing and OCR, which is tedious, error-prone, and loses visual/layout information, hurting retrieval—especially for mixed-modality documents.
method: Propose Document Screenshot Embedding (DSE), a unified retrieval paradigm that indexes documents as screenshots, preserving text, images, and layout without preprocessing. DSE is a bi-encoder where a large vision-language model (Phi-3-vision) encodes screenshots and the same language model encodes text queries; the end-of-sequence token embedding is used for both towers. To capture fine-grained textual/visual details, screenshots are resized and cropped into Cx×Cy sub-images; each sub-image yields 576 patch latents plus a global 576, with every four latents concatenated before feeding the LM alongside a prompt containing a special token. Models are trained with InfoNCE using cosine similarity and hard/in-batch negatives (τ=0.02). This design outperforms text/OCR pipelines: on NQ over Wiki-SS it beats BM25 by 17 points in top-1 (46.2 vs 29.5) and on SlideVQA-open it exceeds OCR-based text retrievers by over 15 nDCG@10 points (75.3 vs 55.8), and surpasses CLIP by 11.1 top-1 on NQ and 12.6 nDCG@10 on SlideVQA.
paper_title: Unifying Multimodal Retrieval via Document Screenshot Embedding

problem: There is a lack of large-scale, screenshot-based corpora and open-domain mixed-modality retrieval benchmarks to fairly evaluate methods that avoid content parsing.
method: Construct Wiki-SS, a 1.27M English Wikipedia page screenshot corpus captured via Selenium with a 980×980 window (May 20–23, 2024), and a matched text corpus (first 500 words per page) for fair comparison. For training/eval with Natural Questions, positives/negatives are built by BM25 pooling and answer matching, yielding 49,095 training triplets and 3,610 test queries. Convert SlideVQA into an open-domain slide retrieval task (SlideVQA-open) with 50,714 slide screenshots and create an OCR-derived text counterpart; train with annotated evidence slides as positives and same-deck slides as hard negatives. These resources enable large-scale evaluation of screenshot-based retrieval versus OCR/text baselines.
paper_title: Unifying Multimodal Retrieval via Document Screenshot Embedding

problem: Referring image segmentation with pre-trained dual-encoder vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) needs strong cross-modal interaction and spatial inductive biases, but full fine-tuning is costly; keeping backbones frozen typically hurts performance.
method: Introduce Bridger, an adapter-like module inserted at intermediate stages of frozen image and text encoders to enable early, bidirectional interaction while injecting vision-specific inductive biases. Bridger comprises: (1) a Zoom Layer that reshapes visual features with convolution/deconvolution (for ViT and ResNet) to capture local spatial priors and control sequence length; and (2) an Interactor that performs cross-modal attention using one modality as query and the other as keys/values, then projects and residually adds outputs back into the next backbone stage. This design updates only 1.61%–3.38% of backbone parameters yet matches or surpasses full fine-tuning; ablations show removing Bridger drops oIoU by ~3.33%.
paper_title: Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation

problem: Accurate pixel-level masks require efficient alignment of multi-scale visual features with sentence-level language while keeping the encoders frozen and decoder lightweight.
method: Design a lightweight decoder with: (i) a Hierarchical Alignment Module that concatenates multi-stage visual features and fuses them with a 1×1 convolution plus 2D coordinate encoding; (ii) a Global Alignment Module that applies Transformer self- and cross-attention between the fused visual sequence and the global sentence embedding with positional encodings to produce a cross-modal representation; and (iii) a Projector that converts text features into dynamic Conv2D kernels applied to cross-modal features to predict masks. Training uses a text-to-pixel contrastive loss to align sentence and pixel representations. Removing HA or GA reduces oIoU by 12.36% and 8.85%, respectively, while the decoder maintains fewer tunable parameters than prior RIS heads.
paper_title: Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation

problem: There is little systematic study of parameter-efficient tuning (PET) for dense multimodal tasks like referring image segmentation, making it unclear how PET methods compare under controlled settings.
method: Conduct a controlled comparison on RefCOCO with standardized reduction factor and comparable trainable parameter counts across PET baselines (Adapter, Conv Adapter, Compacter, CoOp, LoRA). The proposed Bridger+decoder framework (ETRIS) improves oIoU by 1.60%–3.19% over PET baselines and by 3.33% over a fixed-backbone setup, and scales across ResNet-50/101 and ViT-B backbones. Parameter analysis shows markedly fewer total tunable parameters (only ~10.75%–20.15% of other methods) while achieving competitive or superior accuracy.
paper_title: Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation

problem: Data filtering for vision-language model pretraining is typically compute-agnostic and existing scaling laws treat web data as homogeneous. This fails to capture that high-quality data is limited and loses utility with repetition, and offers no way to predict performance for mixtures of different-quality pools without costly joint training.
method: Introduce compute-aware scaling laws tailored to heterogeneous web data. Each data pool is parameterized by a utility exponent b and a repetition half-life τ, modeling exponential decay in utility per repetition (δ = (1/2)^(1/τ)) and yielding a closed-form for repeated epochs. For mixtures, prove Theorem 1: the effective utility b_eff at repetition k is a weighted mean of per-pool utilities and the half-life scales linearly with the number of mixed pools (τ̂ = p·τ); in contrastive CLIP training, account for O(N^2) comparisons so τ̂ scales with total pool size (τ̂ = (N̂/N)·τ). Using these, estimate performance for any pool combination and compute budget without ever training on the combinations.
paper_title: Scaling Laws for Data Filtering: Data Curation cannot be Compute Agnostic

problem: Practitioners must choose filtering thresholds that remain optimal as compute changes, but aggressive filtering can underperform at high compute due to diminishing utility with repeated exposure (the quality–quantity tradeoff). A practical, compute-aware curation procedure is needed to pick the best mixture for a given budget.
method: Fit scaling parameters (a, b, d, τ) for individual quality buckets (e.g., Top 10%, 10–20% by T-MARS or CLIP score) via small-epoch training and a stable grid search, then use the proposed scaling laws to predict performance curves for mixtures across compute budgets and select the pareto-optimal filtering strategy. Empirically demonstrate the QQT: LAION-style 10% filtering outperforms unfiltered data at low compute but is overtaken beyond ~450M samples; predicted optimal strategies switch from Top 10% at ≤100M to Top 20% at 100–350M, and Top 30% beyond 350M. Validate the laws on large-scale CLIP models (up to 34B samples), achieving accurate error predictions when accounting for repetition-driven utility decay.
paper_title: Scaling Laws for Data Filtering: Data Curation cannot be Compute Agnostic

problem: Conventional batch normalization (BN) violates the hierarchical, task-specific i.i.d. assumptions of meta-learning: global running statistics (CBN/BRN) lead to poor accuracy (especially with small batches/few shots), while transductive BN (TBN) uses target-set statistics at test time, yielding brittle behavior and unfair advantages.
method: Introduce Meta-Batch Normalization (METABN): compute batch moments from the task’s context set D_τ and use them to normalize both context and target examples at meta-train and meta-test time. This makes normalization task-local and non-transductive, aligning with the hierarchical model (µ,σ absorbed into ψ_τ). Empirically, METABN improves training speed while maintaining accuracy versus CBN/BRN, but suffers when |D_τ| is small due to high-variance moment estimates (e.g., Section 4.1; lines 145–149; Table 1 and discussion lines 205–209).
paper_title: TASKNORM: Rethinking Batch Normalization for Meta-Learning

problem: A non-transductive normalization that remains accurate and efficient across varying, often small, context sizes is needed; METABN alone degrades with few-shot tasks, and TBN—while strong—depends transductively on the test batch and fails under streaming or class-imbalanced evaluation.
method: Propose TASKNORM: blend task-level context-set BN moments with per-instance, non-transductive moments (from IN or LN) for each example using pooled mean/variance. A learnable blending factor α=SIGMOID(SCALE·|D_τ|+OFFSET) is trained, enabling each layer to adaptively weight context versus instance statistics as a function of context size. TASKNORM is non-transductive (uses only D_τ and the current x*), has variants TASKNORM-I/L (using IN/LN), and includes RN as a special case (fixed α=|D_τ|/(1+|D_τ|)). Experiments across 14 datasets and multiple meta-learners (MAML, ProtoNets, CNAPS) show TASKNORM consistently improves accuracy and training speed; on Meta-Dataset, TASKNORM-I attains the best average rank (e.g., CNAPS rank 2.38, ProtoNets 2.50) and often outperforms transductive TBN, while remaining robust when evaluated one example or one class at a time (Sections 4.2–5; lines 153–166, 191–193, 237–243, 283; Table 2–3; Table D.2).
paper_title: TASKNORM: Rethinking Batch Normalization for Meta-Learning

problem: Vision Transformers keep a fixed spatial resolution across layers, leading to high compute and suboptimal speed–accuracy trade-offs, especially on CPUs and edge hardware.
method: Introduce a multi-stage, pyramid Vision Transformer where resolution is progressively reduced via shrinking attention blocks placed between stages. These blocks subsample before Q, mapping (C,H,W) to (C',H/2,W/2), omit residual connections at downsampling, and set the number of heads to N=C/D to preserve information. This design shifts more compute to small activation maps and yields superior throughput at similar accuracy—for example, LeViT-256 matches EfficientNet-B3 at 81.6% ImageNet top-1 while being ~7× faster on an Intel CPU (42.5 vs 5.9 images/s).
paper_title: LeViT: A Vision Transformer in ConvNet's Clothing for Faster Inference

problem: Absolute positional embeddings in ViTs are injected only once and force the model to carry positional information through layers, while attention computation is costly due to large key dimensions.
method: Replace absolute position embeddings with a learned, per-head, translation-invariant attention bias added directly to each attention map, encoding relative offsets. With this bias in place, reduce the key dimensionality (D∈{16,32}) while keeping larger value projections (2D, and 4D in downsampling blocks) to cut QK^T cost without losing capacity. Ablations show the bias improves accuracy over positional embeddings (−1.5 points when removed), validating the efficiency–accuracy gains of this attention redesign.
paper_title: LeViT: A Vision Transformer in ConvNet's Clothing for Faster Inference

problem: The standard ViT patch projection (a single 16×16 conv) is compute-inefficient and weak for low-capacity regimes, limiting early-stage feature compression and speed.
method: Use a lightweight convolutional stem for patch embedding: four 3×3 stride-2 convolutions with channels 3→32→64→128→256 to reduce resolution and produce a (256,14,14) tensor at only 184 MFLOPs. Remove the class token and use global average pooling for classification, aligning with BCHW activations. Ablations confirm the stem’s importance: replacing it with a single 16×16 conv cuts FLOPs slightly but significantly degrades accuracy.
paper_title: LeViT: A Vision Transformer in ConvNet's Clothing for Faster Inference

problem: Multi-spectral ReID must fuse RGB/NIR/TIR features despite large cross-spectral distribution gaps; most Transformer-based ReID pipelines rely mainly on class-token global features and underuse local patch information, yielding weak alignment and fusion.
method: Use three independent ViT streams to preserve spectrum-specific tokens, then apply a cyclic Token Permutation Module (TPM) that runs three consecutive permutations where each spectrum’s class token performs multi-head cross-attention over another spectrum’s patch tokens (e.g., RGB cls → NIR patches → TIR patches, then back to own patches), with LayerNorm-stabilized queries/keys. The permuted class tokens are concatenated as the fused descriptor. This design lets global class tokens ingest local details from other spectra and implicitly aligns spatial features across spectra, outperforming simple concatenation/fusion (e.g., ablations show notable gains).
paper_title: TOP-ReID: Multi-spectral Object Re-Identification with Token Permutation

problem: Cross-spectral distribution gaps and missing-spectral conditions (at training or inference) degrade multi-spectral ReID performance and complicate unified feature learning.
method: Introduce a Complementary Reconstruction Module (CRM) that performs dense token-level reconstruction across spectra via Transformer-based reconstruction blocks. For each stream, CRM reconstructs the other spectra’s tokens and applies MSE constraints against real tokens to reduce distribution gaps. At inference, when any spectrum is missing, CRM dynamically reconstructs its tokens from the available streams and feeds them into TPM so the cyclic permutation proceeds normally. Token-level reconstruction proves superior to pixel-level methods and enables robust performance under missing-spectral scenarios.
paper_title: TOP-ReID: Multi-spectral Object Re-Identification with Token Permutation

problem: Modern ViT backbones for detection/segmentation often import CNN-style multistage designs (spatial downsampling, multi-scale features, doubled channels), but their actual benefits for transformers on dense prediction are unclear, and they add complexity.
method: Propose Universal Vision Transformer (UViT), a vanilla ViT backbone that keeps constant token resolution and hidden size through all encoder blocks and outputs a single-scale feature map to standard heads (no spatial downsampling, no multi-scale features from the backbone, no channel-doubling). Through controlled COCO ablations under matched params/FLOPs, they show these CNN-like add-ons are unnecessary and often hurt, with the simple single-scale ViT performing best. UViT achieves competitive accuracy with far fewer parameters than hierarchical ViTs (e.g., UViT-B: 52.3 AP with 74.4M params vs Swin-B: 51.9 AP with 145M; same Cascade Mask R-CNN).
paper_title: A Simple Single-Scale Vision Transformer for Object Detection and Instance Segmentation

problem: Choosing how to scale ViTs (depth, width, input size) for dense prediction is underexplored and differs from CNN scaling laws, impacting the accuracy–efficiency trade-off.
method: Systematically study compound scaling of UViT across input sizes (640–1024), depths (12–40 blocks), and widths under fixed windowed attention and ImageNet-1k pretraining. Findings: larger input sizes create more headroom, with 896×896 consistently offering a better trade-off than smaller sizes and slightly better than 1024×1024 in comparable FLOPs; a moderate depth of 18 blocks yields better performance-efficiency than shallower (12) or deeper (24/32/40) models. They instantiate UViT-T/S/B at 18 layers with increasing widths, forming a compact, strong backbone series for COCO fine-tuning.
paper_title: A Simple Single-Scale Vision Transformer for Object Detection and Instance Segmentation

problem: Global self-attention on high-resolution inputs is computationally expensive and may be unnecessary in early layers if those layers mostly attend locally.
method: Measure relative attention receptive fields of a pretrained ViT on COCO and observe early layers act locally while deeper layers become global. Based on this, use attention windows to limit attention range: a constant local window (e.g., 1/2 scale) already preserves accuracy with large compute savings; further, a progressive schedule that uses small windows in early blocks and grows to global windows only in the last blocks (UViT+) reduces FLOPs while maintaining or improving AP. For example, UViT-B+: 52.5 AP at 1160 GFLOPs vs UViT-B: 52.3 AP at 1298.7 GFLOPs; early over-shrinking (e.g., 1/16, 1/8) hurts, and global windows in deep layers are vital.
paper_title: A Simple Single-Scale Vision Transformer for Object Detection and Instance Segmentation

problem: Binarizing self-attention in Vision Transformers causes large errors because softmax attention scores are positive, highly sparse, and long-tailed; softmax-agnostic binarization (e.g., pre-softmax Bool/learned fixed thresholds) mismatches these dynamics and degrades accuracy.
method: Introduce Softmax-aware Binarization (SAB): formulate per-row attention binarization as a sparse-coding/coordinate-descent problem to derive an optimal threshold T* and basis v that minimize quantization error, then approximate it efficiently at inference by predicting T ≈ β·max(a_s) via linear regression and discarding v to keep compute unchanged. Use STE to backpropagate gradients w.r.t. softmax outputs (not pre-softmax), making training softmax-aware. This reduces attention quantization error from 0.683 (BiBERT) to ~2.7e-05 and improves TinyImageNet accuracy when binarizing attention (e.g., NesT-T: 68.51%→70.73%).
paper_title: BiViT: Extremely Compressed Binary Vision Transformers

problem: Directly binarizing all modules in ViTs destroys crucial pretrained information—especially in MLPs with many 1×1 layers—leading to severe performance drops and unstable optimization.
method: Propose Cross-layer Binarization (CLB), a two-stage scheme that decouples modules to preserve pretrained information: first binarize all self-attention blocks (with SAB) while keeping MLPs full-precision; then binarize MLPs. This mitigates mutual interference between modules, accelerates convergence, and yields higher accuracy than one-step or traditional two-step (activations-then-weights) training (TinyImageNet/NesT-T with FP MLP activations: 69.83% vs 58.20% one-step and 65.64% traditional two-step).
paper_title: BiViT: Extremely Compressed Binary Vision Transformers

problem: Binary ViTs have limited representational capacity; fixed per-channel scaling factors in weight binarization underutilize channel importance and hinder recovery of pretrained knowledge.
method: Introduce Parameterized Weight Scales (PWS): replace conventional computed scales with learnable per-channel scale parameters trained end-to-end, enhancing representation without changing architecture or complexity. Applied to both attention and MLP modules, PWS yields consistent gains (e.g., TinyImageNet/NesT-T: +4.2% Top-1 when added to binarized attention; +1.3% when added to binarized MLPs).
paper_title: BiViT: Extremely Compressed Binary Vision Transformers

problem: Self-supervised visual representation methods are reported under inconsistent conditions, making it unclear which methods transfer best across diverse downstream tasks and whether they truly surpass supervised pre-training.
method: Conduct a large-scale, controlled benchmark of 13 state-of-the-art SSL models, all pre-trained on ImageNet and using the same ResNet-50 backbone, and evaluate transfer on 40 downstream tasks spanning many-shot and few-shot recognition, object detection (Faster R-CNN FPN on VOC), and dense prediction (NYUv2 surface normals with PSPNet; ADE20K segmentation with UPerNet), using both frozen linear heads and full finetuning. This standardized setup reveals that top SSL methods generally outperform supervised pre-training on most tasks, with supervised models remaining strongest in few-shot transfer on ImageNet-like data.
paper_title: How Well Do Self-Supervised Models Transfer?

problem: Practitioners rely on ImageNet accuracy to select pre-trained models, but it is unclear how predictive this is for transfer across varied tasks and domain shifts.
method: Quantify transferability by computing Pearson and Spearman correlations between logit-transformed ImageNet top-1 accuracy and downstream metrics across all tasks. Findings show strong correlation for many-shot recognition; weaker correlation for few-shot under domain shift (absent on ISIC); and reduced correlation for detection and dense prediction (AP50 correlates best; frozen backbones correlate more than finetuned). No single SSL method dominates across tasks—e.g., SimCLR-v2 excels with frozen detection backbones, BYOL with finetuning, and PCL-v1 leads on ADE20K segmentation—providing task-specific guidance rather than a one-size-fits-all choice.
paper_title: How Well Do Self-Supervised Models Transfer?

problem: What information SSL features retain (e.g., color), how they attend spatially, and how well they calibrate predictions versus supervised features is poorly understood, yet critical for reliable deployment.
method: Analyze downstream calibration using Expected Calibration Error with/without temperature scaling; probe feature content via deep image prior inversion and quantify perceptual similarity (LPIPS) and color-channel MSE; assess attention using occlusion-based saliency to measure attentive diffusion. Results indicate many SSL models are better calibrated than supervised in linear transfer (supervised best after finetuning unless scaled), SSL features reconstruct with poorer color fidelity (suggesting color-invariant representations due to augmentations), and SSL models exhibit more diffuse attention than supervised models, which may relate to their improved transfer and calibration.
paper_title: How Well Do Self-Supervised Models Transfer?

problem: Archaeological lithic use-wear identification from microscopic images lacks a public, large-scale dataset. Ambiguous imaging magnifications and sensing modalities (texture vs. 3D surface heightmaps), along with complex wear formation, hinder robust model development and reproducible evaluation.
method: The authors construct LUWA, the first open-source and largest LUWA dataset with 23,130 microscopic images, captured using an optical 3D profilometer at 20× and 50× magnifications and two complementary modalities (grayscale texture and 3D heightmap). Data span both machine-controlled and human wear processes to isolate material effects while reflecting real-world complexity, and include expert inputs (wear degrees, attention maps, and prompts). This design explicitly supports studying multi-scale wear patterns and modality effects while enabling reproducible benchmarking.
paper_title: LUWA Dataset: Learning Lithic Use-Wear Analysis on Microscopic Images

problem: It is unclear how well state-of-the-art pre-trained vision models generalize to rarely seen microscopic wear-trace images, and which factors (image granularity, magnification, modality, training strategy) most affect performance relative to human experts.
method: A systematic benchmark evaluates ResNets, ConvNeXts, ViT-H, and DINOv2 under varied training strategies (from-scratch, full fine-tuning, linear probing), image granularities (partitioning each 865×865 wear image into 6 or 24 patches with majority voting), magnifications (20×, 50×, or mixed), and modalities (texture vs. heightmap). Results show linear probing is most stable/optimal; higher magnification improves accuracy; mixing magnifications does not confuse models and helps; heightmaps often yield the best results while larger models tend to favor texture; and DINOv2 delivers the most stable performance. Models surpass human experts (≈49.5%); the best configuration achieves 67.05% top-1 (ResNet152, linear probe, heightmap, 24 partitions, mixed 20×+50×).
paper_title: LUWA Dataset: Learning Lithic Use-Wear Analysis on Microscopic Images

problem: Real deployments face scarce labeled microscopic images and newly discovered categories, necessitating effective few-shot classification and an assessment of whether large vision-language models can aid expert-like reasoning.
method: The paper evaluates a simple pretrain + ProtoNet few-shot pipeline across multiple encoders (ResNet, ConvNeXt, ViT/DeiT, CLIP, DINO) over 600 tasks in 9-way-5/20-shot settings, analyzing magnification and modality impacts; and conducts GPT-4V case studies using archaeologist-crafted prompts for reasoning and few-shot classification. DINO-based encoders excel overall, with 50× texture consistently outperforming other settings in scarce-data regimes. GPT-4V can follow expert reasoning cues but performs poorly on few-shot classification, highlighting a gap between VLM reasoning and domain-specific visual discrimination.
paper_title: LUWA Dataset: Learning Lithic Use-Wear Analysis on Microscopic Images

problem: Camera-only moving object detection (MOD) degrades under low illumination because image-based optical flow becomes unreliable; autonomous driving needs a robust, real-time MOD solution.
method: FuseMODNet is a three-stream mid-fusion CNN that fuses RGB appearance, camera optical flow (rgbFlow from FlowNet), and LiDAR-derived dense optical flow (lidarFlow hallucinated from sparse LiDAR [35]) via separate encoders and feature-level concatenation, followed by an FCN8s decoder. The encoder uses an efficient ShuffleNet backbone (ShuffleSeg) for real-time operation. This fusion yields complementary motion cues that remain reliable in low light, improving moving-class IoU by 10.1% relatively on Dark-KITTI and 4.25% on KITTI over baselines, while running at 18 fps on 256×1224 images.
paper_title: FuseMODNet: Real-Time Camera and LiDAR based Moving Object Detection for robust low-light Autonomous Driving

problem: Public datasets for pixel-level moving/static segmentation are small and lack low-light imagery, limiting training and evaluation of MOD—especially for LiDAR–camera fusion.
method: The authors automatically extend KittiMoSeg to 12,919 frames by projecting 3D LiDAR boxes to image space, computing object velocities from tracklets, estimating ego-motion from GPS, classifying objects as moving/static by velocity differencing, and refining masks with Mask R-CNN. To evaluate low-light robustness, they create Dark-KITTI by training UNIT on 2,000 KITTI day images and 2,000 BDD100K night images, generating 12,919 night counterparts aligned with the annotations.
paper_title: FuseMODNet: Real-Time Camera and LiDAR based Moving Object Detection for robust low-light Autonomous Driving

problem: It is unclear which fusion strategy (data-level vs feature-level vs hybrid) and motion representation (explicit optical flow vs implicit temporal learning) best balances accuracy and real-time constraints for MOD.
method: They systematically compare early (data-level) fusion, mid (feature-level) fusion, and hybrid schemes across RGB, rgbFlow, lidarFlow, and LiDAR depth, and contrast explicit flow inputs with implicit motion learning from pairs of frames. Results show early fusion underperforms, while mid/hybrid fusion improves accuracy; explicit optical flow outperforms implicit motion learning. The best accuracy comes from three-stream mid-fusion RGB+rgbFlow+lidarFlow (e.g., Dark-KITTI moving IoU 43.5 vs 39.5 with RGB+rgbFlow; KITTI 51.46 vs 49.36), with documented real-time trade-offs (baseline 40 fps; two-stream 25 fps; three-stream 18 fps).
paper_title: FuseMODNet: Real-Time Camera and LiDAR based Moving Object Detection for robust low-light Autonomous Driving

problem: Ambiguous medical image segmentation requires predictions that are both accurate and diverse, but existing one-stage stochastic methods and vanilla truncated diffusion models entangle these objectives, so improving diversity typically hurts accuracy.
method: Redefine inference with Data-Hierarchical Inference: marginalize stochasticity before the truncation point and supervise an explicit intermediate distribution at T_trunc (distribution-level objective for accuracy), then generate diverse samples after truncation at the sample level (diversity objective) via a diffusion/flow process. This two-stage, hierarchy-specific objective disentangles accuracy and diversity and enables simultaneous improvement with truncated, efficient inference.
paper_title: Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation

problem: In truncated diffusion models, the distribution at the truncation point is often approximated via adversarial sampling, which leads to low fidelity, inconsistent predictions, and missed low-frequency (rare but plausible) modes.
method: Introduce Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR): explicitly model the truncation distribution on the segmentation logit map at T_trunc as a multivariate Gaussian with learnable mean and covariance (low-rank + diagonal) predicted from image features (U-Net + 1×1 heads). Train this explicit prior by maximizing likelihood (Monte Carlo NLL) against ground truths, then freeze it for inference. This explicit parameterization preserves rare modes, improves consistency, and yields a reliable, high-fidelity truncation distribution.
paper_title: Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation

problem: After truncation, conventional diffusion lacks semantic guidance, yielding diverse but implausible or inaccurate masks; moreover, Gaussian constraints can disturb fine-grained segmentation.
method: Propose Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM): use Flow Matching with an Optimal Transport schedule to transform from the GTR prior to target masks, and enforce semantic consistency at each timestep. Concretely, compute x_t = t·X1 + (1−t)·X_Ttrunc, predict the vector field g_θ(x_t), obtain x1_t = x_t + g_θ(x_t)·(1−t), and add a Dice-based loss against all available annotations per timestep to the FM loss. This semantic-aware flow yields diverse yet plausible segmentations and avoids Gaussian constraints intrinsic to DDPM-style sampling.
paper_title: Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation

problem: With vision transformers that use Global Average Pooling (GAP), combining GAP with layer-wise position embeddings (LaPE) unexpectedly degrades performance, suggesting PE’s influence inside blocks is not preserved after the final block.
method: MPVG preserves PE’s counterbalancing effect by additionally injecting a PE term into the Last LayerNorm (the LN outside the blocks, before the head) while keeping layer-wise delivery with independent LNs for tokens and PE. This maintains the anti-directional (counterbalancing) relationship between token embeddings and PE up to classification. Using the initial PE (pos0) at the Last LN performs best among tested choices. MPVG yields consistent gains across DeiT, Swin, CeiT, and T2T-ViT on ImageNet-1K (e.g., +1.37% top-1 on DeiT-Ti), and also improves COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.
paper_title: Maximizing the Position Embedding for Vision Transformers with Global Average Pooling

problem: Standard LaPE design choices are suboptimal under GAP, limiting the expressiveness and downstream effectiveness of PE.
method: PVG is an improved layer-wise structure tailored for GAP that (i) removes the class token, (ii) adds PE to tokens before entering layer 0 (x+PE) while still using independent LNs per block, and (iii) excludes layer 0 from layer-wise PE delivery and uses hierarchical propagation of PE across subsequent layers. Ablations show these choices boost accuracy (e.g., x+PE adds ~0.23% and excluding layer 0 helps), and PVG outperforms LaPE across backbones, though MPVG further surpasses PVG.
paper_title: Maximizing the Position Embedding for Vision Transformers with Global Average Pooling

problem: The functional role of PE across depth in layer-wise ViTs with GAP is unclear, hindering principled architectural choices.
method: The paper analyzes token–PE correlations across layers and finds they become increasingly anti-correlated, indicating PE counterbalances high-value token dimensions in deeper layers. It further shows that, in PVG, the Last LN’s beta must absorb this role (high beta variance), whereas in MPVG the beta variance drops markedly, implying PE now performs the counterbalancing at the Last LN. An additional control shows adding PE to the Last LN without a layer-wise structure hurts performance (72.14% vs 72.40% with GAP-only), confirming that the Last-LN PE is only beneficial when the counterbalancing directionality has been built up layer-wise.
paper_title: Maximizing the Position Embedding for Vision Transformers with Global Average Pooling

problem: Co-salient object detection (CoSOD) can expose sensitive content by finding common salient regions across an image group. Attacking CoSOD is hard because groups are dynamic and pipelines vary widely, making white-box additive-noise attacks brittle with low success and poor transferability.
method: Proposes Jadena, a full black-box attack that suppresses saliency by minimizing a high-level feature contrast objective computed from intermediate layers of a generic classifier (e.g., ResNet50), without accessing any CoSOD model or saliency maps. The loss reduces channel-wise feature variance within an image (single saliency) and across a group by splicing features over references constructed either from other group images or simple augmentations (flip/rotate) of the target, weakening co-salient cues. The parameters are optimized via sign-gradient (momentum iterative FGSM), yielding high transferability and practicality even when only the single image is available.
paper_title: Can You Spot the Chameleon? Adversarially Camouflaging Images from Co-Salient Object Detection

problem: Effective yet imperceptible attacks require altering local contrast while keeping exposure natural and smooth; naive pixel-wise exposure changes or noise either look unnatural or are ineffective.
method: Introduces a joint perturbation that combines bounded additive noise with a spatially varying exposure field modeled as a low-parameter multivariate polynomial in the log-intensity domain, augmented by a spatial tuning (offset/optical-flow) map to enable local adjustments. A naturalness objective penalizes deviation from the original image and enforces smoothness via total variation on the exposure field, balancing imperceptibility and attack strength. All components are jointly optimized with momentum sign gradients; ablations show both exposure tuning and noise are necessary for strong attacks.
paper_title: Can You Spot the Chameleon? Adversarially Camouflaging Images from Co-Salient Object Detection

problem: Progress in PET-CT lung tumor segmentation is hampered by the lack of a large-scale, publicly available dataset with reliable pixel-level annotations, limiting model robustness and fair benchmarking.
method: Release the PCLT20K dataset: 21,930 2D PET-CT slice pairs from 605 patients, annotated via a rigorous three-stage clinician pipeline to ensure quality and consistency. Data are split 8:2 at the patient level and preprocessed with CT HU clipping to [-1200, -200], PET conversion to SUV and scaling to [0, 255], and resizing to 512×512 to align coordinate spaces. The dataset underpins a comprehensive benchmark across diverse segmentation baselines.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Interactive Perception Network with Mamba for Lung Tumor Segmentation in PET-CT Images

problem: Mamba-based multi-modal segmentation networks tend to overlook cross-modal channel dependencies, making it difficult to learn shared representations and suppress modality-specific noise in PET-CT fusion.
method: Introduce the Channel-wise Rectification Module (CRM). At each encoder stage, CRM concatenates PET and CT features along channels, treats spatially flattened pixels as channel sequences, and applies a 1D selective state space (Mamba) block to model channel correlations across modalities. A linear + sigmoid projection produces per-channel weights, split per modality, which scale the original PET/CT features to emphasize correlated channels and filter modality-specific noise before fusion.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Interactive Perception Network with Mamba for Lung Tumor Segmentation in PET-CT Images

problem: Standard VMamba’s 2D selective scan struggles to capture complementary regional information between PET (lesion localization, high contrast) and CT (structural detail, high resolution), limiting effective cross-modal fusion for accurate tumor delineation.
method: Propose the Dynamic Cross-Modality Interaction Module (DCIM). A convolutional stem yields PET regional tokens and partitions CT features into regional patches with fine-grained local tokens. A region Mamba block processes PET tokens to encode global positional cues, while a local Mamba block models dependencies among CT local tokens. PET regional tokens are then added to each CT local token within the same region to guide CT with PET position cues, enabling dynamic, region-to-local fusion. Integrated at multiple stages within a fully SSM-based encoder-decoder (with CVSS blocks for decoding), DCIM delivers state-of-the-art performance on PCLT20K and the public STS dataset.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Interactive Perception Network with Mamba for Lung Tumor Segmentation in PET-CT Images

problem: State-of-the-art 3D object detectors on point clouds rely on hand-crafted 3D operators, tuned radii, and multi-stage heuristics (e.g., Hough voting and NMS), which complicate end-to-end learning and portability.
method: 3DETR is an end-to-end Transformer that operates directly on point clouds with minimal 3D-specific modifications. A standard Transformer encoder processes downsampled points (no encoder positional embeddings), and a parallel Transformer decoder predicts boxes using non-parametric queries sampled via Farthest Point Sampling whose XYZ are embedded with Fourier positional encodings; boxes are predicted as offsets from query locations along with size, orientation, and class. Training uses a DETR-style set prediction objective with Hungarian matching and a geometric cost (GIoU + center distance) plus semantic terms, which discourages duplicates and removes the need for NMS. Ablations show non-parametric queries and Fourier embeddings are critical (large AP gains over parametric queries), and the model outperforms a strong VoteNet baseline on ScanNetV2 by 9.5 AP50 while being simple to implement.
paper_title: An End-to-End Transformer Model for 3D Object Detection

problem: Pure Transformers may miss beneficial 3D inductive biases such as locality; a flexible way to inject such priors without complex 3D operators is needed.
method: 3DETR-m augments the 3DETR encoder with masked self-attention to bias interactions toward local neighborhoods, using radius-based binary masks per layer and a light additional downsampling step, while keeping the same decoder and set loss. This introduces a weak PointNet++-like locality prior without bespoke 3D convolutions or multi-stage feature propagation. The variant improves detection (e.g., 65.0 AP25/47.0 AP50 on ScanNetV2 vs 62.7/37.5 for vanilla 3DETR) and surpasses the improved VoteNet baseline on ScanNetV2, demonstrating that 3D priors can be cleanly integrated within the Transformer framework.
paper_title: An End-to-End Transformer Model for 3D Object Detection

problem: Inference-time compute budgets vary, but typical 3D detectors require retraining to adjust decoder depth or number of box proposals.
method: Because 3DETR predicts boxes at every decoder layer with shared heads and uses non-parametric queries, it supports test-time adaptation without retraining: using fewer decoder layers or changing the number of queries trades accuracy for speed. Experiments show the same trained model with reduced depth performs as well or better than separately trained shallower models, and varying the query count at test time performs comparably to models trained for those settings, with performance saturating once queries sufficiently cover the scene.
paper_title: An End-to-End Transformer Model for 3D Object Detection

problem: Panoramic renal pathology segmentation across regions, functional units, and cells is difficult because current methods ignore rich anatomical interrelations (subset/superset, mutual exclusion) between structures and struggle to encode these relationships in learning.
method: Introduce a universal proposition matrix that translates kidney anatomical knowledge into computational constraints using Aristotle’s Universal Affirmation (subset) and Universal Negation (mutual exclusion). The matrix captures pairwise class relationships (classification and spatial) and is unique and transmissible across the hierarchy, enabling inference of indirect relations. It is designed to be extendable when new classes are added, providing a simple, scalable mechanism to inject anatomy into training.
paper_title: PrPSeg: Universal Proposition Learning for Panoramic Renal Pathology Segmentation

problem: Existing multi-head or class-specific architectures are brittle for partially labeled datasets, cannot easily handle overlapping structures, and require architectural changes when new classes or scales are introduced.
method: Propose a token-based dynamic head single network with a Residual U-Net encoder augmented by class-aware tokens (one per class) and scale-aware tokens (for 5×, 10×, 20×, 40×) injected into encoder blocks. A fusion controller (2D conv) combines GAP features with the last intervals of class and scale tokens to generate parameters for a lightweight 162-parameter dynamic binary head that targets a specified class and scale.
