Extensibility is achieved by extending token dimensions (not the backbone), enabling stable reuse of the same architecture for new classes and effective learning on partially labeled data with spatially overlapping structures.
paper_title: PrPSeg: Universal Proposition Learning for Panoramic Renal Pathology Segmentation

problem: With partially labeled images, it is hard to enforce spatial correlations between labeled and unlabeled classes during training, limiting comprehensive multi-class segmentation.
method: Define an anatomy loss that operationalizes the universal proposition matrix for online semi-supervised learning: for a labeled class i, predictions for another class j on the same image are constrained so that (i) if i ⊃ j, j’s prediction stays within i; (ii) if i ⊂ j, j’s prediction covers i; and (iii) if i and j are exclusive, their overlap is minimized. This anatomy loss is combined with supervised BCE and Dice losses (weighted by λupl) to inject inter-object relational constraints and improve comprehensive segmentation.
paper_title: PrPSeg: Universal Proposition Learning for Panoramic Renal Pathology Segmentation

problem: One-stage object detectors suffer from severe inter-class imbalance (many background vs. few foreground anchors) and intra-class imbalance (only a few hard negatives), while focal loss reweights per-anchor heuristically and ignores per-image structure.
method: Convert per-anchor classification into an image-dependent ranking task and introduce Distributional Ranking (DR) loss that ranks, with a margin, the expectations of per-image positive and negative score distributions instead of all O(n^2) pairs. Derive these distributions by solving a KL-regularized optimization that ‘pushes’ scores toward the decision boundary while preserving diversity, yielding closed-form, image-dependent soft weights that emphasize harder examples (q− ∝ exp(p/λ−), q+ ∝ exp(−p/λ+)). Optimize a smooth logistic surrogate with margin γ=0.5, which reduces pair complexity to a single pair (ĤP+, ĤP−) per image and enables direct recovery of standard classification.
paper_title: DR Loss: Improving Object Detection by Distributional Ranking

problem: Training the proposed non-convex DR loss efficiently and stably across different mini-batch sizes requires guidance on optimizer behavior and calibration back to classification.
method: Provide an SGD convergence result for DR loss under µ-smoothness with convergence rate O(1/√(mT)) and derive a batch-size-aware learning-rate scaling η ∝ √(m/T) with η ≤ 1/µ. Further, show that setting the ranking margin to γ=0.5 recovers the standard 0.5 classification threshold, enabling a plug-and-play replacement of focal loss in RetinaNet without architectural changes.
paper_title: DR Loss: Improving Object Detection by Distributional Ranking

problem: Multi-task RL often wastes samples or suffers negative transfer when directly reusing models/policies across tasks, especially when state/action spaces vary and inter-task mappings are unknown.
method: Introduce Transition Templates (TTs) that abstract a state-action’s dynamics by sorting its transition probability vector in descending order and pairing it with the reward, enabling grouping of state-action pairs with similar transition patterns across tasks of possibly different sizes. The Online Template Learning (O-TempLe) algorithm estimates rough dynamics after a small visit threshold ms, generates a TT (GEN-TT), assigns it to a TT group using a distance threshold τ, and then bi-directionally shares experience via TT-UPDATE and AUGMENT to ‘permute back’ and refine each pair’s estimates. It plugs into a model-based base learner (RMax) for policy updates when pairs become m-known, and compares TTs across different-sized tasks by padding zeros, yielding sample-efficient transfer without prior inter-task mappings and avoiding negative transfer.
paper_title: TempLe: Learning Template of Transitions for Sample Efficient Multi-task RL

problem: When tasks are drawn from a small, known set of MDPs (finite-model setting), existing methods primarily transfer at the model level and can be inefficient or fail to share finer-grained similarities.
method: Extend O-TempLe to Finite-Model Template Learning (FM-TempLe): first learn and store each model’s TT structure across early tasks, cluster tasks into C model groups by TT structures, then for new tasks identify the true model by ruling out groups whose TT structures conflict with observed TTs. Once the model is identified, infer the TTs for all state-action pairs immediately and augment their estimates without waiting for ms visits per pair, reducing dependence on S and avoiding extra model-elimination steps. Theoretical analysis shows improved sample complexity over FMRL by exploiting TT-level similarities rather than model-only similarities.
paper_title: TempLe: Learning Template of Transitions for Sample Efficient Multi-task RL

problem: There is a lack of PAC-MDP guarantees for multi-task RL under unknown equivalence structures, with a need to ensure provable sample efficiency and no negative transfer.
method: Define the TT gap τ and ranking gap ν to characterize separability and ordering robustness of TTs, and design thresholds so that ms = Õ(1/ω^2) samples per pair suffice for correct TT identification (ω = max{min(τ, ν), O(ε/(1−γ)√(SVmax))}). Prove PAC-MDP sample complexity bounds: for O-TempLe, a bound that (when ε is small) scales linearly with the number of states S and number of templates G and improves over single-task RMax; for FM-TempLe, a tighter bound under finite-model assumptions that outperforms FMRL in most regimes. In worst cases (e.g., no useful TT structure), TempLe matches single-task RMax, ensuring no negative transfer.
paper_title: TempLe: Learning Template of Transitions for Sample Efficient Multi-task RL

problem: Apprenticeship Learning typically assumes batch access and/or known dynamics, and many methods require solving an MDP at each iteration; in online settings with unknown transitions the agent must perform comparably to the expert while learning.
method: Proposes Online Apprenticeship Learning (OAL), a min-max scheme that runs two no-regret Mirror Descent updates per round: (1) a policy MD step using KL-Bregman with an optimistic Q-function (via UCB bonuses) to handle unknown dynamics and induce exploration; and (2) a cost MD step using the gradient ∇cL(π,c)=dE−dπ with Euclidean projection onto bounded costs [0,1]. This simultaneous, stable update avoids best-response MDP solves each iteration and yields a high-probability O(√K) AL regret bound plus an additional linear term in K reflecting finite expert data, with N expert trajectories reducing this term (e.g., dominant term O(√K) when N∝K).
paper_title: Online Apprenticeship Learning

problem: Scaling AL to high-dimensional continuous control without solving an MDP each step or relying on a GAN discriminator, while ensuring stable learning and bounded costs.
method: Implements a deep OAL variant: policy optimization via MDPO (off-policy) or TRPO (on-policy) to approximate the MD update; costs learned either as linear weights (closed-form MD step) or as a neural network constrained to be bounded and Lipschitz using output penalties, gradient penalty (à la WGAN-GP), and clipping. On MuJoCo tasks, both linear and neural OAL directly optimize the AL objective and match or outperform GAIL, with off-policy MDPO providing superior data efficiency versus on-policy TRPO; enforcing Lipschitz regularization improves stability of the learned costs.
paper_title: Online Apprenticeship Learning

problem: In RLHF for LLMs, reward models trained from human preferences are often inaccurate—especially in the mid-reward region—leading PPO to learn from noisy signals and over-optimize spurious reward.
method: Propose Policy Filtration for PPO (PF-PPO): for each prompt, sample N responses from the current policy, rank them with the reward model, and sample a training response according to a rank-based weight vector that emphasizes more reliable regions (e.g., Best-Random, Best-Worst) while de-emphasizing mid-range rewards. Train PPO to optimize a filtered policy µθ but, in practice, use samples collected from the unfiltered policy πθ as if from µθ, changing only the sampling stage. This increases the reward–score reliability (higher R^2) and improves downstream performance, achieving SOTA among 7B models on HumanEval/MBPP and large gains on the challenging LeetCode Contest.
paper_title: Policy Filtration for RLHF to Mitigate Noise in Reward Models

problem: Selecting an effective filtration strategy is hard to tune via full PPO runs; a lightweight predictor is needed to decide which ranks/weights yield reliable rewards before expensive training.
method: Use the coefficient of determination (R^2) between reward-model outputs and actual correctness on samples generated by candidate filtered policies (using the SFT or intermediate policies) as a proxy for reward reliability. Compute R^2 by grouping similar-reward samples and fitting a linear predictor; choose strategies that maximize R^2. Empirically, R^2 correlates well with final pass@1 and favors BR/BW over BoN across training stages, guiding strategy selection prior to PPO.
paper_title: Policy Filtration for RLHF to Mitigate Noise in Reward Models

problem: Beyond rank-only selection, practitioners may need flexible filtration mechanisms that still avoid unreliable mid-reward samples while being robust across tasks and reward models.
method: Introduce alternative filtering schemes within PF-PPO: threshold-based selection (PPO-top, PPO-top-random, PPO-top-bottom) and reward reweighting (PPO-pow-k with sample weights proportional to |Rϕ|^k). Experiments show most filtering variants outperform vanilla PPO/PPO-M, with rank-based PF-PPO (BR/BW) generally strongest; R^2 remains a useful selector across these variants.
paper_title: Policy Filtration for RLHF to Mitigate Noise in Reward Models

problem: Estimating the full set of rewards that make an expert policy optimal (the feasible reward set) lacks a principled accuracy metric and stability guarantees with respect to estimation errors in the environment and expert policy.
method: Introduce a Hausdorff-distance–based evaluation H_d between feasible reward sets induced by the L∞ metric on rewards (d_G), and define a premetric ρ_G on IRL problems that combines L1 distance between transition models and differences in the support of the expert policy. Prove a Lipschitz continuity result (Theorem 3.2) showing that, with unrestricted bounded rewards, small changes in the IRL problem (per ρ_G) imply small Hausdorff changes in feasible reward sets; and show via counterexamples that common restrictions (state-only, time-homogeneous, β-margin rewards) break this Lipschitz property.
paper_title: Towards Theoretical Understanding of Inverse Reinforcement Learning

problem: The minimax sample complexity of estimating the feasible reward set in finite-horizon IRL with a generative model has been unknown.
method: Formulate a PAC framework for feasible-set estimation under the Hausdorff metric (Definition 4.1) and derive the first minimax lower bounds (Theorem 5.1). In the time-inhomogeneous case the bound scales as Ω(H^3 S A / ε^2 · (log(1/δ) + S)), and in the time-homogeneous case as Ω(H^2 S A / ε^2 · (log(1/δ) + S)). The proof constructs hard instances exhibiting two regimes (small-δ and large-δ), leveraging Bretagnolle–Huber and Fano inequalities with carefully designed MDP families (Figure 2).
paper_title: Towards Theoretical Understanding of Inverse Reinforcement Learning

problem: It is unclear whether simple exploration strategies can achieve the optimal (or near-optimal) sample complexity for feasible reward set estimation under the proposed PAC criterion.
method: Analyze a uniform sampling strategy (US-IRL; Algorithm 1) that queries each (state, action, stage) uniformly and stops when per-triple KL-based confidence bounds imply an ε-accurate Hausdorff estimate. Using stronger KL concentration for categorical models (Jonsson et al., 2020) to control L1 transition errors, prove US-IRL is (ε, δ)-PAC (Theorem 6.1) with sample complexity O(H^3 S A / ε^2 · (log(1/δ) + S)) for time-inhomogeneous, and O(H^2 S A / ε^2 · (log(1/δ) + S)) for time-homogeneous transitions—matching the lower bounds up to logarithmic factors.
paper_title: Towards Theoretical Understanding of Inverse Reinforcement Learning

problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
method: IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.
paper_title: Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks

problem: Position-only affordances guide exploration spatially but fail to promote safe, compliant behavior in contact tasks, and direct stiffness penalties require fragile reward tuning across environments.
method: The framework introduces affordance coupling by adding a stiffness affordance that rewards compliance—encouraging low stiffness unless higher stiffness is necessary—combined with position affordances. Atomic and gripper primitives have affordance 1 due to general utility. This coupling improves exploration efficiency, reduces interaction forces, and mitigates per-task reward weight tuning.
paper_title: Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks

problem: Once a primitive is triggered in HRL, stiffness is typically fixed during execution, preventing adaptation to varying contact conditions and potentially causing excessive forces or task failure.
method: An adaptive stiffness controller updates stiffness online during primitive execution using closed-loop tracking error and energy consumption, scaled by β and γ, while ensuring critical damping with D(t) = 2√K(t) and smoothing via trajectory interpolation. Controller gains β and γ are obtained from kinesthetic demonstrations by fitting to human-like stiffness profiles via MSE minimization. This dynamic adaptation reduces interaction forces and improves performance in contact-intensive tasks (e.g., substantially higher wipe success rates).
paper_title: Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks

problem: Rule-based automated experts in simulators (e.g., CARLA Autopilot) are suboptimal coaches for end-to-end driving and model-free RL for urban driving often suffers from poor sample efficiency and unstable training.
method: Roach: a PPO-based RL expert that maps bird’s-eye-view (BEV) semantics plus ego measurements to continuous low-level actions parameterized by Beta distributions (bounded support) instead of Gaussians, improving optimization and avoiding squashing/clipping. It introduces an exploration loss that, in the final steps before specific terminal infractions (collision, red light/sign, route deviation, blockage), shifts the policy toward event-specific action priors (e.g., decelerate to avoid collisions/lights, accelerate to escape blockage, uniform steering near route deviation) via KL to prior Beta distributions. Trained from scratch without expert data, Roach achieves stable, sample-efficient learning (~10M steps across Town1–6) and sets a new performance upper bound on CARLA, yielding higher driving scores than the Autopilot by better handling traffic lights.
paper_title: End-to-End Urban Driving by Imitating a Reinforcement Learning Coach

problem: End-to-end imitation learners trained with hard, deterministic actions from automated experts generalize poorly under covariate shift and miss informative supervision signals beyond actions.
method: Use the RL coach as a teacher to provide dense, on-policy, informative targets for IL. Replace the L1 action loss with a KL-divergence to the coach’s action distribution (Beta) as soft targets; add a value head to the IL model trained to regress the coach’s state-value; and match the coach’s latent feature (BEV-based j_RL) via an MSE feature loss. Implemented on a DA-RB/CILRS agent with monocular input (and command-conditioned branch or command-as-input variant), these supervisions significantly improve generalization, achieving ~78% success rate in the NoCrash-dense new town & new weather setting and state-of-the-art/expert-level performance on CARLA public LeaderBoard routes; feature and value supervisions show synergy, with best gains when command information is included to support feature matching.
paper_title: End-to-End Urban Driving by Imitating a Reinforcement Learning Coach

problem: Learning-based dynamic obstacle avoidance struggles to scale and generalize from sparse to dense crowds, and black-box predictors are hard to optimize online; classic CBFs require known dynamics and face feasibility issues with many moving obstacles.
method: Propose Sequential Neural Control Barrier Functions (SN-CBFs) that learn a per-obstacle neural barrier B(x, h) conditioned on a length-k sequence of the obstacle’s relative states (encoded by an LSTM) and the ego state x (encoded by an MLP). Train model-free from demonstrations with a loss that encodes CBF conditions, including a finite-difference approximation of the Lie derivative, then perform boundary refinement by sampling controls near the learned safety boundary to relabel violations and retrain. At runtime, aggregate per-obstacle barrier values with a clipping-and-aggregation rule that marks a state unsafe if any individual barrier is violated while ignoring far obstacles, and select sampled controls whose predicted next states keep the aggregated barrier positive and progress to the goal. This compositional, sequence-conditioned barrier landscape works with nonlinear ego dynamics, alleviates freezing and narrow-corridor issues, and generalizes from training with few obstacles to much denser scenes.
paper_title: Sequential Neural Barriers for Scalable Dynamic Obstacle Avoidance

problem: Modeling collective interaction dynamics across many obstacles is sample-inefficient and does not transfer across densities, causing distribution shift when moving from few to many agents.
method: Formalize sequential decomposability: the joint interaction dynamics can be approximated by aggregating per-agent predictions that depend only on each agent’s recent state sequence. Empirically validate this on pedestrian dynamics (ORCA) by comparing three designs—CoSM (all-agent permutation-invariant encoder), CSM (per-agent sequences only), and ICSM (our interaction-based compositional sequential model that incorporates nearby-agent interactions)—showing ICSM yields the lowest error and best generalization when testing in denser environments. This supports conditioning SN-CBFs on individual obstacle sequences (capturing implicit interactions) to enable robust generalization from sparse-training to dense-testing scenarios.
paper_title: Sequential Neural Barriers for Scalable Dynamic Obstacle Avoidance

problem: Explainable RL methods that attribute state importance lack a unified, objective framework to evaluate and compare their quality across environments and input modalities.
method: XRL-Bench provides a standardized benchmark with pre-trained agents and datasets across tabular and image-based RL environments, a suite of state-importance explainers (TabularSHAP, TabularLIME, SARFA, Perturbation Saliency, DeepSHAP, GradientSHAP, Integrated Gradients), and objective evaluators. It defines five quantitative metrics: AIM and AUM (mask important/unimportant features with reference padding to assess post-hoc accuracy), PGI and PGU (prediction gaps under Gaussian perturbations to important/unimportant features), and RIS (Relative Input Stability). Simple APIs (Environment, Explainer, Evaluator) enable end-to-end benchmarking and reproducible comparisons.
paper_title: XRL-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating and Comparing Explainable Reinforcement Learning Techniques

problem: Interpreting deep RL policies on high-dimensional tabular states is challenging; gradient- or perturbation-based explainers can be unstable, slow, or misaligned with the agent’s decision logic.
method: TabularSHAP trains an ensemble tree student model (e.g., LightGBM) on state–action pairs generated by the DRL policy to mimic its state→action mapping, then applies TreeSHAP to compute optimal local SHAP attributions for actions. On XRL-Bench, TabularSHAP attains top AIM/AUM fidelity in 7/8 tabular evaluations across datasets, demonstrates commendable stability, and is highly efficient (e.g., ~0.003s per sample on large tabular states), enabling faithful and fast explanations of RL decision-making.
paper_title: XRL-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating and Comparing Explainable Reinforcement Learning Techniques

problem: In real-world deployments, RL agents’ opaque decision logic hampers debugging and timely remediation of anomalous behaviors.
method: A practical XRL workflow built on TabularSHAP: collect state–action interaction logs, estimate state-to-action influences via SHAP values, perform global analysis with Summary/Dependence plots, and per-episode analysis with Waterfall plots to reveal factors driving or inhibiting specific actions. Applied to a commercial online game, this process surfaced an API bug causing incorrect action legality flags; fixing it resolved the observed behavioral anomaly.
paper_title: XRL-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating and Comparing Explainable Reinforcement Learning Techniques

problem: Learning precise 6-DoF end-effector pose tracking on legged mobile manipulators is hard when position and orientation are commanded separately, leading to representation discontinuities and reward trade-offs that destabilize training and degrade accuracy.
method: Command the pose using three keypoints that define the vertices of a cube rigidly attached to the end-effector (side length 0.3 m), yielding a single 9D, continuous target that jointly encodes position and orientation. Use a delayed keypoint-tracking reward applied only in the last 2 seconds of each 4-second command to emphasize final accuracy, plus a progress reward to reduce oscillations. This unified representation and reward design simplifies optimization and significantly outperforms quaternion, Euler, and 6D rotation alternatives in simulation, reducing both position and orientation errors.
paper_title: Whole-Body End-Effector Pose Tracking

problem: End-effector commands that are feasible for a fixed base limit workspace and often become invalid on rough terrain; furthermore, switching between locomotion and manipulation can induce instability without careful initialization.
method: Pre-sample 10,000 collision-free arm-only end-effector poses in the base frame, then expand reachability by applying a random base transform TΔb (±0.2 m in x/y, −0.3 to +0.1 m in z, and ±π/6 in roll/pitch/yaw) to each command to elicit whole-body motions. Enforce more uniform spatial coverage via concentric-cylinder binning when sampling commands, and filter terrain-infeasible targets using a precomputed coarse height map (20×20 cm cells) with an 8 cm clearance margin; issue new commands every 4 s within 12 s episodes. Initialize robot states from stable configurations harvested from a pretrained locomotion policy to maintain a robust leg posture and ensure smooth policy switching without jumps.
paper_title: Whole-Body End-Effector Pose Tracking

problem: Achieving accurate, robust whole-body 6-DoF pose tracking across unstructured terrains without relying on terrain models is challenging; model-based MPC struggles with self-collision trade-offs and rough ground.
method: Train a PPO whole-body policy that uses only proprioception plus the 9D keypoint command and outputs 18 joint PD position targets. Shape behavior with rewards for final keypoint tracking, progress, maintaining all four feet in contact, and keeping leg joints near locomotion-like postures; train with a terrain curriculum over flat, rough, discrete obstacles, and stairs, and apply sim-to-real randomization (payloads up to 2 kg, base and end-effector perturbations, sensor noise). The learned controller attains 2.03 cm/2.86° average error on flat terrain and 2.64 cm/3.64° on stairs, and it outperforms a tuned whole-body MPC in mean tracking errors while avoiding self-collision stalls.
paper_title: Whole-Body End-Effector Pose Tracking

problem: Off-policy TD-learning from pixel observations is highly unstable: critics quickly overfit to their own noisy targets and fail to learn from sparse, low-magnitude rewards, and it is unclear why image augmentations help.
method: The paper diagnoses a 'visual deadly triad'—exclusive reliance on TD-loss, unregularized CNN encoders, and low-magnitude sparse rewards—showing it induces catastrophic self-overfitting. Using offline policy-evaluation on fixed transitions, the authors demonstrate near-perfect correlation to target Q-values but poor correlation to Monte-Carlo returns without augmentations, and that overfitting is tied to high-frequency, spatially inconsistent CNN features. They introduce the Normalized Discontinuity (ND) score to quantify feature/gradient discontinuities, show ND strongly correlates with performance in online and offline training, and explain random shift augmentations as implicit gradient smoothing through shifted computation graphs.
paper_title: Stabilizing Off-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels

problem: Prevent catastrophic self-overfitting in pixel-based off-policy RL without relying on image augmentations or auxiliary losses.
method: They propose Local Signal Mixing (LIX), a feature-space layer that randomly mixes each feature with its spatial neighbors via bilinear interpolation within a radius S, explicitly smoothing discontinuous gradient components during backprop while minimally perturbing information content. They further introduce Adaptive LIX (A-LIX), which learns S online via a dual objective that targets a (robustified) ND score, akin to automatic temperature tuning, causing S to anneal as TD-targets become informative. Integrated after encoder nonlinearities in standard agents (DrQ-v2 for DMC, DER for Atari) with negligible overhead and no augmentations, A-LIX achieves state-of-the-art results on DeepMind Control and Atari 100k.
paper_title: Stabilizing Off-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels

problem: Beyond ad-hoc prompting, there is a need to systematically induce and amplify implicit toxicity in LLMs that evades detectors, for red-teaming and safety evaluation.
method: Propose an RL-based attack on a trainable LLM (LLaMA): (1) warm-start via supervised learning on implicit-toxic data auto-generated by GPT-3.5; (2) train a reward model from comparison data that prefers implicit toxic over explicit toxic and non-toxic outputs, using a three-class labeling scheme and GPT-3.5 as annotator to cut costs; (3) ensemble the reward with an existing toxicity classifier to penalize detectable toxicity and optimize with PPO under KL regularization to the supervised policy. This raises attack success rates across classifiers (e.g., BAD 64.29%→90.16%, Davinci003 58.34%→62.85%), and ablations show both the implicit-toxicity reward and classifier penalty are critical.
paper_title: Unveiling the Implicit Toxicity in Large Language Models

problem: Learn to act in two-player zero-sum stochastic games in the average-reward (undiscounted, non-episodic) setting against an arbitrary, potentially non-stationary opponent, achieving sublinear regret without knowing mixing times; prior methods (e.g., R-MAX) had weaker bounds and returned non-stationary policies.
method: Propose UCSG, an optimism-based algorithm that extends UCRL2 to stochastic games. UCSG maintains confidence sets for transition kernels from empirical counts (Step 1–2), constructs an extended SG M+ where Player 1’s actions include admissible transitions from the confidence sets (Sec. 4.1), and computes an optimistic stationary policy via MAXIMIN-EVI (value iteration with Schweitzer transform) (Alg. 2). It runs in phases with doubling counts (Alg. 1). With high probability the true model lies in all confidence sets (Lemma 4.2). UCSG achieves regret ˜O(D S √(A T) + D^3 S^5 A) under a stronger diameter/mixing assumption (Proof of Theorem 3.1) and ˜O((D S^2 A T^2)^{1/3}) under a weaker reachability assumption by reducing to fixed-horizon analysis (Proof of Theorem 3.2).
paper_title: Online Reinforcement Learning in Stochastic Games

problem: Regret analysis in SGs is hard because the opponent can be non-stationary, breaking standard perturbation and bias-span arguments that rely on stationary dynamics.
method: Introduce a per-phase artificial stationary opponent policy (¯π2_k) that matches empirical play frequencies to replace the non-stationary opponent only in the analysis (Sec. 5.1). Show that ¯π2_k is undefined only for a bounded number of steps (Lemma 5.1), and most phases are ‘benign’ (Lemma 5.3). Combine this with new perturbation bounds for Markov chains on mean first passage times (Theorem C.9) to control the bias-span sp(h(M^1_k, π^1_k, ¯π2_k, ·)) ≤ 2D in benign phases (used in Lemma 5.5), enabling tight bounds on the key regret terms Λ(3) and Λ(4) (Lemmas 5.4–5.5) and yielding the overall regret results.
paper_title: Online Reinforcement Learning in Stochastic Games

problem: In the offline (self-play) setting, find a stationary ε-maximin policy for average-reward stochastic games with polynomial sample complexity in 1/ε; prior results focused on discounted settings or had worse ε-dependence.
method: Use an offline variant of UCSG where after Player 1’s optimistic policy is chosen, Player 2 computes an optimistic best response via extended value iteration on the extended model with π1 fixed (Sec. 7). Prove PAC-style sample complexities: L_ε = ˜O(D^3 S^5 A + D^2 S^2 A/ε^2) under the stronger diameter assumption (Theorem 7.1) and L_ε = ˜O(D S^2 A/ε^3) under the weaker assumption (Theorem 7.2). Show the algorithm can output a single stationary ε-optimal policy by selecting the phase with the smallest provable upper bound on suboptimality (Appendix L).
paper_title: Online Reinforcement Learning in Stochastic Games

problem: In cooperative MARL under CTDE, agents observe many entities, but dense self-attention assigns non-zero weight to all entities, distracting learning and slowing convergence. Simply switching to sparse attention early in training hurts exploration and leads to suboptimal policies because agents initially cannot tell which entities are important.
method: S2RL introduces an auxiliary sparse-attention pathway alongside the standard dense self-attention within each agent’s utility network. The dense and sparse paths share WQ, WK, WV projections and a GRU history encoder; the dense path drives executed actions and is trained with a standard TD loss via a mixing network, while the sparse path forms an auxiliary per-agent utility that is mixed into an auxiliary joint value and optimized with an auxiliary TD loss. A weighted total loss L = L_td + λ L_aux trains the shared parameters end-to-end, letting dense attention ensure convergence while sparse attention guides agents to focus on critical entities. This plug-and-play module integrates with VDN, QMIX, and QPLEX and yields large gains on SMAC, e.g., +55% win rate over QPLEX on 6h_vs_8z and enabling QMIX to reach ~80% win rate where the baseline fails.
paper_title: S2RL: Do We Really Need to Perceive All States in Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning?

problem: Softmax-based attention in MARL produces dense weights, preventing agents from discarding irrelevant entities and limiting sample efficiency, especially in complex scenarios with many entities.
method: S2RL equips each agent with a sparse attention mechanism inspired by sparsemax to explicitly zero out low-importance entities. It computes logits Z = QK^T, applies a per-row sorting-based threshold to determine the maximal support size n_m, forms a threshold matrix τ via cumulative sums, and generates sparse attention weights P = [Z − τ]_+ that assign zero to irrelevant entities. The resulting sparse context Y_sparse focuses computation and credit on a small set of salient entities, complementing dense attention and improving discrimination and credit assignment within the CTDE mixing framework.
paper_title: S2RL: Do We Really Need to Perceive All States in Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning?

problem: Model-based Bayesian RL is often intractable due to high-dimensional transition models and posterior marginalization, while existing model-free BRL lacks a provably Bayes-optimal formulation and typically ignores aleatoric uncertainty.
method: Formulate model-free BRL by modeling the Bellman target distribution P_B(b|h,a,ϕ;ω) over bootstrapped next-Q values with a prior over ϕ, and define the predictive optimal Bellman operator B+[Q] as the posterior-marginalized expectation of b. Prove B+[Q]=B*[Q] (Theorem 3.2), so any fixed point Q=B+[Q] is Bayes-optimal. Derive an exact mapping from model-based transition models to Bellman-target models via the coarea formula (Corollary 3.4), highlighting that accurate aleatoric modeling in Bellman space is essential and often non-Gaussian.
paper_title: Bayesian Exploration Networks

problem: It is unclear whether existing model-free Bayesian RL methods (myopic BRL and QBRL) can yield Bayes-optimal policies.
method: Provide a theoretical analysis showing myopic BRL optimizes a Bellman operator that does not propagate epistemic uncertainty across timesteps (Sec. 3.4), and QBRL optimizes over a restricted class Π_QBRL of mixtures of context-conditioned policies. Prove Theorem 3.5 and construct a counterexample (the tiger problem) where Π*_QBRL ∩ Π*_Bayes = ∅ (Corollary 3.6), establishing these approaches can be arbitrarily Bayes-suboptimal.
paper_title: Bayesian Exploration Networks

problem: Practically learning Bayes-optimal policies model-free requires a tractable algorithm that captures both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in the Bellman operator while handling history dependence.
method: Introduce Bayesian Exploration Network (BEN): a recurrent Q-network Q_ω(h,a) to encode histories; an aleatoric normalizing-flow density estimator B(z_al,q_t,ϕ) to model P_B(b|h,a,ϕ;ω); and an epistemic normalizing-flow posterior P_ψ(ϕ) learned via an ELBO. Train by minimizing the Mean Squared Bayesian Bellman Error using the predictive Bellman operator with two-timescale SGD (faster ψ than ω) and partial optimization per timestep; in the limit BEN recovers Bayes-optimal policies. Experiments on tiger and a higher-dimensional search-and-rescue task show near-Bayes-optimal behavior and superiority over BootDQN+Prior/BBAC.
paper_title: Bayesian Exploration Networks

problem: Off-policy RL for surgical robot automation struggles with exploration when only a modest set of demonstrations is available. Prior demo-regularized actor-only methods suffer from critic overestimation that assigns high values to expert-unlike actions, hindering efficient exploration.
method: Introduce an expert-guided actor-critic framework that regularizes both the critic and the actor using an action-dissimilarity term to expert actions. Concretely, augment the reward with a behavior gap penalty and define a regularized Q-function Qπ(s,a)=E[∑γ^t (rt − γα d(at+1, a^e_{t+1}))], so the critic lowers value estimates for expert-unlike actions while the actor maximizes Q plus a similarity term to expert behavior. This joint regularization mitigates critic overestimation and explicitly steers exploration toward expert-like actions, improving sample efficiency. (Supported: paper III-A lines 58–72; lines 29–33; code dex/agents/dex.py update_critic/update_actor)
paper_title: Demonstration-Guided Reinforcement Learning with Efficient Exploration for Task Automation of Surgical Robot

problem: Demonstration data do not cover all states the agent visits early in training, so guidance is unavailable far from demo states; moreover, regularizing only demo-covered Q-values introduces underestimation bias due to the negative regularization term.
method: Propagate expert guidance to unseen states via a non-parametric regression module: for any state, find k nearest demo states and compute a locally weighted regression (exponential kernel) over their actions to estimate an expert-like action. Use this propagated action in both the critic target (to penalize dissimilarity at next states) and the actor objective (to encourage expert-like outputs), enabling robust guidance beyond the demo manifold. Empirically, this non-parametric propagation is more robust than a parametric BC approximator. (Supported: paper III-B lines 76–84; Fig. 2 caption; Implementation details line 88; ablation lines 164; code dex/agents/dex.py compute_propagated_actions and its use in update_critic/update_actor)
paper_title: Demonstration-Guided Reinforcement Learning with Efficient Exploration for Task Automation of Surgical Robot

problem: Expected policy gradients (EPG) can yield zero/low-variance updates but previously required analytic integrals only available for limited critic and policy families (e.g., polynomial critics, exponential-family policies). There is a need for analytic, low-variance policy gradients for broader critics and more general policies, including mixtures and hybrid discrete–continuous cases.
method: Introduce Fourier Policy Gradients (FPG): define an auxiliary policy ˜β(µ−a)=β(a) so the inner policy-gradient integral becomes a convolution (Q * ˜β)(µ), then apply the Fourier transform to turn it into a product F(Q)F(˜β) and take the inverse transform. Using the multiplication–derivative property, factors of iω in frequency space correspond to critic/policy derivatives, yielding tractable analytic expressions for gradient components (Theorems 1–2 and Corollary 2.1). Because the derivation depends only on the characteristic function F(˜β), it supports essentially any policy with unbounded support (and mixtures/hybrid discrete–continuous policies) and delivers closed-form updates for universal-approximation critic families such as trigonometric and radial basis functions.
paper_title: Fourier Policy Gradients

problem: When analytical integration is infeasible, stochastic policy gradients rely on Monte Carlo estimators that can have high variance and are derived via disparate techniques. A unified framework is needed to derive and relate these estimators and enable variance–accuracy trade-offs.
method: From the Fourier formulation, derive a general family of sample-based (Monte Carlo) estimators parameterized by the derivative order M applied to the critic/policy term in frequency space. This recovers and unifies known estimators: M=0 corresponds to REINFORCE/score-function estimators, M=1 to reparameterization-based estimators, and for Gaussian policies M=2 to a sample-based version of Gaussian policy gradients; interpolations between estimators are also enabled.
paper_title: Fourier Policy Gradients

problem: In MDPs with periodic action spaces (e.g., angles), standard critics ignore periodic structure, leading to inefficient learning.
method: Adopt a trigonometric critic Q(a)=cos(fᵀa−h) (and related forms) with a Gaussian policy, and use FPG to derive closed-form mean and covariance updates. The updates include a frequency-damping term e^{-½ fᵀΣf} and modulate exploration—decreasing near maxima and increasing near minima—reflecting the periodic structure. On a turntable control task, this periodic critic (F-EPG) learns faster than a strong DPG baseline with a neural-network critic.
paper_title: Fourier Policy Gradients

problem: Unsupervised text style transfer methods that learn global (inductive) style rules often produce context-inconsistent expressions (e.g., choosing a style word inappropriate for the content) due to the lack of parallel supervision.
method: Adopt a transductive paradigm (TSST) that conditions generation on specific, retrieved target-style sentences for each input. Retrieve top-K target-style sentences using either sparse BM25 or a jointly learned dense retriever, and feed them into an attention-based encoder–decoder that, at each decoding step, attends to both the input and retrieved samples. Train end-to-end with standard reconstruction, cycle, and adversarial style losses, augmented by a retrieval loss and a bag-of-words loss that encourages selecting new words from retrieved samples, yielding context-aware style expressions and improved consistency.
paper_title: Transductive Learning for Unsupervised Text Style Transfer

problem: Term-based retrieval misses semantic context and style words bias similarity, making it hard to find cross-style, content-similar references needed for effective transductive transfer.
method: Design a dense retriever built on a style-independent text embedding q(x), computed as a weighted sum of encoder hidden states with word weights α(w) initialized to down-weight style-discriminative terms based on their across-style frequency distribution. Use cosine similarity with MIPS to retrieve target-style neighbors, and jointly optimize with a retrieval loss that aligns embeddings of the input and its transferred output; embeddings are periodically refreshed during training to keep retrieval up to date.
paper_title: Transductive Learning for Unsupervised Text Style Transfer

problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
method: Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.
paper_title: Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval

problem: Multiple document embeddings can collapse to similar vectors and fail to specialize, and early training may not fairly activate different viewers.
method: Train with a Global-Local Loss that combines a standard global contrastive loss over documents with a local uniformity loss that, for each query, pulls the max-scoring viewer closer while pushing other viewers away, preventing collapse and encouraging specialization. Use an annealed temperature schedule (starting high and decreasing by a factor controlled by α across epochs) to make the softmax over viewers initially more uniform—so all viewers receive gradient—and progressively sharper to stabilize assignments. Ablations show both the local loss and annealed temperature contribute; removing either degrades SQuAD recall and reduces diversity (PPL/LV) among viewers.
paper_title: Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval

problem: Producer-side A/B tests in recommender systems must merge treatment and control items into a single list per consumer, which typically violates SUTVA and induces confounding via different effective attentions across groups. Existing counterfactual interleaving methods resolve conflicts ad hoc (e.g., random tie-breaking), leading to biased readouts, especially under imbalanced traffic splits.
method: The paper formalizes producer-side experimentation using an attention function h over ranks and shows observed readouts equal intrinsic utility u composed with a convoluted attention h_k via convolution kernels π_kj induced by the merging rule. It proposes two design principles: (1) Consistency—π_kj must be invariant across treatment k so h_k does not differ by group; (2) Monotonicity—π_kj must be stochastically non-decreasing in j so h_k remains non-increasing. Theorem 4.1 identifies the attention-convolution confounding; with these principles, the expected experiment readouts correctly reflect ranker quality (Corollary 4.4).
paper_title: Producer-Side Experiments Based on Counterfactual Interleaving Designs for Online Recommender Systems

problem: How to construct a valid merged ranker that resolves conflicts between counterfactual rankings R0 and R1 while enabling unbiased producer-side comparisons at any ramp percentage.
method: Introduce a rigorous counterfactual interleaving procedure: (i) compute control and treatment counterfactual rankings R0 and R1; (ii) form the SUTVA ranker R* by assigning each item its position from its group’s counterfactual; (iii) derive the final merged ranker R by preserving R*’s relative order and resolving ties with a non-constant probability β0(x,y). The tie-break β0(x,y) is derived in closed form (Theorem 5.1) as a function of traffic allocations p0,p1 and whether each conflicting item would rank above j under the opposite model (R1(x), R0(y)), guaranteeing identical position distributions across groups (consistency) and, further, stochastic dominance of kernels with rank (monotonicity; Theorem 5.2). In some cases β0 is 0 or 1, yielding deterministic resolution instead of random tie-breaking.
paper_title: Producer-Side Experiments Based on Counterfactual Interleaving Designs for Online Recommender Systems

problem: Deploying producer-side experiments at scale can be computationally heavy when generating both counterfactual rankings across multi-stage pipelines, and prior interleaving led to significant creator misplacement in practice.
method: Provide a deployment recipe and computational shortcut: when treatment’s candidate generators strictly superset control’s, compute only the treatment counterfactual ranking once, then obtain the control ranking by filtering and filling from the treatment list, avoiding duplicate pipeline passes. In LinkedIn’s online edge recommender, replacing UniCoRn with the proposed design corrected widespread misplacements (≈85% of creators previously off by ±2–3 positions) and yielded fair, interpretable producer-side readouts.
paper_title: Producer-Side Experiments Based on Counterfactual Interleaving Designs for Online Recommender Systems

problem: A/B tests for search ranking on e-commerce platforms suffer from long runtimes and low sensitivity for sparse, delayed outcomes like conversion; prior interleaving methods are click-focused, can be biased, and are costly to run at production scale.
method: Introduce a competitive-pair Team Draft interleaving: flip a single coin per query to decide first picker, then iteratively draft the next available item from each ranker to form competitive pairs; identical items are inserted without team assignment. Credit is computed by pairwise 'wins' (e.g., based on clicks/bookings) aggregated at the user level, yielding an unbiased estimator because each ranker has equal probability to appear first in a pair (position effects cancel). The design enables efficient serving via parallel calls and supports built-in data quality checks (e.g., impression balance and first-in-pair rate near 50/50). Deployed in production, it delivers about 50X sensitivity speedup over A/B on conversion while meeting latency/scale constraints.
paper_title: Harnessing the Power of Interleaving and Counterfactual Evaluation for Airbnb Search Ranking

problem: Interleaving can break set-level optimization, risk UX disruption (e.g., map views), and struggles with continuous outcomes; there is a need for a generalizable, high-sensitivity online evaluation that preserves the original user experience.
method: Propose an online counterfactual evaluation that generates both control and treatment result lists per search but shows only one (randomized by user and experiment ID), using the unseen list as counterfactual. Build metrics from (i) direct metric decomposition using relative/absolute rank positions and (ii) a novel estimated-reward estimator that depends only on rank differences with an exponential attention decay f(r)=γ^-r and a similarity threshold α; also define an OEC by combining the two (equal weights). This hybrid preserves UX, improves sensitivity and fidelity for set-level objectives, and achieves up to 100X speedup (for the τ_g estimator) with A/B-consistent point estimates (overall correlation ≈0.65).
paper_title: Harnessing the Power of Interleaving and Counterfactual Evaluation for Airbnb Search Ranking

problem: Sequential recommenders often need different embedding/model sizes to balance accuracy and efficiency across deployment settings, but training and tuning separate models for each size is costly and inflexible.
method: fMRLRec extends Matryoshka Representation Learning to sequential recommendation by nesting multiple granularities M={2,4,8,...,D} inside a single model and optimizing them jointly. It introduces an fMRLRec operator that applies structured masking to weight matrices so the first M[j] input channels only interact with corresponding weight blocks, enabling all sub-dimensions to be trained in one forward/backward pass and later extracted by slicing weights for deployment. The training objective adds explicit multi-dimension loss terms to directly supervise each sub-model. A Linear Recurrent Unit (LRU) state-space backbone provides parallel training and efficient inference, yielding a train-once, deploy-anywhere framework. (Supported by: lines 56–79, 80–94, 150–156, 164–169, 118–131, 133–147).
paper_title: Train Once, Deploy Anywhere: Matryoshka Representation Learning for Multimodal Recommendation

problem: Integrating textual and visual item attributes into sequential recommenders without heavy fusion modules while ensuring the features align in a shared space.
method: fMRLRec encodes item text (title, price, brand, categories) and images using pretrained encoders, concatenates the embeddings, and applies a single linear projection to align them into the model’s hidden dimension D. This lightweight fusion plugs directly into the LRU-based sequence module. Ablations show both modalities contribute to performance, with language features having the larger impact. (Supported by: lines 100–117, 261–269, 243–256).
paper_title: Train Once, Deploy Anywhere: Matryoshka Representation Learning for Multimodal Recommendation

problem: Training multi-granularity recommendation models independently imposes substantial memory and compute overhead for parameters and activations.
method: By embedding smaller weights and activations into larger ones via the fMRLRec linear transformation and computing all slices with a single masked matrix multiplication, fMRLRec avoids training separate models. Theoretical analysis shows about 33% savings in parameters and activations compared to independently training each size, and empirical results confirm convergence toward this savings across target sizes. (Supported by: lines 26–31, 171–187, 241–242).
paper_title: Train Once, Deploy Anywhere: Matryoshka Representation Learning for Multimodal Recommendation

problem: Feedback loops in collaborative filtering bias observed ratings, making it unclear which user–item ratings reflect intrinsic preferences versus recommender-induced effects. Can we recover the underlying true preference matrix using only a single snapshot of the observed ratings?
method: Model the recommender as an iterative item–item similarity process where observed ratings are updated via Hadamard-masked recommendations, and replace unknown acceptance masks with a constant acceptance probability α. After centering users and normalizing items (adjusted cosine similarity), assume series coefficients are powers of α and enforce convergence via a spectral radius bound. This yields a closed-form relation between the normalized observed and true matrices that can be inverted via SVD: compute ˆR_obs = U Σ_obs V^T and obtain ˆR_true = U Σ_true V^T with per-singular-value shrinkage σ_true = (-0.5 α σ_obs) + sqrt(0.25 α^2 σ_obs^2 + 1/α); use truncated SVD for scalability. (Supported by Assumptions 1–5 and Theorem 1; Algorithm 1.)
paper_title: Deconvolving Feedback Loops in Recommender Systems

problem: How to quantify, for each user–item rating and for entire datasets, the extent to which a recommender system influenced the observed ratings?
method: First deconvolve to obtain ˆR_true. For each item, plot observed vs. deconvolved ratings and robustly fit a line using RANSAC, then rotate/translate to align the fit with the y-axis and scale to equalize maxima. Define a hyperbola-based score per point with an asymptote of slope θ=1 through the origin; use the vertex as the score so larger values indicate stronger recommender influence. Aggregate to a dataset-level RS score as the fraction of non-zero scores, and average per item to rank likely recommended items. (Figure 2 procedure; Algorithm 2 description; score in Eq. (10); RS scores described in Table 1 and Sec. 3.4.)
paper_title: Deconvolving Feedback Loops in Recommender Systems

problem: Conventional exploration–exploitation (E&E) methods for CTR prediction largely focus on estimating uncertainty and ignore how exploration changes subsequent model training and convergence, exacerbating feedback-loop and exposure bias, especially for new/long-tail items.
method: Introduce a Pseudo-Exploration module that simulates one-step model updating after exploring an item, and show this is equivalent to adding an adversarial perturbation to the input embedding. The proposed AGE computes an exploration-adjusted score by perturbing the input embedding h along the normalized adversarial gradient (fi_g) of the loss w.r.t. h, scaled by prediction uncertainty δy, and then evaluates f(h + Δh | θ) for ranking (Eq. 7–10). Uncertainty δy is estimated via MC-Dropout (UCB: variance; TS: difference to non-dropout model), and fi_g is computed with FGM or multi-step PGD. This reframes exploration as adversarial embedding perturbation rather than directly adding uncertainty to scores, yielding significant gains over strong baselines (e.g., +5.41% vs. UR-gradient-TS and +15.3% vs. UR-gradient-UCB offline; online A/B: +6.4% CTR, +5.5% AFR).
paper_title: Adversarial Gradient Driven Exploration for Deep Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: In industrial top-K recommendation, exploration budget is scarce and exploring items with intrinsically low CTR often yields little utility because they remain non-competitive even after better estimation, wasting impressions.
method: Propose a Dynamic Gating Unit (DGU) to pre-determine whether to apply exploration at the user–item level. The gate explores only when the predicted CTR exceeds an item-level CTR baseline estimated by a shallow DNN that uses item features (sharing embeddings with the main model but not updating them). This concentrates exploration on potentially displayable items and stabilizes variance-heavy strategies (notably UCB). Ablations show removing DGU causes large drops (e.g., AGE-UCB −19.9% clicks), and a learned dynamic threshold outperforms fixed thresholds.
paper_title: Adversarial Gradient Driven Exploration for Deep Click-Through Rate Prediction

problem: Existing stochastic learning-to-rank methods for Plackett–Luce (PL) models (e.g., PL-Rank-1/2, StochasticRank) perform unbiased policy-gradient estimation but their computational cost scales poorly with ranking length K and the number of items D, making long-cutoff optimization impractical.
method: Introduce PL-Rank-3, an unbiased policy-gradient estimator that computes the same metric-based gradient approximation as PL-Rank-2 while reducing complexity from O(N·D·K) to O(N·(D + K·log D)) for N samples. It samples rankings using Gumbel perturbations and then, per sampled ranking, computes placement reward PR_y,i, risk RI_y,i, and expected direct reward DR_y,i in O(K), reusing these quantities across all D items to eliminate per-item summations over K. The overall cost is thus dominated by the (partial) sorting inherent to sampling, matching the order of fast sorting algorithms.
paper_title: Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity

problem: In practice, training time of LTR algorithms grows sharply with K, limiting scalability without sacrificing performance; a faster method is needed that preserves or improves ranking quality while reducing wall-clock time.
method: Empirically evaluate PL-Rank-3 on Yahoo! Webscope-Set1, MSLR-Web30k, and Istella across K∈{5,10,25,50,100} and N∈{100,1000}. PL-Rank-3 completes epochs substantially faster and is nearly insensitive to K, consistently achieving the lowest epoch times versus PL-Rank-2 and StochasticRank (e.g., on Istella N=100, increasing K from 5 to 100 adds ~28s for PL-Rank-3 versus +25–49 minutes for baselines). Over 200 minutes of training, PL-Rank-3 attains equal or higher NDCG in most scenarios, despite StochasticRank’s higher sample efficiency, and provides the first direct comparison to StochasticRank by adapting it to Gumbel noise.
paper_title: Learning-to-Rank at the Speed of Sampling: Plackett-Luce Gradient Estimation With Minimal Computational Complexity

problem: Pointwise PLM-based news recommenders scale well but ignore comparative preferences between candidates, while pairwise approaches capture comparisons but are often costly or lack guarantees.
method: GLIMPSE jointly fine-tunes a single generative PLM to perform both pointwise relevance prediction and pairwise preference comparison using a text-to-text formulation with discrete task prompts and target tokens. The model outputs Rel(x|H) via Yes/No token probabilities and Pref(xi, xj|H) via Candidate A/B token probabilities, with pairwise probabilities realized through a Bradley–Terry mapping from token probabilities. Multi-task training mixes samples from both tasks to align with the PLM pretraining objective and improve ranking metrics, yielding one model usable for both scoring and comparisons.
paper_title: Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation

problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
method: GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.
paper_title: Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation

problem: Sparse pairwise re-ranking methods often lack theoretical guarantees on when and why they improve rankings over pointwise baselines.
method: The paper models the pairwise refinement as a Markov chain over binary relevance state vectors, with a transition matrix parameterized by correct/incorrect pairwise outcomes (µ, ν). It proves that a single or multiple RTL passes yield a positive gain in expected MRR under testable conditions relating µ and ν to the pointwise-induced state distribution (e.g., µ ≤ (π_{i+1}/π_i)·ν). It further analyzes the pointwise state distribution via Beta assumptions over Rel scores to estimate these conditions, providing a principled criterion for when RTL reranking is beneficial.
paper_title: Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation

problem: Recommender systems typically assign a uniform, large embedding size to all users/items, which is memory-inefficient. Prior RL-based embedding-size search operates over small, discrete action sets, limiting granularity and often yielding suboptimal accuracy–memory trade-offs under fixed budgets.
method: CIESS formulates per-user/item embedding size selection as a continuous-action RL problem using an actor–critic (TD3) framework with separate user/item actors. It enforces chosen sizes via a binary mask over a max-size embedding table, and designs states that include normalized popularity, current size, and a quality-ratio indicator capturing accuracy drop vs. full-size. The reward blends ranking quality with a squared memory-cost term (scaled by λ) to encourage fast early pruning and fine-grained later adjustments. The approach is model-agnostic and plugs into latent factor recommenders.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: Exploring an enormous continuous action space (embedding sizes) makes critic estimates high-variance and greedy evaluation over all actions infeasible; simple noise injection is insufficient for efficient, robust convergence.
method: CIESS introduces a random walk-based local exploration around the actor’s proposed size: it constructs an action graph linking neighboring integer sizes within a threshold and performs short random walks to sample a small candidate set. The critic scores these candidates and the highest-Q action is selected to update the policy. This targeted local search, combined with small Gaussian exploration noise, improves sample efficiency and convergence, with ablations showing significant drops when random walk is removed.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: During RL search, the base recommender is only partially trained per iteration, so a single embedding-size decision can be brittle; practitioners also need precise sparsity targets and masks for multiple budgets without rerunning search.
method: CIESS maintains a top-l pool of candidate embedding masks that meet the target sparsity based on their quality indicators during search. After search, it performs selective retraining to convergence for each candidate and selects the best-performing mask; the scheme can be extended to additional lower sparsities to obtain optimal masks for multiple memory budgets in one run.
paper_title: Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems

problem: Conversational dense retrieval models are trained on limited, fixed multi-turn conversations and struggle to generalize to the diverse ways users express intents; they also lack hard negatives that teach sensitivity to subtle intent shifts.
method: CONVAUG generates multi-level augmented conversations: token-level (token masking as positives; entity replacement as hard negatives), turn-level with a dependency-aware DAG (mask only non-ancestor turns, reorder turns while preserving topological order, and insert noisy but on-topic turns), and conversation-level (paraphrasing as positives; intent shifting as hard negatives). An LLM (Llama 2-Chat 7B) is used to identify dependencies and produce alterations. Training uses a multi-task objective that combines a passage-ranking loss with contrastive learning: two positive augmentations per conversation are pulled together while in-batch and generated hard negatives are pushed apart, yielding a more robust conversational context encoder.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: LLM-based augmentation can introduce false positives/negatives and hallucinations, degrading training data quality for conversational retrieval.
method: A cognition-aware, three-step prompting process guides generation: (1) Comprehension Synthesis to extract key themes and intents/entities, (2) Associative Expansion to produce related alternatives, and (3) Conclusion to compose the final augmented conversation. This template is instantiated for paraphrasing, entity replacement, dependency identification, noisy turn insertion, and intent shifting, reducing off-intent or hallucinated outputs. Ablations show replacing this process with a naive prompt yields ~3% MRR degradation on QReCC, indicating higher-quality augmentations.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: Uniformly sampling augmented pairs can underfit complex conversations and overfit simple ones; models benefit from matching augmentation difficulty to conversation complexity.
method: A difficulty-adaptive sample filter scores each conversation as Diff(C) = |Th| + (|Topic(C)| × PPL(C)), where topics are counted via a BERTopic model and PPL is computed by prompting an LLM. For positives, similarity between augmented pairs is measured with a sentence-transformers encoder (BERTSim), and one positive pair whose dissimilarity matches the conversation’s difficulty bucket is selected. For negatives, k hard negatives are chosen by higher average similarity to the selected positives, emphasizing more challenging negatives for difficult conversations. This targeted selection improves over random or ‘easy’ selection baselines.
paper_title: Generalizing Conversational Dense Retrieval via LLM-Cognition Data Augmentation

problem: Multitask explainable recommenders often produce incoherent outputs where the generated textual explanation does not align with the predicted rating, since explanations are not explicitly conditioned on the rating.
method: CIER uses a pipeline with an LLM backbone (LLaMA2-7B fine-tuned via LoRA) to first predict a soft rating distribution using a verbalizer over tokens {'1'...'5'}, then embeds this distribution into the LLM word embedding space via SR2WE (a weighted sum of the embeddings of the rating tokens). The explanation generator then conditions on user/item info and this rating embedding to produce rating-aware explanations, trained in a pipeline manner akin to next-token prediction. Ablations show that removing rating-aware conditioning or SR2WE degrades explainability and text quality, confirming the benefit of explicit rating conditioning.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Coherence between ratings and generated explanations is hard to evaluate at scale; prior automatic metrics rely on manually annotated data or bespoke classifiers, which are costly and limit reproducibility.
method: The paper proposes an annotation-free automatic coherence evaluation using public models: GPT-4 (gpt-4o) is prompted to judge whether an explanation matches a given rating, and a BERT-based sentiment classifier (bert-base-multilingual-uncased-sentiment) predicts a sentiment rating for each explanation. Coherence is measured as the proportion of cases where the sentiment rating is within ±1 of the model’s rating. This framework is applied to all models and correlates with human judgment, showing CIER achieves higher coherence than baselines.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Explainable recommenders struggle to capture salient item features in explanations (low explainability) and face a train–inference mismatch when training on hard labels but inferring with soft rating distributions.
method: CIER introduces two training techniques: (a) a curriculum learning strategy with an auxiliary keyword generation task that gradually transitions to full explanation generation via a linear schedule, helping the model first learn salient features then compose complete explanations; and (b) rating smoothing that spreads probability mass only to neighboring ratings, leveraging rating ordinality and better matching the soft distribution used at inference. Experiments show curriculum learning outperforms vanilla and two-stage training, and ablations indicate removing rating smoothing reduces FMR/ROUGE across datasets.
paper_title: Coherency Improved Explainable Recommendation via Large Language Model

problem: Validating dense phrase retrievers typically requires building full-corpus indexes, which is costly; moreover, validation on small random subcorpora correlates poorly with full-scale retrieval, leading to unreliable model selection.
method: Construct a compact 'hard subcorpus' H_k by using a pre-trained retriever to gather the context passages of the top-k retrieved phrases for each development query and merging them with the gold-passages set C0, ensuring answers are present. This creates a validation index that better mimics full-corpus difficulty while remaining small. Empirically, retrieval accuracy on H_k rapidly approaches full-corpus accuracy and preserves model ordering with much smaller indexes (e.g., ordering stabilizes around H_10 ~6.1M entries and is more reliable than random subsets of comparable or even much larger size).
paper_title: Bridging the Training-Inference Gap for Dense Phrase Retrieval

problem: DensePhrases training optimizes separate losses with relatively few negatives, while at inference the model must select a single phrase against billions of candidates—creating a training-inference mismatch that harms retrieval quality.
method: Replace the separate in-passage and in-/pre-batch objectives with a unified contrastive loss that pools all negatives together and applies source-specific weights λ to better mimic the large negative pool at inference (λ_inb, λ_inp, λ_hard). Expand negatives from only start/end tokens to all tokens in each negative passage, increasing negatives to B×L and B×T×L for in-/pre-batch sets. Further reduce the gap by mining model-based hard negatives: retrieve top phrases with a previous model, remove likely false negatives by excluding retrieved phrases whose surrounding passage contains an answer, and during fine-tuning add all tokens from h sampled hard-negative passages to the unified loss while updating both encoders. This yields consistent gains, e.g., +2.7 Acc@1 on NQ and +2.0 on TriviaQA for phrase retrieval, and up to +4.0 Acc@20 on NQ for passage retrieval.
paper_title: Bridging the Training-Inference Gap for Dense Phrase Retrieval

problem: Recommendation foundation models are typically text-centric and struggle to natively fuse image content with textual and personalization signals, limiting their ability to handle diverse recommendation tasks within a single interface.
method: VIP5 introduces multimodal personalized prompts that interleave textual, visual, and personalization tokens into one unified sequence. A mapping network with two linear layers converts CLIP image features into k image tokens that are appended to the corresponding item tokens, and whole-word plus category embeddings indicate token boundaries and types (text vs. visual). A T5-based JointEncoder consumes these sequences and is trained with a conditional generation objective over 29 prompts spanning sequential recommendation, direct recommendation, and explanation, unifying training and inference across modalities and tasks.
paper_title: VIP5: Towards Multimodal Foundation Models for Recommendation

problem: Fully fine-tuning large foundation backbones for multimodal recommendation is computationally expensive, memory-intensive, and can overfit, especially when adding visual tokens.
method: VIP5 adopts adapter-based parameter-efficient tuning by freezing the P5/T5 backbone and inserting lightweight bottleneck adapters after self-attention and feed-forward layers in the encoder and after cross-attention in the decoder. Only adapter parameters are updated, with a reduction factor (e.g., 8) controlling the bottleneck size. Ablations show that activating adapters in both self- and cross-attention matches or exceeds full fine-tuning while cutting trainable parameters to about 3.6% and reducing training time and memory by ~21% and ~18% on the Toys dataset (11.10 vs. 14.08 min/epoch; 29.0 vs. 35.6 GB). VIP5 consequently outperforms strong baselines across sequential, direct, and explanation task groups.
paper_title: VIP5: Towards Multimodal Foundation Models for Recommendation

problem: Standard heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) methods assume fixed binary treatments and cannot capture scenarios where an ordinal/continuous treatment only causes an effect after crossing an individual- or subgroup-specific threshold, nor can they prescribe the minimal effective dose/offer.
method: They formalize trigger-based HTE and propose trigger-based causal trees that, within each subpopulation (tree node), learn a trigger θ that binarizes the treatment (Ti = 1 if ti ≥ θ) and selects θ to maximize a trigger-based partition score proportional to Nℓ·τ̂(Sℓ)^2. During splitting, the algorithm jointly chooses the covariate split and the child-node triggers by enumerating all candidate treatment thresholds in the node and picking those that maximize the ACE-based objective. Triggers can differ per node/level, and trees are pruned by statistical significance to improve robustness. (Supported by: problem/formulation lines 99–105, 147–153; Algorithm 1 lines 123–127; implementation of threshold enumeration/binarization and per-child triggers in CTL.objective_cont/effect, lines 486–585, 625–636, 285–306, 395–399.)
paper_title: Learning Triggers for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

problem: Causal trees can overfit and yield HTE estimates that do not generalize to unseen data, a challenge amplified when simultaneously learning triggers and partitions.
method: They introduce a validation-based generalization penalty in the node splitting score: define C(Svalℓ) = Nvalℓ · |τ̂(Svalℓ) − τ̃(Strℓ)| and a new partition measure FC that maximizes training effect magnitude while penalizing poor agreement with validation, controlled by λ and normalized for sample sizes. This yields CT-L; they further combine with ‘honest’ variance control to create CT-HL (separate estimation set) and CT-HV (use validation as estimation), integrating variance penalties into the split objective. These criteria are applied in both binary and trigger-based (continuous/ordinal) settings. (Supported by: validation penalty and FC lines 111–121; honest variants lines 133–142; implementation in CTL.objective/objective_cont with train–val ACE and λ-weighted cost, lines 444–485 and 486–585; variance terms in honest modes, lines 242–252, 288–296.)
paper_title: Learning Triggers for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects

problem: Autoencoder-based hybrid recommenders typically predict ratings with matrix factorization (MF) factors while using neural network representations only as regularizers, leading to unclear design motivation, difficult alternating optimization over large U,V matrices, extra hyperparameters to tune, and limited expressiveness from dot-product prediction.
method: Introduce the Neural Representation for Prediction (NRP) framework that removes MF latent variables and alignment penalties, and uses the encoders’ outputs as the sole user/item representations for prediction. The objective is trained end-to-end without alternating over U,V, eliminating the λ2,λ3 closeness hyperparameters and reducing parameters/memory. A theoretical analysis proves that prior MF+autoencoder objectives become equivalent to NRP as λ2=λ3→∞, explaining that earlier neural representations behaved as regularizers and clarifying a feasible-set view of solutions; it also argues why optimizing the prior objective with very large λ is ill-conditioned and impractical.
paper_title: Neural Representations in Hybrid Recommender Systems: Prediction versus Regularization

problem: Standard network embedding methods compress all information of a node into a single vector, which fails when nodes are multifaceted (polysemous) and connect to disparate neighborhoods for different reasons.
method: Introduce polysemous network embedding where each node has K facet-specific embeddings (target and context matrices U_i, H_i with shape K×D), and treat the activated facet as a latent variable within a skip-gram objective. Derive a tractable lower bound of the likelihood via Jensen's inequality so the final objective mirrors skip-gram with an external sum over facet assignments, enabling standard negative sampling and SGD with minimal changes to existing toolchains. Train by sampling an activated facet for each node in each observation (Algorithm 1; controlled by sampling rate R) and updating only the corresponding facet embeddings along with negatives.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Learning with multiple facets requires estimating which facet is active for a node under each context, ideally without relying on attributes and while remaining efficient.
method: Estimate global node–facet priors by factorizing the adjacency matrix with nonnegative matrix factorization (community discovery) to obtain p(k|v) and per-node facet distributions p(v). For each observation o (e.g., DeepWalk window), compute a context facet distribution p(o) by averaging the involved nodes’ p(v), then define the node-conditional assignment p(v|o) ∝ min(p(k|v), p_k(o)) (normalized) to discourage unlikely facets; sample facets from p(v|o) during training. This facet estimation/assignment scheme also generalizes: for heterogeneous networks (PolyPTE) use asymmetric NMF on the bipartite adjacency and per-edge p(o), and for PolyGCN construct per-facet neighborhoods and graphs (A ≈ Σ_k A_k with A_k(i,j) ∝ P(i,k)·Q(j,k)) to run separate GCNs per facet.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Reconstruction-based autoencoders and dot-product predictors can be inefficient and insufficiently expressive for rating prediction in hybrid recommendation, especially with high-dimensional side information and large user–item spaces.
method: Propose a direct neural structure within NRP that removes decoders and predicts ratings without reconstruction. It learns user/item representations from interaction vectors (rows/columns of the rating matrix) and multiple side-information sources via dedicated encoders, concatenates user/item embeddings into a joint representation, and maps it to ratings using an MLP instead of a dot product. Using interaction vectors rather than IDs and replacing the dot product with an MLP increases expressiveness while cutting memory and training time. Experiments on MovieLens, Amazon, and Ichiba show improved RMSE/precision over MF, autoencoder-based hybrids (DHA, aSDAE), and ACCM, with faster training and lower memory usage.
paper_title: Neural Representations in Hybrid Recommender Systems: Prediction versus Regularization

problem: Downstream tasks must leverage multiple facet embeddings per node for prediction, not a single fused vector.
method: For node classification, concatenate facet embeddings or probability-weighted concatenations using p(k|v) to form a joint vector.
