For link prediction, define similarity between two nodes as the sum over all facet pairs of their inner products, weighted by the product of their facet probabilities, allowing all plausible facet interactions to contribute to the score.
paper_title: Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding

problem: Link recommenders trained and evaluated on observed graphs suffer exposure bias: many missing links are false negatives due to users not being exposed, causing underestimation of link probabilities for low-propensity items and biased evaluation.
method: Proposes three propensity-aware estimators of the true risk (risk if all nodes were exposed): R_w (inverse-propensity weighted positives with downweighted negatives), R_PU (adapts positive–unlabeled correction by removing appropriately weighted negatives per positive), and R_AP (adds weighted positives for negatives). Each estimator reweights examples using learned exposure propensities π and link probabilities y. The paper proves unbiasedness conditions (e.g., R_w and R_AP are unbiased when π and y are correct; R_PU when π is correct), variance ordering (Var(R_AP) < Var(R_w) < Var(R_PU) < Var(naive)), and provides sufficient conditions under which their bias is lower than the naive estimator.
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Learning both exposure propensities and link probabilities without amplifying exposure bias, while ensuring low true risk (not merely fitting observed links).
method: Derives a generalization bound (via a Rademacher-style complexity term) showing that the proposed estimators tightly upper-bound true risk, and uses it to construct a training objective that jointly learns π and y: minimize a weighted sum of the estimator-based risk (using R_w/R_PU/R_AP) and the negative log-likelihood of observed data to avoid trivial solutions. Implements parametric models (e.g., SciBERT-based features for y and a categorical propensity model for π) trained with SGD, showing accurate propensity recovery and improved precision/recall against simulated true links, plus more diverse recommendations on real citation graphs.
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Feedback loops in link recommendation amplify exposure bias over time, further suppressing relevant low-propensity items.
method: Analyzes an iterative training-and-recommendation process under a categorical model of nodes, proving that naive training skews recommendations toward high-propensity categories with high probability and at a rate depending on the ratio of propensity-weighted link probabilities. Shows theoretically and via simulation that correcting for exposure (using the proposed estimators) stabilizes recommendation proportions, mitigating the feedback loop and maintaining exposure to relevant low-propensity items.
paper_title: Correcting Exposure Bias for Link Recommendation

problem: Dense text retrieval training relies on negative sampling, but common strategies either pick uninformative negatives (random sampling) or include false negatives (top-k hard negatives), both of which harm convergence and stability.
method: Analyze gradients under BCE loss to characterize negative instances: uninformative negatives yield near-zero gradient means, while false negatives increase gradient variance. An empirical study with AR2 on MS MARCO ranks negatives by model relevance scores and shows that those near the positives have relatively larger gradient means and smaller variances. This identifies 'ambiguous negatives' (neither too easy nor too hard) as high-quality training negatives to prioritize.
paper_title: SimANS: Simple Ambiguous Negatives Sampling for Dense Text Retrieval

problem: Design an efficient negative sampling scheme that emphasizes informative yet safe (non-false) negatives at scale for dense retrievers.
method: Propose SimANS, a sampling probability distribution that assigns higher probabilities to negatives whose relevance scores are close to a positive’s score and penalizes much lower or higher scores. Instantiate the distribution with an exponential function and two hyperparameters: a (controls density) and b (controls the peak). For efficiency, restrict candidates to the top-k ANN-retrieved negatives and replace the mean positive score with the score of a randomly sampled positive, reducing computation to O(k). Precompute probabilities and sample during training. SimANS is model-agnostic and can be plugged into various dense retrievers.
paper_title: SimANS: Simple Ambiguous Negatives Sampling for Dense Text Retrieval

problem: Generic LM pre-training (e.g., MLM/seq2seq) under-develops sentence-level representations needed for dense retrieval, and prior AE-style pre-training can be too easy or data-inefficient (loss on few masked tokens).
method: RetroMAE introduces a masked auto-encoder workflow tailored for retrieval: each input sentence is masked twice with different masks. A full-scale BERT encoder (12 layers, 768-d) consumes a moderately masked input (15–30%) to produce a sentence embedding (CLS). A one-layer transformer decoder receives the sentence embedding plus an aggressively masked input (50–70%) and reconstructs the original sentence via an MLM objective. The asymmetric architecture and masking ratios make reconstruction rely on the sentence embedding, forcing the encoder to capture in-depth semantics, while remaining simple (no data augmentation or negative sampling).
paper_title: RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder

problem: Standard decoding in AE/MLM pre-training derives loss only from masked positions and uses a single, fixed context per token, under-utilizing training data and limiting signal diversity.
method: RetroMAE proposes enhanced decoding based on two-stream self-attention and a position-specific attention mask. It builds query (H1) and context (H2) streams conditioned on the sentence embedding and applies a position-specific mask matrix that always masks each token’s own position (diagonal) while exposing sampled visible tokens, enabling every token to be reconstructed and each to use a unique context. With a single-layer decoder, this yields 100% token utilization and diversified contexts, improving data efficiency and retrieval quality (e.g., MSMARCO MRR@10 0.3553 vs. 0.3462; NQ MRR@10 0.7704 vs. 0.7562 in ablations).
paper_title: RetroMAE: Pre-Training Retrieval-oriented Language Models Via Masked Auto-Encoder

problem: Conversational dense retrieval often concatenates the entire conversation history, which is long and noisy, causing shortcut history dependency where the retriever over-relies on irrelevant past turns and neglects the current query.
method: HAConvDR introduces context-denoised query reformulation via pseudo relevance judgments (PRJs) of historical turns. For each prior turn, it tests whether concatenating the historical query and its ground-truth passage to the current query improves retrieval metrics; only turns that improve performance are labeled relevant. The reformulated query is constructed by concatenating the current query with only these selected (relevant) historical queries and passages, reducing noise while retaining useful context. This PRJ explicitly considers both historical queries and their passages, capturing intent more fully than query-only selection.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: A single ground-truth passage per query provides weak supervision for training conversational retrievers, especially when reformulated queries still contain residual noise.
method: HAConvDR mines additional supervision from history using the same PRJs: historical passages labeled relevant become pseudo positives, while those labeled irrelevant serve as historical hard negatives. It then performs history-aware contrastive learning in a dual-encoder setup, combining these pseudo positives and historical hard negatives with standard in-batch/BM25 negatives. This teaches the model to leverage helpful historical passages and avoid harmful ones, alleviating shortcut dependency and strengthening retrieval training. In implementation, the passage encoder is frozen and the query encoder is updated, with one pseudo positive and one historical hard negative sampled per instance for efficiency.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: In real-world settings, ground-truth passages for historical turns may be unavailable, limiting the applicability of history-aware training.
method: HAConvDR adapts by substituting historical ground-truth passages with top-k retrieved passages (pseudo relevance feedback) when computing PRJs and during reformulation/training. This preserves the PRJ-driven selection and history-aware contrastive framework with minimal changes, and experiments show PRJ still improves reformulated queries while the full model remains competitive, especially at top ranks.
paper_title: History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval

problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
method: Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: How does dense-retrieval fine-tuning redistribute knowledge within transformer layers (knowledge decentralization), and is this effect architecture- and pooling-dependent?
method: Apply integrated gradients neuron attribution to the intermediate and output dense layers by varying each neuron's weight from 0 to its learned value, normalizing per-example by max attribution, and counting the percentage of examples where neurons exceed a threshold (0.1×max; 0.01 for Llama) to enable cross-dataset comparability. Compare before/after fine-tuning for DPR-query/passage vs BERT-CLS on NQ and MS MARCO, Contriever vs BERT-Mean, and RepLlama vs Llama (EOS). Results: DPR-query shows increased intermediate-layer activations and reduced early output activations (knowledge decentralization), while DPR-passage maintains strong later output activations (Sec. 5.3–5.4). Contriever activates fewer intermediate neurons than its backbone and shows more uniform output-layer behavior (Sec. 5.5). RepLlama exhibits reduced intermediate-layer activation breadth vs Llama (Sec. 5.6), indicating architecture- and pooling-specific redistribution.
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: Reproducing prior claims on dense retrieval knowledge acquisition is hindered by missing details on hard-negative construction and probing-layer training, and absence of released code.
method: Provide a fully reproducible pipeline and datasets: construct NQ and MS MARCO subsets with 1 positive + 4 hard negatives per query using DPR hard negatives, with defined oversampling/removal rules (Sec. 2, 4.2.1); specify the probing architecture (concatenate query/passage embeddings and a linear projection) and training hyperparameters (e.g., lr=1e-4, batch size=32768, up to 30–50 epochs; Table 1, Sec. 4.1–4.2.3); and release code implementing embedding precomputation, probe training/evaluation, and IG-based neuron attribution with plotting (GitHub: ielab/DenseRetriever-Knowledge-Acquisition).
paper_title: Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition

problem: Shallow-fusion contextual biasing in ASR often fails for embodied agents because partial subword matches are pruned early in beam search and performance degrades when the provided context is irrelevant. A robust biasing mechanism is needed to favor visually grounded words without hurting general speech recognition.
method: They introduce a modified beam search decoder that enables word-level biasing with subword look-ahead while preserving general ASR performance. The decoder uses: (i) a sampling function S with a cumulative probability threshold C to avoid generating very low-scoring continuations (eq. 3; lines 100–105); (ii) a contextual rescoring function R applied at word boundaries (eq. 4–5; lines 108–120) that interpolates CTC and an n-gram LM, scales boosts using unigram probabilities, and imposes a soft conditional lexicon constraint by down-weighting non-bias OOVs (δ) and boosting bias OOVs (γ); and (iii) a bias-aware, non-greedy pruning strategy using a rescoring-likelihood ψ (eq. 6; lines 122–135) based on progress in a bias trie (tn, nl) to swap K candidates between forward and prunable sets, effectively reserving beam capacity for subword completions of bias terms (line 58). This yields large gains on a robot instruction ASR task (WER 8.48 vs 20.83; 59.28% WERR; lines 171–178, 195) and shows lower degradation under anti-context settings (lines 201–219).
paper_title: Can Visual Context Improve Automatic Speech Recognition for an Embodied Agent?

problem: Robots need a compact, relevant, and dynamic biasing vocabulary derived from their egocentric visual scene; naively using all caption tokens leads to large, noisy vocabularies that hurt ASR when context is irrelevant and is impractical for frequent WFST recompilation.
method: They build RoSI’s dynamic context extraction: capture the ego-view image, run DenseCap to generate region-level descriptions (lines 84–87), then use a lightweight BiLSTM sequence tagger with GloVe embeddings to label bias phrases (B-B/I-B/O) from captions (lines 88–93). The resulting words/phrases are compiled into a dynamic prefix tree (trie) B_T (lines 62–65), which updates as the robot moves and guides the decoder’s rescoring and pruning without WFST recompilation. This visual-context-driven, on-the-fly bias vocabulary drives consistent ASR gains, contributing to 59.3% WERR and a 38% relative increase in transcription accuracy over the base ASR (lines 195).
paper_title: Can Visual Context Improve Automatic Speech Recognition for an Embodied Agent?

problem: Low-resource ASR lacks labeled data, while purely supervised transfer ignores abundant unlabeled target audio and purely self-supervised pretraining may not learn phoneme-aligned, SR-specific features that transfer well across languages and domains.
method: Pretrain a wav2vec 2.0-style encoder with a unified multitask objective that combines supervised phonetic CTC and phonetically-aware contrastive self-supervision. On labeled source data, jointly optimize CTC to align contextual representations with phoneme labels and a masked contrastive loss between contextual embeddings and quantized latent targets; on unlabeled target data, apply the same contrastive objective to adapt the codebook and contextual space. The architecture uses a convolutional feature encoder, a Transformer context network, and a Gumbel-softmax vector quantizer with codebook diversity regularization, followed by fine-tuning (freezing the feature encoder) on small labeled target sets.
paper_title: UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data

problem: Quantizers in contrastive speech pretraining can collapse or learn codebooks that are not phoneme-discriminative; simply combining CTC and contrastive losses yields limited gains.
method: Explicitly guide the quantizer to encode SR-specific, phonetic content by randomly replacing a proportion r of contextual embeddings with their quantized counterparts during CTC training and computing the CTC loss on these mixed representations. This forces codewords to directly predict phoneme sequences, aligning discrete units with phonetic categories, activating more codewords, and reducing ambiguity in phoneme–codeword mappings.
paper_title: UniSpeech: Unified Speech Representation Learning with Labeled and Unlabeled Data

problem: Diffusion-based and consistency-distilled text-to-audio systems require many iterative steps, and curved probability paths make few-/one-step generation inaccurate and slow for real-time use.
method: FlashAudio adopts rectified flows to learn straight transport paths for text-conditioned audio latents. It initializes from a strong pre-trained conditional flow matching model, then applies a single reflow step to obtain a near-straight 2-rectified flow ODE, and finally performs one-step distillation to compress the ODE solution into a single Euler step. This yields fast few-step and one-step generation that surpasses diffusion models using hundreds of steps in audio quality, with RTF≈0.0025 (~400× faster than real time) and improved metrics (e.g., lower FAD, higher CLAP).
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: Uniform timestep sampling in rectified flow training wastes capacity on easy regions, and arbitrary pairing between Gaussian noise and data creates mixing/ambiguity that hinders stable learning.
method: FlashAudio introduces Bifocal Samplers to reweight training timesteps: a logit-normal distribution prioritizes challenging mid-range t for 1-RF, while a mixture-of-exponentials emphasizes boundary regions near noise/data for reflow (2-RF). It further proposes Immiscible Flow, which assigns batches of noise to audio samples by minimizing total pairwise distances (Hungarian algorithm), preserving Gaussianity while reducing confusion in mappings. Ablations show these choices improve KL/CLAP and FAD versus uniform sampling and unassigned noise (e.g., 2-RF Mix-Exp vs w/o Mix-Exp; and w/ vs w/o immiscible flow).
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) with large guidance scales amplifies simulation/regression errors in rectified flows and perturbs the marginal distribution, degrading audio quality and text-audio alignment.
method: Anchored Optimization uses an ω=1 simulation as a pivot trajectory and, during paired generation for reflow and distillation, optimizes only the unconditional embedding around this anchor while sampling with ω>1, keeping model weights and textual embeddings fixed. The model then regresses to the guided velocity field v_cfg, enabling stronger guidance without destabilizing the flow. This improves quality and alignment (e.g., FAD 1.43→1.26, CLAP 0.639→0.652) compared to naive CFG in RF.
paper_title: FlashAudio: Rectified Flows for Fast and High-fidelity Text-to-Audio Generation

problem: ASR post-correction must reliably detect which tokens are wrong without perturbing the many correct ones; implicit detection (e.g., attention/CTC) gives no explicit signal, while explicit detection (predicting deletion/substitution/insertion) is brittle.
method: Introduce a soft error detection encoder trained with an anti-copy language modeling loss that yields per-token probabilities using bidirectional context in a single pass. The loss adds a special GT token and a regularization term that predicts GT over the vocabulary excluding the ground-truth token, discouraging trivial copy and making GT a decision boundary for correctness. The encoder ingests aligned N-best ASR candidates, performs position-wise candidate selection using these probabilities, then combines the encoder’s token probability with the ASR output probability to compute an error score; tokens below a threshold are flagged as errors.
paper_title: SoftCorrect: Error Correction with Soft Detection for Automatic Speech Recognition

problem: Standard CTC-based non-autoregressive correction duplicates all source tokens, inviting unnecessary edits, higher latency, and potential new errors; we need a decoder that only edits detected errors while preserving correct tokens.
method: Propose a constrained CTC loss that duplicates only the tokens detected as incorrect (repeated three times) and treats other tokens as fixed anchors with no dynamic alignment. The decoder focuses alignment and generation only on the repeated (error) positions, enabling parallel, low-latency correction and minimizing changes to correct tokens. During training, 5% of correct tokens are marked as pseudo errors to improve robustness to detector mistakes; at inference, softmax is applied over allowed error positions and duplicates/blanks are removed, and correction can be skipped entirely if all tokens are detected correct.
paper_title: SoftCorrect: Error Correction with Soft Detection for Automatic Speech Recognition

problem: On-device ASR must serve both virtual-assistant (VA) and speech-to-text (STT) use cases with very different linguistic distributions, but naively training a single LM on merged data degrades accuracy; training per-application LMs meets accuracy but doubles disk/memory.
method: They propose a data balancing scheme that derives per-source sampling weights for a single, application-agnostic NNLM. First, build a balanced dev set (≈equal VA/STT). Compute optimal interpolation weights that minimize perplexity for application-specific LMs on this dev set to obtain redistribution masses for VA and STT. Use these to rescale initial per-source sampling weights (from count-merging) and renormalize, then sample heldout/train data accordingly. This yields a unified training mix that benefits both applications (Sec. 3, lines 59–71).
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: A single FOFE NNLM trained on combined data can still lose accuracy across applications; need an application-agnostic architecture that adapts to heterogeneous inputs while remaining on-device feasible.
method: Introduce a Mixture FOFE NNLM: encode history with FOFE, then run M parallel FOFE-based sub-networks and a separate mixture sub-network that outputs a softmax over experts; combine sub-network outputs via the learned mixture weights before the final softmax. This mixture-of-experts over FOFE networks improves or matches WER versus baseline FOFE and Transformer across languages (often best on STT), but incurs higher latency than the baseline AA FOFE (Sec. 4, lines 79–80; Results lines 148, 171; Table 2–3).
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: Need a single on-device LM that preserves per-application specialization (VA vs STT) without the speed and memory cost of maintaining two separate models.
method: Introduce an Application-Dependent FOFE (AD-FOFE): a multi-task FOFE LM with separate sub-networks and softmax biases per application. During training, only the sub-network for the sample’s application is updated; at inference, the invocation (wake phrase vs mic button) selects the corresponding sub-network, so runtime is comparable to a single FOFE. This model achieves best VA accuracy, competitive STT accuracy, fastest latency among proposed models, and reduces overall disk size by roughly half compared to maintaining two application-specific LMs (Sec. 4, lines 81–84; Abstract line 9; Results lines 148, 171; Table 2).
paper_title: Application-Agnostic Language Modeling for On-Device ASR

problem: End-to-end speech translation encoders must simultaneously transcribe speech, extract linguistic/semantic features, and learn cross-lingual mappings, but conventional ASR-only pre-training teaches mainly transcription and leaves the encoder weak in semantics and bilingual alignment.
method: Introduce a curriculum pre-training pipeline with increasing task difficulty: (1) an elementary ASR course with multi-task CTC + cross-entropy to learn frame/word alignments and transcription; (2) two advanced courses to inject sentence understanding and cross-lingual mapping. Training is hierarchical: the bottom N encoder blocks (e.g., 8 of 12) are optimized for ASR and source-token objectives, while the top blocks are optimized for bilingual mapping. This staged, layer-partitioned pre-training strengthens encoder representations and yields consistent BLEU gains on LibriSpeech En–Fr and IWSLT En–De.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: ASR pre-training alone does not endow the encoder with explicit sentence-level understanding of the source utterance beyond surface transcription.
method: Propose a Frame-based Masked Language Model (FMLM) for speech: use forced alignment to locate word-level time spans, randomly mask 15% of whole-word speech segments (filled with the utterance mean), and have the encoder predict the masked source word distribution from the average hidden states over the masked span. The loss is a KL-divergence to a target distribution over source BPE tokens and is computed at the N-th encoder layer (shared with ASR), explicitly training source-language understanding in the encoder.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: End-to-end ST encoders lack explicit supervision to map source speech segments to target-language words, forcing the decoder to learn most of the cross-lingual alignment.
method: Introduce Frame-based Bilingual Lexicon Translation (FBLT): for each speech segment aligned to a source word, predict a distribution over the target vocabulary from the averaged encoder states of that segment (applied to all segments, masked or not). On triplet data, obtain source–target word alignments via Moses/GIZA++ with grow-diag-final; on ASR-only pairs, supervise using a bilingual lexical table built from estimated translation probabilities. Train this with a KL-divergence loss at the top encoder layer so upper layers specialize in cross-lingual word mapping, reducing decoder burden and improving ST performance.
paper_title: Curriculum Pre-training for End-to-End Speech Translation

problem: Text-guided diffusion models struggle to precisely modify targeted audio events while preserving unrelated content and overall structure; existing approaches often retrain on editing pairs or regenerate whole clips, causing drift.
method: PPAE is a training-free, plug-in editing module for pre-trained text-to-audio latent diffusion models that operates by manipulating cross-attention maps during denoising. It inverts the source audio into the model’s latent/noise space, then computes cross-attention for the source and edited prompts and blends them with a Fuser that gradually transitions from source to target maps via a cosine-annealed schedule, with token-level control over which text tokens are edited. This hierarchical design confines changes locally and yields smooth transitions, enabling Replace/Refine/Reweight edits while preserving global structure. Ablations show the Fuser scheduler improves editing quality over direct attention replacement.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: A single classifier-free guidance scale is unreliable for audio editing because the needed edit strength varies widely across samples; manual tuning is costly and unstable.
method: Guidance bootstrapping evaluates a set of guidance scales in parallel and selects the best output using a CLAP-based filter that scores alignment with the target prompt. This removes per-sample tuning, adapts edit strength automatically, and is fully parallelizable. Experiments and ablations show there is no universal guidance scale and that bootstrapping improves performance versus fixed-scale or simple batch sampling.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: Recombining desired elements from two different audio tracks (e.g., speech from one with the background of another) without retraining is challenging and risks structural artifacts.
method: Audio Refusion extends PPAE by fusing token-specific cross-attention maps from two source audios with a balanced Fuser schedule, producing composites such as preserving speech from one clip while swapping to the music background of another. The method recommends balanced fusion (e.g., ηmin/ηmax ≈ 0.4/0.6) to control contributions. It demonstrates attention-map-level compositing across sources, though structural precision is not always guaranteed.
paper_title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models

problem: Robotic picking in clutter requires deciding when to push versus when to grasp, and how to sequence them, without relying on hand-crafted heuristics or explicit object models.
method: Introduce Visual Pushing for Grasping (VPG): a joint Q-learning policy composed of two fully convolutional networks (FCNs), φ_p for pushing and φ_g for grasping, that map an RGB-D heightmap to dense pixel-wise Q-values for all push and grasp primitives. Orientations are handled by rotating the input into 16 angles and predicting horizontal actions per rotation, then selecting the action (push or grasp) with the highest Q across all pixels and orientations. Training is end-to-end and self-supervised: grasps receive reward 1 on success, and pushes receive a small intrinsic reward when they produce detectable scene change, so the policy learns pushes that enable future grasps and grasps that leverage prior pushes. This discovers non-trivial synergistic behaviors directly from experience without explicit object segmentation or dynamics models.
paper_title: Learning Synergies between Pushing and Grasping with Self-supervised Deep Reinforcement Learning

problem: Deep RL on real robots is sample- and compute-intensive; policies need an action representation that tightly couples perception with control while remaining tractable and data-efficient.
method: Represent state as an orthographic RGB-D heightmap and parameterize actions as motion primitives anchored at each pixel: a 10 cm straight push starting at the pixel location, or a top-down parallel-jaw grasp centered at the pixel, each in 1 of 16 orientations (via rotated inputs). Each FCN uses dual DenseNet-121 towers (RGB and cloned depth) followed by 1×1 convolutions to output a Q-value per pixel. This fully convolutional action-value formulation provides spatial locality and weight sharing across positions and orientations (equivariance), allowing evaluation of all 1,605,632 actions in ~2.5 s and enabling effective learning with few hours of real-robot data.
paper_title: Learning Synergies between Pushing and Grasping with Self-supervised Deep Reinforcement Learning

problem: Can a pushing policy be learned without direct push supervision—i.e., purely from its influence on future grasp success—rather than from hand-crafted push objectives?
method: Train an ablation (VPG-noreward) with R_p set to 0, so pushes are reinforced only through delayed grasp rewards within the same joint Q-learning framework. Experiments show it still learns effective push-then-grasp sequences (pushes increasingly followed by successful grasps) and achieves near-VPG performance; adding a small change-detection reward (R_p=0.5 when the heightmap changes) accelerates learning but is not essential. This validates that the synergy arises from reinforcement over future grasps rather than engineered push goals.
paper_title: Learning Synergies between Pushing and Grasping with Self-supervised Deep Reinforcement Learning

problem: Action-conditioned visual prediction typically requires action labels, but abundant passive videos (e.g., humans, dashboard cams) lack actions, making it hard to learn models that can be used for control.
method: Introduce a latent action variable z_t in a generative model p(x_{t+1}|x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) with an action decoder p(a_t|z_t). For interaction data with actions, infer z_t via an action encoder q_act(z_t|a_t); for action-free observation, infer z_t via an inverse model q_inv(z_t|x_t, x_{t+1}). Train with amortized variational inference by optimizing ELBOs for both cases and add a Jensen–Shannon divergence regularizer to align q_act and q_inv. The transition model is a modified SAVP network conditioned on z, enabling action-conditioned prediction using both labeled and unlabeled videos.
paper_title: Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction

problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
method: Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.
paper_title: Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction

problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies typically require large amounts of task- and hardware-specific real data and struggle to generalize; training purely from RGB while keeping the representation interpretable and transferable is challenging.
method: Introduce a modular, fully differentiable pipeline with three learned components trained solely on synthetic/simulation data: (1) a variational affordance encoder-decoder (VAED) that takes an RGB image and outputs both a pixel-wise affordance map and a low-dimensional latent affordance vector s; (2) a policy network that maps s concatenated with camera pose to a compact action latent a, trained in simulation with a supervised loss on the end-effector goal using differentiable forward kinematics (FK) to backpropagate through the FK computation; and (3) a trajectory VAE that decodes a into a full joint-space trajectory. Using VAEs enforces smooth, low-dimensional latent spaces for both perception and action, improving data efficiency. Composing the frozen perception and trajectory modules with the learned policy enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a Franka Panda for a ball-in-container task and robustness under significant visual clutter.
paper_title: Affordance Learning for End-to-End Visuomotor Robot Control

problem: Collecting pixel-wise affordance labels for training is labor-intensive and limits scalability and generalization across objects and scenes.
method: Propose an automatic, domain-randomized data generation pipeline in Blender that renders paired RGB images and ground-truth affordance masks (e.g., wrap-grasp, contain) without any manual labeling. The pipeline randomizes cup shape, object/textures, camera pose, lighting, table/clutter (using YCB objects), producing a 1M-image dataset. Training VAED on this data yields an RGB-only affordance detector that transfers to real images and achieves competitive results on the UMD affordance benchmark, comparable to RGB-D baselines. Code for dataset generation and training is released (https://github.com/gamleksi/affordancegym).
paper_title: Affordance Learning for End-to-End Visuomotor Robot Control

problem: Robots need visual perception that generalizes across object instances and clutter while requiring minimal task-specific supervision, so policies can be learned efficiently for real-world manipulation.
method: Introduce a two-level, object-centric attention framework. A task-independent meta-attention uses a region proposal network (RPN) trained on MSCOCO to produce object hypotheses with locations g(oi) and semantic features f(oi) from a pretrained ImageNet CNN (AlexNet conv5 pooled over proposal crops). A task-specific attention, parameterized by vector(s) W over f(oi), is learned from a few trajectories by predicting next movement using a soft Boltzmann attention with entropy regularization; during policy learning W is fixed and a hard argmax box is used. The attended object coordinates are concatenated with robot state and used by an RL policy (e.g., guided policy search), yielding robust generalization to unseen mugs and clutter.
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Practitioners need to control how broadly a robot’s perception and policy generalize (e.g., avoid distractors vs. broaden to a category) without retraining large detectors or collecting extensive labels.
method: Adjust the task-specific attention using a handful of additional demonstrations: include distractor objects to narrow the attention to a specific instance, or include diverse relevant objects to broaden to a category. Training W to predict demonstrated motion pushes attention onto objects predictive of trajectories, enabling user-controlled scope (e.g., finetuning with a pink mug as distractor to focus on a brown mug; or adding lemons/limes so attention generalizes beyond oranges to citrus).
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
method: Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.
paper_title: Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning

problem: Conventional RGB-D sensing fails on transparent/reflective objects, especially in clutter, making it hard to infer where transparent objects actually occupy space for safe and effective grasping.
method: Construct a 3D Depth Likelihood Volume (DLV) over the workspace from multi-view light field observations. For each sampled 3D point, aggregate multi-aperture ray consistency by triangulating corresponding rays in sub-aperture images with sub-pixel shifts (via frequency-domain shifting), measure ray differences using color and color-gradient terms, sum over apertures, and normalize to a likelihood. Suppress specular artifacts by computing variance of ray differences across viewpoints at saturated/high-likelihood pixels and excluding high-variance, farther-than-alternatives rays along the same image pixel, yielding a reflection-robust transparency occupancy estimate. (Supported by: multi-view DLV generalization and formulation, sub-pixel triangulation, reflection suppression via variance and exclusion.)
paper_title: GlassLoc: Plenoptic Grasp Pose Detection in Transparent Clutter

problem: Detecting reliable 6-DoF grasp poses for transparent objects lacks clean depth data and object models, requiring a grasp classifier and data generation strategy that work directly with transparency cues.
method: Sample 6-DoF grasp candidates and, for each, voxelize a graspable cuboid aligned to gripper axes. From the enclosed DLV, extract compact 2D feature maps: center slice, average map, and sliced-difference, each projected along the x/y/z axes to form a 9-channel input, and classify with a LeNet-based network. Generate supervision despite transparency by wrapping objects to obtain point clouds, using depth-based grasp pose detection and collision checks to label positive/negative grasps, then mapping those poses into the DLV for training. At inference, apply a particle-filter-based grasp search to focus on high-confidence regions and execute on a robot, achieving 81% grasp success over 220 trials across five transparent object types in clutter. (Supported by: grasp feature projections and 9 channels, LeNet classifier, training via wrapped-object point clouds mapped to DLV, particle-filter grasp search, and experimental success rates.)
paper_title: GlassLoc: Plenoptic Grasp Pose Detection in Transparent Clutter

problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
method: Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Optimally placing fixtures is a contextual bandit with continuous actions and discontinuous rewards (small pose changes can flip success to failure), which breaks standard Lipschitz/zooming assumptions and is hard with high-dimensional image context.
method: Propose the Smoothed Zooming algorithm: cover the continuous action space with adaptive covering balls initialized with radius h; select a ball via UCB1 using per-ball empirical means and confidence radii; uniformly sample an action within the selected ball to smooth discontinuities; shrink ball radii as samples accrue and activate new balls to cover newly exposed regions. This exploration strategy enables efficient learning of Q(s, a) for high-dimensional contexts and discontinuous reward landscapes when training the outer-loop fixture placement policy.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
method: Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.
paper_title: Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills

problem: Robots need to infer object physical properties quickly and without explicit labels to perform dynamic in-hand manipulations; tactile sensors like GelSight provide rich but complex signals, and single exploratory actions capture only partial information.
method: SwingBot performs two complementary in-hand explorations—tilting (at 20° and 45°) and shaking—using a GelSight sensor. Marker displacement fields (14×12×2) from tilting are encoded by a CNN into a 40-D feature, while a sequence of per-frame CNN features from shaking is aggregated by an LSTM into an 80-D feature; a fusion network concatenates them into a joint physical embedding. The entire pipeline is trained end-to-end in a self-supervised manner by predicting the final swing-up angle, enabling the model to also capture the gel’s own dynamics. Fusing both actions significantly outperforms using either alone or no tactile input (e.g., 10.2°/12.9° error on seen/unseen vs 25.4°/26.8° with no exploration).
paper_title: SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation

problem: Choosing control parameters for dynamic swing-up on previously unseen objects is challenging because outcomes depend on unobserved physical properties (mass, COM, friction, inertia).
method: A forward dynamics predictor takes the fused physical embedding and an action parameter (gripper looseness/width at impulse) and outputs the predicted final swing-up angle. At inference, the robot samples candidate gripper widths and executes the one whose predicted angle is closest to the goal, within an impulse–momentum swing-up policy. This self-supervised predictor enables accurate swing-ups on unseen objects, achieving a mean error of 17.2° across targets (45°, 90°, 135°, 180°) and outperforming baselines that lack physical exploration by over 13°.
paper_title: SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation

problem: Learned embeddings should be interpretable and transferable, enabling recovery of explicit physical parameters that drive task dynamics.
method: With the fusion encoder frozen, a lightweight MLP head regresses mass, center of mass, and moment of inertia, and classifies friction from the learned embedding. Analysis shows complementary strengths of the two explorations on unseen objects—tilting excels at mass and COM (e.g., mass error 0.184 vs 0.263 for shaking), while shaking excels at friction (90.1% vs 75.6% for tilting); the combined embedding balances properties and improves friction to 93.9%. PCA visualizations further show the embedding is task-oriented: objects with similar swing-up policy–angle distributions cluster together, supporting generalization and policy reuse.
paper_title: SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation

problem: Grounding natural-language spatial instructions into precise pick-and-place coordinates generalizes poorly when scene object positions are fed as unordered coordinate lists and processed with fully connected layers, which overfit to absolute locations and fail to capture spatial patterns (e.g., rows).
method: Adopt a two-stage pipeline: (1) detect and localize objects, then quantize detections into a binary 2D grid with one-hot channels per object type plus a size map; (2) condition on the instruction by correlating learned instruction embeddings with the object grid to form spatial ‘soft-selections’, and pass these through a convolutional hourglass U-Net to produce start/end heatmaps from which coordinates are extracted via spatial softmax. The convolutional, translation-invariant grid representation enables robust spatial reasoning (e.g., recognizing rows) and eliminates hard-coded offsets, outperforming list+FC and end-to-end baselines (e.g., on the synthetic set: TA 95.99% vs 59.88% for Lang-FCNet and 44.89% for LSTM+CNN+SA).
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: Biased datasets can cause language-only or non-attentive models to memorize spurious associations between object IDs and offsets (e.g., certain blocks often ‘above’), hurting generalization, especially for predicting end targets.
method: Introduce multi-head attention over a BiLSTM instruction encoder with four heads: two heads project to select start/anchor objects via correlation with the object map; two capture spatial-relation attributes (offset/direction) and are concatenated with visual features before the U-Net. This attention forces the model to focus on the correct tokens (e.g., the offset word), improving end-coordinate prediction and mitigating bias (e.g., after randomizing block numbers, attention-based models retain 19/20 correct vs 6/20 for a no-attention baseline).
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: End-to-end vision–language models must jointly learn detection and grounding from limited data, which degrades instruction-following performance in manipulation tasks.
method: Use a pipelined architecture separating perception (a fine-tuned object detector, e.g., SSD) from language-conditioned spatial grounding (the above grid+U-Net model). Empirically, pipelined models (Lang-UNet/Lang-FCNet) outperform end-to-end LSTM+CNN(+SA) across Blocks and a more diverse synthetic dataset. The full system executes on a real Dobot arm, picking the correct card in all 15 trials and placing within 1 cm in 14/15 cases.
paper_title: Spatial Reasoning from Natural Language Instructions for Robot Manipulation

problem: Robots trained from prior experience often fail to zero-shot generalize to unseen industrial connectors, and in realistic deployment the ground-truth reward (e.g., precise socket-relative pose) is unavailable, making online adaptation difficult.
method: Pretrain from vision on a large offline dataset (≈70k trajectories over 50 connectors) using IQL to learn policy, Q, V, and a visual reward classifier Rψ. At deployment on a novel connector, perform self-supervised online finetuning by using the learned reward model to label new rollouts (r̂ = Rψ(s′)) and update IQL with mixed replay (25% online without augmentation, 75% offline with augmentation). Convnets are frozen and the bottleneck is used deterministically during finetuning. This offline-to-online 'learning on the job' pipeline adapts policies within ~200 trials (~50 minutes) and outperforms localization+hand-policy and search baselines under ±10–20 mm initial offsets.
paper_title: Learning on the Job: Self-Rewarding Offline-to-Online Finetuning for Industrial Insertion of Novel Connectors from Vision

problem: Standard domain generalization for vision (ERM or full domain invariance) either overfits to spurious cues or discards connector-specific information, limiting cross-domain generalization of both reward models and policies for insertion.
method: Introduce the Domain Adversarial Information Bottleneck (DAIB), a split latent z = (zI, zS) where zI is trained with a domain-adversarial loss to be invariant across connectors and zS is constrained by a variational information bottleneck (KL penalty, λ=0.01) to pass minimal domain-specific information. Apply DAIB to the reward classifier and as a policy bottleneck (optimize Lπ + LDAIB(zπ)), using deterministic encodings during finetuning. DAIB yields a more generalizable reward (88% accuracy vs. 71–79% for DANN/VIB/ERM) and, per ablations, finetuning consistently succeeds across all four test connectors only when DAIB is used for both reward and policy.
paper_title: Learning on the Job: Self-Rewarding Offline-to-Online Finetuning for Industrial Insertion of Novel Connectors from Vision

problem: A single navigation policy must both explore unknown environments safely (undirected) and reach visually specified goals (goal-conditioned), which requires representing complex, multimodal future action distributions and flexibly handling the presence or absence of a goal.
method: NoMaD introduces a unified goal-masked action diffusion policy. A ViNT-style backbone (EfficientNet-B0 encoders for observations and goal plus a Transformer) produces a context c_t, and an attention-based goal mask m either includes or blocks the goal token (m=0 for goal-directed, m=1 for undirected), with m sampled at 50% during training. A conditional diffusion model over sequences of future actions p(a_t|c_t) uses a 1D conditional U-Net denoiser trained with noise prediction to capture multimodal, collision-averse action distributions that narrow appropriately when conditioned on a goal. This is the first goal-conditioned action diffusion policy and it improves exploration success over subgoal-image diffusion by over 25% with ~15× fewer parameters, while reducing collisions and running on onboard compute.
paper_title: NoMaD: Goal Masked Diffusion Policies for Navigation and Exploration

problem: Reactive visual policies struggle with long-horizon navigation and exploration in large, unseen environments; they need a memory and planner to guide coverage and reach distant goals.
method: NoMaD is integrated with an episodic topological memory and a frontier-based high-level planner (ViKiNG-style). The graph stores visual observations as nodes and uses the policy’s goal-conditioned temporal distance predictions to form navigable edges; planning over this graph selects subgoals, while NoMaD provides short-horizon action samples to execute them. This system enables effective long-horizon exploration and goal-reaching on real robots, matching specialized goal-conditioned navigation performance and surpassing prior exploration baselines with fewer collisions and far smaller models.
paper_title: NoMaD: Goal Masked Diffusion Policies for Navigation and Exploration

problem: Generalist robot policies are largely trained on vision and proprioception and lack semantic grounding for scarce but crucial modalities like touch and audio. Naively finetuning on small heterogeneous-sensor datasets causes the policy to ignore new sensors and fails to enable zero-shot multimodal reasoning in partially observable settings.
method: FuSe finetunes pre-trained generalist policies by using natural language as a common cross-modal ground. It adds modality encoders (TVL tactile encoder for DIGIT images; audio-to-spectrogram processed as an image via a ResNet26) and tokenizes all modalities into a shared transformer backbone. Two auxiliary language-grounding losses are introduced alongside the behavior cloning loss: (1) a CLIP-style multimodal contrastive loss that builds an observation embedding (via a multi-head attention fusion over modalities) and aligns it with every instruction formed from available modality annotations; (2) a multimodal generative language loss that uses a single transformer head with modality tokens to generate the appropriate instruction from each modality combination. The final objective is L = L_BC + β L_gen + λ L_contrast, and instruction space is broadened via LLM rephrasing of templated annotations. This language-grounded finetuning unlocks zero-shot multimodal prompting, cross-modal prompting, and compositional reasoning, and applies across architectures (Octo and a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA), improving success rates by over 20% vs. baselines.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: There is a lack of physically grounded multimodal datasets that include robot actions jointly with vision, touch, and audio, limiting the ability to train and evaluate policies that reason over heterogeneous sensors.
method: The authors collect and release a real-world dataset of 26,866 teleoperated manipulation trajectories on three tasks (tabletop grasping, shopping-bag grasping, and button pressing), with synchronized RGB (third-person and wrist), tactile (two DIGITs), audio, IMU, proprioception, robot actions, and language instructions. After-the-fact templated annotations describe modality-specific properties (e.g., feels squishy, plays piano), which are further expanded via LLM-based rephrasing to support free-form test-time prompts. This is, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, the first such dataset that includes actions for physically grounded multimodal policy learning, enabling rigorous evaluation of zero-shot multimodal prompting and cross-modal compositional capabilities.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: Finetuning methods for heterogeneous sensors often bind tightly to a single architecture and separate language grounding from action prediction, limiting generality and scalability to VLA models.
method: FuSe is applied to a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA by modifying the VLM to accept arbitrary observation modalities and directly integrating the multimodal generative language modeling loss into the unified VLA backbone (rather than using an external language head). The model is first pre-trained on OXE with visual-only inputs and then finetuned on the multimodal dataset, yielding competitive or superior performance to the Octo-based FuSe, including outperforming it on the challenging shopping-bag task. The resulting VLA FuSe is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first open-source VLA finetuned on heterogeneous (non-visual) sensory inputs.
paper_title: Beyond Sight: Finetuning Generalist Robot Policies with Heterogeneous Sensors via Language Grounding

problem: Robots need to anticipate plausible post-interaction configurations of objects from a single scene image (visual affordances) that generalize to unseen objects and environments, without requiring action annotations or lab-specific training data.
method: Train a two-stage conditional generative model on large-scale passive image pairs from diverse internet datasets (e.g., Something-Something v2, Berkeley, JHU CoStar) to predict a distribution over achievable goal images given an input image. First, learn a discrete latent space with a VQ-VAE; then learn an autoregressive Transformer prior to model p(z_g | z_c) and sample diverse goal latents conditioned on the input latent, decoding them to images. Modeling in discrete latent space with an autoregressive Transformer yields diverse, compositional generations that plausibly rearrange multiple objects (e.g., re-orientation, stacking) and generalize beyond the training distribution.
paper_title: Visual Affordance Prediction for Guiding Robot Exploration

problem: Self-supervised robot exploration for learning goal-conditioned manipulation policies requires diverse, plausible, and reachable goals; curiosity-based exploration or in-domain goal generators often fail to cover such goals and generalize poorly.
method: Use the learned visual affordance model as a goal-sampling distribution during data collection: at episode start, condition on the current view to sample a goal image, execute the current policy, and store the trajectory. Train a single goal-conditioned policy via behavior cloning with hindsight goal relabeling by setting each trajectory’s goal to its final image (o_g = o_T) and maximizing log π(a_t | o_t, o_g). Without any fine-tuning of the affordance model in the lab, this pipeline drives richer exploration and yields higher success on manipulation tasks (pushing, pick-and-place, stacking) than curiosity and CVAE/Pix2Pix goal samplers.
paper_title: Visual Affordance Prediction for Guiding Robot Exploration

problem: Accurate, scalable simulation of coupled soft–rigid grasping with robust frictional contact is challenging: IPC is precise but traditionally too slow and fragile for large-scale, multi-environment data generation.
method: They develop a GPU-parallel, multi-environment IPC simulator that preserves IPC’s intersection- and inversion-free frictional contact while scaling efficiently. Key design choices include environment isolation during collision detection and step-size filtering so each environment advances with its own step size, per-environment convergence tracking to freeze finished environments, failure detection to isolate bad runs, and parallel linear solves. Run in FP64, the system achieves strong scalability on an H100 GPU, e.g., ~40× speedup for UMI grippers with 400 environments and ~16× for LEAP Hands with 512 environments, enabling large-scale grasp validation.
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: Existing grasp datasets largely omit soft grippers and deformable manipulands and often rely on simulators that permit penetrations, limiting data quality and model generalization.
method: GRIP is a fully automated pipeline that generates and validates 100K grasp poses over 1,200 objects with both soft UMI grippers and rigid LEAP Hands. Soft fingers are tetrahedralized (TetWild) with real-world material/friction parameters; objects are watertight/tetrahedralized with domain-randomized properties. Candidate grasps are synthesized via GPD (UMI) and DexGraspNet adapted to LEAP with an added normal-alignment energy; inverse kinematics adjusts fingertips along object normals to meet IPC’s contact threshold (d̂=1e−3 m), discarding remaining penetrations; bimanual grasps are composed by clustering/pairing unimanual candidates and filtering with an approximate force-closure metric. Validation applies six axis-aligned gravity perturbations under IPC contact; resulting grasps have zero penetration (D1=0) and tight clearance (D2≈0.12 cm), and training UGG on GRIP outperforms Isaac Gym–filtered data (success 26.3% vs 7.9% on full set; 31.6% vs 17.5% on bottles).
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: Predicting contact-induced stress in deformable objects during grasping is important to avoid damage, but labeled stress fields across diverse grasps are scarce.
method: Using GRIP’s per-trajectory stress annotations, they train a stress-prediction network that inputs the object point cloud and gripper mesh at a grasp pose. The model follows a GNN backbone with a cross-attention module to encode hand–object interactions and outputs pointwise von Mises stress. On mugs and bowls with leave-object-out testing (~200–250 grasps/object), it achieves 7–9% Relative MAE and KL divergence <0.2, and real-to-sim experiments show qualitative agreement between predicted and simulated stress distributions.
paper_title: GRIP: A General Robotic Incremental Potential Contact Simulation Dataset for Unified Deformable-Rigid Coupled Grasping

problem: Robot caregiving lacks a large-scale, diverse, expert-collected multimodal dataset that captures real-world ADLs with occlusions, rich physical contact, caregiver attention, and long-horizon task structure.
method: OpenRoboCare provides 315 expert sessions (19.8 h) from 21 occupational therapists across 15 ADL tasks on two manikins, recorded in five synchronized modalities: RGB-D video, pose tracking (mocap plus an occlusion-robust video-pose pipeline using manual labels to fine-tune YOLOv11 and triangulated 3D keypoints), eye-gaze, natural-language task/action annotations, and a custom whole-body tactile skin (88 resistive sensors, calibrated to force). Data, documentation, and the fine-tuned pose model are released.
paper_title: OpenRoboCare: A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: Robots lack distilled expert caregiving strategies and quantitative interaction patterns to inform control timing, contact planning, and hardware requirements.
method: The authors analyze demonstrations to extract OT guiding principles (pre-positioning, anticipation, efficiency) and concrete techniques (e.g., bridge, segmental roll, wheelchair recline, stabilizing key control points). They quantify caregiver gaze look-ahead (~2.02 s), contact distributions across body regions, and force ranges (≈0.1–2 N for gentle tasks vs >20–30 N for demanding repositioning), and translate these into guidelines for policy lookahead and hardware design (e.g., distributed whole-arm compliance and sensing).
paper_title: OpenRoboCare: A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: State-of-the-art pose perception and long-horizon activity recognition models generalize poorly to caregiving scenarios with heavy occlusions, multi-body contact, and domain shift.
method: Using OpenRoboCare as a benchmark, the paper evaluates 2D/3D pose estimation and task recognition: off-the-shelf YOLOv11 yields very low 2D mAP, but fine-tuning on small subsets (1–5 OTs) yields large gains, especially for occlusion-heavy dressing and transfer; 3D baselines (RTMOPose3D, MixSTE, HoT, MHFormer) report notable MPJPEs. VidChapters-7M on long videos shows terminology and segmentation failures (e.g., mislabeling 'Hoyer'), highlighting the dataset’s challenge and headroom for improvement.
paper_title: OpenRoboCare: A Multimodal Multi-Task Expert Demonstration Dataset for Robot Caregiving

problem: Affordance cues learned from human videos are typically human-centric and not directly actionable for robots. We need a scalable, robot-amenable way to learn where to interact and how to move after contact, while handling multi-modality, egomotion, and human-robot visual domain shift.
method: Adopt a robot-centric affordance representation as contact points (c) and post-contact trajectories (τ) in pixel space. Automatically extract labels from egocentric videos by detecting first hand–object contact with 100DOH, sampling hand–object boundary points via skin segmentation, and fitting a K-component GMM for multi-modal contact regions; obtain τ by tracking post-contact hand boxes with egomotion compensation via per-frame homographies. Map all labels back to a human-less starting frame to remove the human from conditioning. Train a ResNet-based model to predict K heatmaps for GMM means (fixed covariance) and a Transformer to predict relative post-contact trajectories from local crops.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: Real-world robot learning is sample-inefficient and struggles to decide what and where to act in continuous spaces, impeding imitation, exploration, goal-conditioned learning, and RL.
method: Leverage the affordance model to bootstrap four paradigms. (A) Offline imitation: execute (c, τ) from fθ to collect trajectories, then perform k-NN or behavior cloning using goal images. (B) Reward-free exploration: rank collected trajectories by an environment-change metric, fit a distribution h over (c, τ), and mix sampling from h and fθ to focus on human-salient interactions. (C) Goal-conditioned learning: similarly rank by goal-image distance and fit h to guide data collection. (D) Affordance as action space: densely query fθ, fit separate GMMs over c and τ, take the cross-product to form a discrete action set, and train a DQN over this reduced, meaningful action space.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: Generic visual pretraining may not yield features aligned with manipulation progress or downstream control.
method: Pretrain/finetune the visual encoder via affordance learning (VRB), then freeze it for behavior cloning on Franka Kitchen tasks and assess goal-distance in feature space during execution. The affordance-pretrained encoder outperforms R3M on BC success rates and produces feature distances that decrease monotonically with task progress, indicating more control-relevant representations.
paper_title: Affordances from Human Videos as a Versatile Representation for Robotics

problem: Vision-language models struggle to infer physical concepts of everyday objects (e.g., mass, fragility, material, contents) from images, limiting their usefulness for robotic manipulation and planning. Existing datasets emphasize visual attributes or interaction-heavy setups and are not tailored for object-centric physical reasoning in real household scenes.
method: Create PHYSOBJECTS, an object-centric dataset of real household object images with 39.6K crowd-sourced and 417K automated annotations covering eight main physical concepts and two held-out concepts. Categorical concepts use labels (e.g., material, transparency, contents), while continuous concepts (e.g., mass, fragility, deformability) are collected as pairwise preferences to capture relational judgments from static images. Large-scale automation assigns labels by category and generates preference pairs via high/low tiering, and crowd-sourcing focuses on harder cases with attention checks and high inter-annotator agreement.
paper_title: Physically Grounded Vision-Language Models for Robotic Manipulation

problem: Training VLMs to understand continuous physical concepts from preferences is non-trivial because VLM outputs are discrete text tokens, while preference learning requires continuous scores.
method: Cast physical reasoning as VQA over object crops and design a preference-learning objective: prompt the VLM with yes/no questions for continuous concepts and derive a scalar score s(o,c) from the log-likelihood ratio log p(yes|o,c) − log p(no|o,c). Use a Bradley–Terry model and binary cross-entropy over annotated pairs to fine-tune InstructBLIP (Flan-T5-XXL), while training categorical concepts with standard label likelihood. The resulting PG-InstructBLIP substantially improves accuracy across all main concepts (e.g., 87.5% avg vs 66.2%) and generalizes to held-out concepts like density and liquid capacity (71.7% vs 59.8%), and to paraphrased prompts.
paper_title: Physically Grounded Vision-Language Models for Robotic Manipulation

problem: LLM-based robot planners need reliable, object-centric physical grounding beyond detection to plan tasks that depend on properties like material, contents, and fragility.
method: Integrate the physically grounded VLM into an interactive planning framework where GPT-4 receives open-vocabulary detections (OWL-ViT), queries the VLM about physical concepts of detected objects, and uses the VLM’s top answers with confidences to form plans from predefined primitives. On 51 real scenarios, this boosts planning accuracy from 56.9% (base VLM) to 88.2%, and on a Franka Panda robot increases task success from 4/10 to 9/10 executions, demonstrating tangible gains for manipulation tasks requiring physical reasoning.
paper_title: Physically Grounded Vision-Language Models for Robotic Manipulation
